5 Aug 1941 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) departed Dundee for Scapa Flow. (1)
7 Aug 1941 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) arrived at Scapa Flow where she was to participate in A/S exercises. (1)
8 Aug 1941 In the morning, HMS Euryalus (Capt. E.W. Bush, DSO, DSC, RN), conducted A/S exercises at Scapa Flow with HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN).
In the afternoon HMS Euryalus conducted torpedo firing and gunnery exercises at Scapa Flow. (10)
21 Aug 1941 HMS Euryalus (Capt. E.W. Bush, DSO, DSC, RN) conducted gunnery and RDF exercises off Scapa Flow.
On return to Scapa flow A/S exercises were carried out with HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN). (10)
9 Sep 1941 Around 1500 A/9, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) departed Scapa Flow for Dundee. She was escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN). (11)
10 Sep 1941 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) and HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN) arrived at Dundee from Scapa Flow. (1)
20 Sep 1941 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) is taken in hand for a short refit at Dundee before she was to proceed to the Mediterranean. (1)
23 Sep 1941 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) is docked in the east graving dock at Dundee. (1)
25 Sep 1941 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) is undocked. (1)

30 Sep 1941 Around 1400 A/30, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) departed Dundee for Gibraltar. She was escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN) until position 290° - Bishops Rock - 5 miles.
Passage to the vicinity of Scapa Flow was made together with HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN).
Around 1200 A/30, off Scapa Flow, HrMs O 10 (Lt. D.T. Mackay, RNN) joined for passage towards Oban. HrMs O 10 parted company off Oban around 1730 A/2.
At 1800 A/2, off Oban HMS H 32 (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) joined for the passage south through the Irish Sea as she was to proceed to Portsmouth.
Around 0615 A/5, HMS P 31 parted company with HMS H 32 and HMS Cutty Sark.

On parting company with the escort HMS P 31 is to proceed dived by day to position 46°00'N, 09°00'W, thence on surface at the Commanding Officers discretion to position 43°00'N, 10°40'W where E.T.A. is 0200 A/8. Thence as ordered by the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.).
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (1)

7 Oct 1941 In a signal timed 1136 A/7, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders to HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN); Onward route through positions (P) 38°00'N, 10°30'E, (Q) 36°35'N, 09°00'E, (R) 350° - Cape Spartel - 10 miles to make rendezvous with the inward escort, Report position, course and speed of advance on crossing 43°N. (12)
11 Oct 1941 Around 1310 A/11, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) arrived at Gibraltar from Dundee. (13)
13 Oct 1941 At 1930 A/13, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) departed Gibraltar for Malta.
She was to proceed through position 38°01'N, 09°01'E around 0500 A/17. Further routing would be sent by the Capt. S.10.
For the daily positions of HMS P 31 during this passage see the map below.
14 Oct 1941 At 1939 A/14, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) sighted the lights of two vessels which were presumed to be fishing.
HMS P 31's position at 2000 A/14 was 36°33'N, 01°21'W.
At 2200 A/14, a lighted large steamer and her escort were seen proceeding on course 170° at a speed of 6 knots. These were most likely Vichy-French vessels proceeding to Oran.
[We have been unable to identify these vessels.] (14)

15 Oct 1941 In a signal timed 1255 A/15, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN); Adjust speed to make passage 38°00'N, 11°44'E to 37°16'N, 12°56'E submerged on the 19th, thence direct by N.W. channel to arrive Marsamxett before dark on 20th. (12)

16 Oct 1941 At 0805 A/16, well to the north-north-east of Bougie, in position 37°32'N, 05°16'E, a periscope was sighted. HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) dived and Asdic contact was obtained at 1500 yards indicating the other submarine was on course 190°. P 31 surfaced at 0945 hours and proceeded at full speed in the hope of drawing an attack but nothing happened and P 31 dived again.
[We can not place any German or Italian submarine in this area at this time. Also no other Allied submarine was in the area. Most likely the contact was bogus.] (14)

17 Oct 1941 In a signal timed 1145 A/17, HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) and HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) were informed that HMS Rorqual (Lt. L.W. Napier, RN) and HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) would proceed from position 37°16'N, 12°56'E to position 38°00'N, 11°44'E during daylight/19. HMS Rorqual would then proceed towards Cavoli Island and HMS P 34 was to operate east of 12°00'E and south of 38°40'N. [This patrol area for HMS P 34 is however a bid strange as HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) departed Malta on the 19th to operate in exactly this patrol area. It therefore seems likely that HMS P 34 had been ordered to patrol somewhere else, most probably further to the east, most probably between Cape St. Vito and Palermo, but no signal on this is listed in the war diary summaries.] (12)
20 Oct 1941 Late in the morning, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) arrived at Malta from Gibraltar. (14)

29 Oct 1941 Around 1500 A/29, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) departed Malta for her 4th war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol near Capo Colonna, Crotone, Italy in area 'R' (38°45'N, 16°30'E - 000° - 75 - 45). She was to proceed through position 35°51'N, 15°13'E at 2000 A/29 and then proceed on course 30° towards her patrol area.
On departure attack exercises were carried out during which HMS Abingdon (Lt. G.A. Simmers, RNR) acted as target. (14)
29 Oct 1941 For the daily positions of HMS P 31 during her 4th war patrol see the map below.

1 Nov 1941 At 0815 A/1, in the Gulf of Taranto, in position 39°45'N, 16°45'E, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) sighted two motor torpedo boats proceeding on course 270°. The enemy were one mile apart in line abreast.
[We have been unable to identify these vessels.]
At 1400 A/1, north of Lido Sant'Angelo, in position 39°38'N, 16°39'E, a small steamer was sighted proceeding towards the Lido Sant'Angelo pier.
[We have been unable to identify this vessel.] (14)

2 Nov 1941 At 0042 A/2, in the Gulf of Taranto, north of Punta Alice, in position 39°36'N, 17°10'E, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) had to dive for a motor torpedo boat. HE of a second motor torpedo boat was heard after diving. Both were proceeding northwards after which they were heard to be patrolling.
At 0400 A/2, both motor torpedo boats were heard proceeding southwards.

3 Nov 1941 At 0146 A/3, off the Gulf of Taranto, to the east of Punta Alice, in position 39°23'N, 17°23'E, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) sighted a Spica class torpedo boat bearing 340° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy courses were 120° and later 180°. HMS P 31 dived to avoid being sighted, surfacing again at 0242 A/3.
[We have been unable to identify a destroyer or torpedo boat in this area at this time. No matches could be found in the available Italian documents.]
At 0850 A/3, in position 235° - Cape Colonne - 5 miles an auxiliary schooner was seen patrolling. No shipping was seen in the harbour of Crotone.
At 0950 A/3, in position 090° - Crotone lighthouse - 3 miles, two trawlers were seen minesweeping on a course of 090°. Several small craft were seen fishing inshore. (14)

6 Nov 1941 At 1230 A/6, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) sighted two large schooners patrolling off Crotone. Enemy course was 360° until 1330 A/6 when they changed course to 180°. P 31 closed to investigate but the schooners entered Crotone harbour at 1415 A/6. (14)

7 Nov 1941 At 0500 A/7, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) left patrol to return to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1130 B/4. She was to proceed trough positions 38°00'N, 16°00'E, 35°51'N, 15°15'E and 36°00'N, 14°53'E to arrive at Malta at 0730 B/9. (15)

7 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 2015 B/7, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) of the following; At 1602 B/7 three medium motor vessels and one escort vessel in position 38°57'N, 17°14'E, course north, speed 4 knots.

This referred most probably to a convoy made up of the Italian transports Fedora (5016 GRT, built 1910), Rubicone (4965 GRT, built 1906) and Penelope (1160 GRT, built 1907). They were escorted by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Olbia (3514 GRT, built 1929). The convoy sailed from Messina at 2400 B/6, at 8 knots and arrived at Taranto at 1100 B/8. (16)
9 Nov 1941 At 0700 A/9, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) ended her 4th war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (14)
18 Nov 1941 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) departed Malta for her 5th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Ionian Sea in position 38°05'N, 19°43'E.
On departure independent exercises were carried out.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 31 during this patrol see the map below.

18 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1647 B/18, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the submarines listed below; Establish a patrol line as quickly as possible; 4 - 36°35'N, 18°30'E - 200° - 15 - 290° - 40. Positions are to be taken up as follows; HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) in position (A) 36°35'N, 18°30'E. HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.deB. Kershaw, RN) in position (B). HMS Trusty (Lt.Cdr. W.D.A. King, DSO, DSC, RN) in position (C). HMS Perseus (Lt.Cdr. E.C.F. Nicolay, DSO, RN) in position (D). Route to position HMS Upright as laid down in my signal timed 1641 B/18 remainder as routed by Capt. S.10. After arrival in vicinity patrol position submarines are not to be attacked. Adjacent submarines are to endeavour to communicate on SS/T.

In a follow up signal timed 2155 B/18, the Capt. S.1 cancelled his orders given to HMS Thunderbolt (Lt. C.B. Crouch, DSO, RN) in his signal timed 1641 B/18. In the signal timed 1847 B/18 for HMS Perseus, HMS Thunderbolt should be read throughout. She was to remaining to northward of 37°50'N, until 0200 B/19 then proceedeed through position 37°29'N, 20°00'E direct to position (D) 35°53'N, 18°12'E. (16)
20 Nov 1941 At 0205 B/20, in position 36°35'N, 17°37'E, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) transmitted a dummy message for deception purposes.
This was accordance with the C-in-C's signal timed 2203 B/9.
HMS P 31 then continued her passage to position 'B' (36°21'N, 18°24'E) in the patrol line as had been ordered in the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1647 B/19. (14)

21 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1611 B/21, the Capt. S.1, referring to his signal timed 1647 B/18, ordered the four submarines in the patrol line, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.deB. Kershaw, RN), HMS Trusty (Lt.Cdr. W.D.A. King, DSO, DSC, RN) and HMS Thunderbolt (Lt. C.B. Crouch, DSO, RN), to move their patrol positions 082° - 45 miles. (16)
23 Nov 1941 At 0250 B/23, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) set course to patrol in the vicinity of Cape Colonne as in accordance with the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2021 B/22.
This signal stated the following regarding HMS P 31; Proceed to area 'R' within 45 miles of Cape Golonne (39°06'N, 17°16'E) passing through position 36°58'N, 19°30'E. (15)

24 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 0921 B/24, the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN) and HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.deB. Kershaw, RN) of the following; One enemy cruiser in position 36°16'N, 20°22'E at 0800 B/24 on course 343°, speed 18 knots.
In a follow up signal by the Capt. S.1 timed 1611 B/24, the same submarines were informed of the following; My 0921 B/24. Enemy altered course at 1345 B/24 to 320°.

These signal referred to the Italian light cruiser Luigi Cadorna which sailed unescorted from Benghazi at 1755 B/23 and arrived at Taranto at 0825 B/25. She had brought stores and Axis troops to Benghazi and was returning with PoWs and Axis troops. (16)

26 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1220 B/26, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN); Leave patrol as necessary through positions 36°20'N, 17°00'E and 35°31'N, 15°15'E to arrive at Malta at 0730 B/30. (16)

26 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1931 B/26, the following orders were sent by the Capt. S.1 to HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN) and HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.deB. Kershaw, RN); Establish patrol forthwith, HMS Thrasher between 39°N and 40°N and eastwards of 18°E. HMS P 31 in present area 'R' west of 17°50'E. HMS Thrasher will be recalled about 10 December to arrive at Alexandria A.M./16. (16)

27 Nov 1941 At 0128 B/27, south-south-west of Cape Rizzuto, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) dived for a Spica-class torpedo boat which approached at high speed and stopped her engines in the vicinity of the submarine. After a quarter of an hour she went slow ahead and finally disappeared at high speed to the southwest.

Possibly this was the Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza escorting the Italian tanker Iridio Mantovani (10540 GRT, built 1939). They had sailed from Taranto at 1500 B/26 and arrived at Messina at 1500 B/27. Giuseppe Dezza was however one of the 'old torpedo boats and not 'a more modern vessel' of the Spica-class. It is possible the identification was not correct in the dark.

At 2331 B/27, south-east of Cape Rizzuto, in position 38°44'N, 17°15'E, HMS P 31 sighted two merchant vessels and two destroyers at long range. Enemy course was 180° at 10 knots. An enemy report was sent to the Vice Admiral Malta and Capt. S.10.

A possible march could be the Italian transport Carlo del Greco (6837 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the Italian destroyers Corazziere and Carabiniere which sailed from Taranto at 1520 B/27-1530 B/27 and arrived at Messina at 0704 B/29-0735 B/29. [no second merchant ship was with them though throughout their passage !]. (14)
28 Nov 1941 At 1450 B/28, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.deB. Kershaw, RN) noticed that she was being followed by an auxiliary schooner in position 39°22'N, 17°21'E (east of Punta Alice). (14)

29 Nov 1941 At 0238 B/29, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 0131 B/29, ordering her to return to area 'R'. Course was set to proceed toward Crotone to fix position.
At 0600 B/29, east of Crotone, in position 39°05'N, 17°25'E, three torpedo boats were seen. These proceeded to the latitude of Cape Rizzuto and then proceeded slowly north presumably on an A/S sweep.

At 0955 B/29, HMS P 31 set course for position 39°24'N, 17°46'E to take up the central position in the patrol line ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0530 B/29. This signal stated the following; Establish patrol line 3 - 39°13'N, 17°29'E - 050°- 18 - 140° - 30. HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, DSO, RN) in position 'H' (39°13'N, 17°29'E), HMS P 31 position 'J', HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN) position 'K'. Heavy unit of enemy fleet expected to proceed southwards shortly. (15)
29 Nov 1941 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) attacked the Italian light cruiser Muzio Attendolo with torpedoes in the Gulf of Taranto. No hits were obtained.

The cruiser force attacked by P 31 was made up of the Italian light cruisers Emanuelle Filiberto Duca d’Aosta, Raimondo Montecuccoli and Muzio Attendolo. They were escorted by the Italian destroyers Aviere, Geniere and Camicia Nera.

The light cruiser Raimondo Montecuccoli (third cruiser in the formation) observed Muzio Attendolo suddenly turning to port and imitated her immediately. Torpedo tracks were seen passing to starboard between the two cruisers.

The cruiser squadron sailed from Taranto at 1135 B/29-1214 B/29 to cover convoys to Libya and returned at 1059 B/1-1158 B/1. Simultaneously, the battleship Duilio screened by the destroyers Granatiere, Alpino, Bersagliere and Fuciliere (13th Destroyer Squadron), sailed from Taranto at 1730 B/29-1937 B/29 and the light cruiser Giuseppe Garibaldi screened by the destroyers Corazziere and Carabiniere, sailed from Messina at 2135 B/29-2220 B/29. At 0946 B/30, Duilio opened fire on a low flying aircraft which briefly emerged in the poor visibility. At 1200 B/30, Bersagliere signalled a submarine and the battleship took evasive action while Bersaglere and Alpino hunted it. At 1421 B/30, Bersagliere reported another submarine but this appeared to be doubtful. At 1453 B/30, they were joined by the Garibaldi group. The entire force returned to Taranto at 1111 B/1-1447 B/1, except Corazziere and Carabiniere which returned to Messina at 2349 B/1 and 0235 B/2 respectively. Despite the deployment of these covering forces, the Italian armed merchant cruiser Adriatico (1976 GRT, built 1931) was sunk by Force K at 0300 B/1, 56 miles north of Benghazi and at 1300 B/1, 60 miles north-west of Tripoli, the Italian tanker Iridio Mantovani (10540 GRT, built 1939) escorted by the destroyer Alvise da Mosto, was attacked by three Blenheim bombers of 18 Squadron and hit by two bombs. The destroyer attempted to tow her but at 1650 B/1, a new attack by four Blenheims from 107 Squadron finished the tanker off.

1705 B/29 - In position 39°20'N, 17°33'E, when at 80 feet, HE was heard to the north-north-east. Turned on to estimated 45° track. HE of two destroyers and heavy units was reported. One of the destroyers was on a steady bearing, so P 31 remained at 40 feet until she passed overhead. This gave her course accurately as 165°. She was doing 200 revs.

Turning on to a 100° track and on coming to periscope depth, a cruiser was seen right ahead. This was the leading cruiser out of three. She was thought to be a heavy cruiser of the Trento-class. There were at least four destroyers in the screen, one of which was thought to be a Navigatori-class. The other two cruisers could not be identified in the prevailing weather conditions. Both were seen to have a funnel abaft the mainmast.
1719 B/29 - Fired four torpedoes at the second cruiser. All torpedoes were reported running correctly. Three explosions were heard 3 to 4 minutes after firing.
After firing P 31 turned to starboard on the same course of the enemy, at 8 knots. The main HE drew ahead very quickly, but slow HE of two ships was reported on the port quarter.
1726 B/29 - An explosion of three times the intensity of the previous ones, took place and no further HE was heard from that bearing. The remaining ships disappeared at high on a bearing of 165°.

1745 B/29 - Returned to periscope depth but it was too dark to see anything. It was decoded to reload at once in case a damaged ship might be in the vicinity. This however, took much longer than was expected due to a new fore-ends crew, and it was not completed until 2100 hours. (14)

30 Nov 1941 At 0035 B/30, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) sighted two torpedo boats which appeared to be carrying out an A/S sweep, frequently altering course and speed. Dived in position 39°16'N, 17°51'E and went deep.
[We have been unable to identify these vessels. No destroyers or torpedo boats should have been in this area at this time.]
At 0122 B/30, P 31 was back at periscope depth and sighted a motor torpedo boat at only 200 yards.
At 0235 B/30, P 31 surfaced.
Between 1230 B/30 and 1400 B/30, about twenty-five underwater explosions were heard. (14)
1 Dec 1941 At 0410 B/1, east-south-east of Punta Alice, in position 39°10'N, 17°57'E, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) dived when two destroyers were seen at long range proceeding on course 320°.
This were probable destroyers from the Duca d’Aosta- squadron [see the event for 29 November for more information].
At 0510 B/1, underwater explosions were heard. [This must have been the attack by HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, DSO, RN) on an Italian cruiser force.]
At 1900 B/1, course was set to leave patrol and return to Malta.

At 2130 B/1, to the east of Crotone, in approximate position 39°00'N, 18°00'E, two Spica-class torpedo boats were sighted sweeping to the north-west. They did not approach P 31 which was running on the surface at that time. She did not had to dive.
[We have been unable to identify these destroyers / torpedo boats. None should have been in the area around this time.] (14)
4 Dec 1941 At 0830 B/4, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) ended her 5th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (14)

13 Dec 1941 At 1300 B/13, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) departed Malta for her 6th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to form a patrol line in the Ionian Sea with HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki). The patrol line was as follows 4 - 37°10'N, 18°46'E - 070° - 10 - 160° - 30 with HMS P 34 in position 'J' (37°00'N, 18°46'E), with HMS Upholder in position 'K', HMS P 31 in position 'L' and ORP Sokol in position 'M'.
HMS P 31 was thus to patrol in approximate position 37°17'N, 19°09'E.
14 Dec 1941 At 2000 B/14, in position 37°26'N, 19°16'E, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) dived to evade an illuminated hospital ship proceeding on course 180°.
This was the Italian hospital ship Sicilia (9646 GRT, built 1924) which had sailed from Bari at 1230 B/13 and arrived at Benghazi at 1030 B/16. (14)

14 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 2145 B/14, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) and ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki); Proceed with despatch to establish patrol line 2 - 39°11'N, 17°46'E, - 60° - 15 - 150° - 20. P 31 in position 'L' (39°13'N, 17°46'E), Sokol in position 'M'. (17)
15 Dec 1941 At 1530 B/15, in position 38°18'N, 18°27'E, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) sighted a single two-funnel destroyer hull down. Enemy course was 140° at 24 knots.
This was the Italian destroyer Antonio Pigafetta. She had sailed from Taranto at 0855 B/15, carrying petrol for the Afrikakorps and reached Benghazi at 1130 B/16. (14)

16 Dec 1941 At 0001 B/16, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) set course to proceed to position 39.27'N, 17.24'E to relieve HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), this in accordance with the orders received in the orders in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1150 B/15. (18)

16 Dec 1941 At 1340 B/16, east of Punta Alice, in approximate position 39°20'N, 17°29'E, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat. This vessel was transmitting and was carrying out an A/S sweep in the area at a speed of 10 knots.
At 2200 B/16, in position 39°20'N, 17°24'E, HE of a single destroyer or torpedo boat was picked up but nothing was sighted. Visibility was about one mile at that time.
In both instances this was most likely the Italian torpedo boat Lince sailed from Taranto for an A/S patrol at 0709 B/16 and arrived at Crotone at 2400 B/16. (14)
17 Dec 1941 At 0230 B/17, in position 39.26'N, 17.25'E, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) sighted an auxiliary schooner patrolling.

At 0935 B/17, in position 39°22'N, 17°19'E, at a distance of 7000 yards, HMS P 31 sighted an Italian submarine of the Santorre [Santarosa] class apparently returning to Taranto. P 31 proceeded to intercept but the enemy submarine dived 5 minutes later and the attack was broken off.

There is no evidence of Italian submarines in this area at that time. Ammiraglio Cagni was returning to Taranto on this morning but she was about 45 miles to the north-east of P 31 and remained on the surface until she reached Taranto. (14)

17 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1903 B/17, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) and ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki) to form a patrol line. HMS Unbeaten was to occupy the central position in 39°16'N, 17°43'E with the other two submarines on a line of 060°, 10 miles apart. (17)

18 Dec 1941 At 0320 B/18, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) arrived in a new position (39°05'N, 17°32'E) as had been ordered a signal from the Capt. S.10 timed 1903 B/17. She formed a new patrol line together with HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) and ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki).
At 0900 B/18, the upperworks of a 3000 ton merchant vessel and smoke of another vessel were seen rounding Cape Colonne. They were proceeding northwards.
[We have been unable to identify this vessel. Possibly due to part of the Maricotraf file for Dec. 41 / Jan. 42 is not available to us or might even be missing in the Italian archives.]
At 2300 B/18, HMS P 31 withdrew to the southward for the night in accordance with the orders in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2141 B/18. (14)
19 Dec 1941 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) is hunted and depth charged by an Italian destroyer or torpedo boat.

This was the Italian torpedo boat Lince which sailed from Taranto at 0000 B/19 for an A/S sweep between 39°00'N / 39°20'N and 17°42'E / 17°55'E and returned at 2110 B/19. [Unfortunately, we do not have an action report for this day and there is no mention in the Supermarina diaries of her dropping depth charges.]

Later, to the east of Crotone, HMS P 31 attacked what are believed to be Italian cruisers. A full salvo of four torpedoes was fired but no hits were obtained. This was most likely an attack on the light cruisers of the 7th Cruiser Division, Emanuele Filiberto Duca d’Aosta, Eugenio Montecuccoli and Muzio Attendolo which were returning to Taranto after having covered a convoy to North Africa. These cruisers were escorted by the destroyers Aviere, Ascari and Camicia Nera.

Raimondo Montecuccoli reported being missed by five torpedoes (only four were actually fired). Muzio Attendolo immediately dropped depth charges, imitated by the destroyers but these were only for intimidation purposes. Seems like some of these landed quite close to HMS P 31.

0915 B/19 - In position 39°00'N, 17°30'E, HMS P 31 sighted the top and mast of a cruiser and smoke of another ship. HE was reported as 'probably reciprocating'. The vessel altered course 180° and proceeded northwards. This odd behaviour made it seem possible that this was a decoy hoping to make submarines attack her and disclose their positions.

1316 B/19 - A small destroyer (or torpedo boat) was sighted in position 39°05'N, 17°28'E. She was closing and A/S transmissions were picked up. P 31 remained at periscope depth and took avoiding action. The destroyer's tactics were to stop and presumably listened and then go ahead using her Asdic.
1330 B/19 - the destroyer attacked and force HMS P 31 deep. Twelve depth charges were dropped at intervals up to 1400 B/19.

1406 B/19 - HMS P 31 returned to periscope depth and proceeded at slow speed to keep the stern on to the destroyer. Up to 1500 B/19, eight more depth charges were dropped but the enemy did not appear to be in contact.
1540 B/19 - The destroyer increased speed and turned towards forcing P 31 to go deep. Sixteen depth charges were dropped.
1610 B/19 - P 31 appeared to be clear and returned to periscope depth. The destroyers was still sweeping in the area.
1613 B/19 - The destroyer dropped one depth charge and then proceeded to the southward. Further HE was reported.
1632 B/19 - Sighted destroyer bearing 135° with haze of smoke behind her.
1640 B/19 - Two large ships with smoke of a third were seen bearing 151°. Range was 9000 yards. Closed at high speed for six minutes. Four destroyers were now in sight.

1648 B/19 - It was observed that the enemy had altered course. The destroyers appeared to be zigging independently and were rushing about at high speed. The large ship that had been seen appeared to have a large beam and were thought at the time to be battleships. After return to Malta and consultation it was thought they were cruisers.
1652 B/19 - P 31 was right ahead of a destroyer at 900 yards. Went to 50 feet and the destroyer passed over the stern.
1654 B/19 - It was observed that the enemy had altered course to 305°. The big ships were seen to alter course together.
1658 B/19 - In position 39°05'N, 17°31'E, fired four torpedoes from 1000 yards. On completion P 31 altered course and increased speed to full.
1703 B/19 - Destroyers were reported coming in on bearing 340°.
1704 B/19 - Depth charging started. A total of twenty-seven were counted. The second pattern dropped was fairly close.
1738 B/19 - Returned to periscope depth. Two destroyers were seen bearing 010°. They were making a lot of smoke. Enemy course was 350°.
2010 B/19 - Surfaced and made an enemy report. Also reloaded the torpedo tubes.
2330 B/19 - Set course to return to Malta as ordered by the Capt. S.10's signals timed 1635 B/19 and 2125 B/19. (14)
22 Dec 1941 Around 0930 B/22, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) ended her 6th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (14)
27 Dec 1941 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) is docked in No.1 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (19)

6 Jan 1942 While in dock at Malta, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) is damaged by bomb splinters during an air raid. The result was 28 holes in the pressure hull, 23 holes in the superstructure and 4 holes in underwater fittings. Electrical equipment was damaged in the vicinity of the splinter holes. Three of the crew were injured. (20)
24 Jan 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) is undocked. (20)

2 Feb 1942 At 2015 B/2, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) departed Malta for her 7th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the west of Tripoli in area 'N', between 11°50'E and 12°55'E and south of 33°55'N.
On departure a deep dive trial was carried out.
4 Feb 1942 At 1330 B/4, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) entered area 'N' and proceeded to concentrate patrol in the vicinity of convoy routes converging on position 33°10'N, 12°00'E. (21)
6 Feb 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) attacked but missed a vessel which was most probably the Italian transport Bolsena (2384 GRT, built 1918) off Zuwara, Libya.
The Bolsena had sailed from Tunis at 0400/5 and arrived at Tripoli at 0900 B/7.

1431 B/6 - In position 33°07'N, 12°03'E sighted a laden merchant vessel of about 1500 tons. Range was about 5 nautical miles. No surface escort was present but there was an air escort. Enemy (mean) course was 090° but she was zig-zagging. Closed at high speed.
1459 B/6 - Fired three torpedoes from 5000 yards. No explosions were heard.

1508 B/6 - Observed that the ship had altered course and was now heading towards the coast. The aircraft prevented further observations or gun action. As the sea was oily calm the torpedo tracks were most likely sighted.
2020 B/6 - Wile deep during twilight, HE was reported. Surfaced and sighted a fully illuminated hospital ship.
This was the Italian hospital ship Aquileia (9448 GRT, built 1914) which sailed from Tripoli at 1630 B/6 for Naples where she arrived at 0835 B/9.
2135 B/6 - A torpedo boat carried out an A/S sweep in the area. HMS P 31 dived and the torpedo boat was heard stopping her engines every 5 minutes to listen.
2200 B/6 - The torpedo boat was clear so HMS P 31 surfaced.

This was most probably the Italian torpedo boat Perseo which had sailed from Tripoli at 1849 B/5, to meet the German transport Trapani (1855 GRT, built 1926) and escort her to Tripoli. At 1016 B/6, she encountered Bolsena on the opposite course. At 1053 B/6, she sighted Trapani near position 34°50'N, 12°00'E and they arrived at Tripoli at 1245 B/7. There are no explanations why it took so long except that aircraft were heard at 0030 B/7, and flares were observed at 0255 B/7.

The Italian torpedo boat Calliope was also at sea but could not have been the ship sighted. She had sailed at 1800 B/6 from Tripoli, escorting the Italian transport Bosforo (3648 GRT, built 1929) to Sidi Bilal where they arrived at 2135 B/6 and they sailed from there at 0135 B/7 for Pantelleria (2315 B/7-0752 B/8) and arrived at Palermo at 2110 B/8. (21)

7 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 0006 B/7, the Vice Admiral, Malta, informed HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) of the following; At 2300 B/6, one medium motor vessel and one escort vessel in position 34°02'N, 11°37'E. Enemy course 180°, speed 8 knots. Swordfish attacking at about 0200 B/7.
In a follow up signal timed 0533 B/7, HMS P 31 was informed of the following; My 0006 B/7, Swordfish claim one hit on merchant ship in approximate position 33°40'N, 11°15'E at 0320 B/7.

These signals referred to the Italian tanker Rondine (6468 GRT, built 1924) escorted by the Italian destroyer Premuda and the Italian torpedo boat Polluce. The tanker had sailed from Palermo at 1000 B/5 escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Aretusa followed at 1125 B/5, by the Italian torpedo boat Polluce which sailed from Palermo and reinforced the escort at 1310 B/5. At 1338 B/5, Aretusa sighted a torpedo wake and dropped depth charges, then proceeded to Trapani and arrived at 1500 B/5. The destroyer Premuda sailed from Trapani at 1710 B/5 to take over command of the escort. The convoy was located by an ASV Wellington which dropped flares and guided two Swordfish of 830 Squadron, one equipped with flares and the other armed with a torpedo and they attacked at 0255 B/7 and the Swordfish torpedo bomber (Sub. Lt. Swithinbank) was shot down by the combined fire of Polluce and Rondine, 15 miles north of Ras Turgoeness (Djerba Island) The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1708 B/7-1714 B/7. (22)

7 Feb 1942 At 0415 B/7, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) picked up HE of a destroyer or torpedo boat. It appeared this vessel was carrying out an A/S search in the area. Eight minutes later HMS P 31 dived to avoid being sighted. The enemy vessel later proceeded eastwards towards Tripoli.

This was possibly the Italian torpedo boat Calliope which had sailed at 1800 B/6 from Tripoli, escorting the Italian transport Bosforo (3648 GRT, built 1929) to Sidi Bilal where they arrived at 2135 B/6 and they sailed from there at 0135 B/7 for Pantelleria (2315 B/7-0752 B/8) and arrived at Palermo at 2110 B/8. (22)

8 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1112 B/8, the Vice-Admiral Malta informed ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) and HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) of the following; One small merchant ship had been sighted at 0940 B/8 in position 35°50'N, 11°50'E, course 175° at 10 knots. (22)

11 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1415 B/11, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) and HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN); Leave patrol to arrive Marsamxett at 0720 B/14 by proceeding through the following positions; HMS Unbeaten 35°00'N, 12°25'E and 35°36'N, 14°10'E. HMS P 31 33°45'N, 12°35'E and 35°32'N, 14°14'E. Submarines encountered after 2200 B/13 are not to be attacked. (22)

12 Feb 1942 At 0618 B/12, a main vent was accidentally opened and HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) took a steep angle bow down. A large quantity of water was shipped down the conning tower. Full speed astern was ordered.

At 0630 B/12, the submarine was stopped and surfaced on a level keel. It was discovered that Lt. M.H. Gardner, RN, and S/Lt. R.C. Bucknall, RN were missing. The flooded compartment was bailed out and the boat was ventilated as clorine gas had filled the boat. Meanwhile S/Lt. Bucknall, RN was seen in the water and he was able to climb back on board.
At 0645 B/12, a seach for Lt. Gardner was commenced in position 33°18'N, 12°01'E.
At 0730 B/12, HMS P 31 dived and continued the search until 1030 B/12. Lt. Gardner was unfortunately not sighted.
At 2015 B/12, HMS P 31 surfaced and left patrol to return to Malta in accordance with the orders in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1415 B/11. (23)
14 Feb 1942 At 0815 B/14, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) ended her 7th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (21)

26 Feb 1942 At 1945 B/26, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) departed Malta for her 8th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to off Kerkennah in area 'K' between 35°00'N and 34°20'E and west of 12°26'E.
She was to proceed trough position 35°34'N, 14°12'E at 0001 B/27 and then through position 34°40'N, 12°30'E where she was to patrol until dark on the 28th before entering area 'K'.

28 Feb 19420400 B/28, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) commenced patrolling on position 34.40'N, 12.30'E on the Tripoli-Lampion route. This in accordance with the sailing orders. This position should have been left at dusk on the 28th Lt. Kershaw decided to extend this till dawn on 1 March as the prevailing gale and sea state might well have delayed the expected enemy shipping (from a large convoy that had arrived at Tripoli on the 23rd) in unloading at and leaving Tripoli.
No enemy shipping was encountered though. (21)
1 Mar 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) commenced to patrol in the vicinity of the convoy routes between Kerkennah No.4 buoy and Tripoli. (21)
2 Mar 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) patrolled near Kerkennah No.7 buoy. (21)

2 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 1944 B/2, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) was given the following orders by the Capt. S.10; Shift patrol to within 30 miles of Lampion south of 35°30'N in anticipation of north and south traffic between position 33°08'N, 12°57'E and close to west of the island. (25)
4 Mar 1942 At 0220 B/4, well to the south of Lampedusa, in position 34°56'N, 12°24'E, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) sighted a large destroyer proceeding on course 350° at 20 knots.
This was the Italian torpedo boat Circe which had sailed from Tripoli at 1800 B/3 and arrived at Trapani at 1303 B/4. (21)

5 Mar 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Marin Sanudo (5958 GRT, built 1926) about 10 nautical miles west-south-west of Lampedusa in position 35°27'N, 12°12'E. P 31 was hunted by her two escorts, the Italian torpedo boats Cigno and Procione following this attack and they dropped respectively twenty-two and twelve depth charges.
Marin Sanudo and her escort had sailed from Trapani at 1900 B/3-1940 B/4 for Tripoli (carrying Italian troops, 40 German troops, 59 vehicles, 30 motorcycles and 900 tons of German army stores).

After the torpedo attack, Procione dropped twelve 50 kg depth-charges set at 50 metres and Cigno thirteen 50 kg and nine 100 kg depth-charges also set at 50 metres (according to the Procione report, however the Cigno report mentions that she only dropped twenty depth charges). Procione picked up 120 survivors (two died soon afterwards) and Cigno another 45 of the 220 on board. The two torpedo boats were returning to Trapani when they received an order from Supermarina to continue to Tripoli, and they arrived there at 0900 B/6-0950 B/6. The Italian naval auxiliary F 55 / Meta (243 GRT, built 1929) sailed from Lampedusa at 1250 B/5 to look for survivors and returned at 1855 B/5 apparently without finding any.
1245 B/5 - In position 35°27'N, 12°12'E sighted an aircraft carrying out an A/S sweep. Went deep.
1305 B/5 - Heard H.E., bearing 340° and came to periscope depth.

1306 B/5 - Sighted a motor vessel escorted by two Folgore-class destroyers, one on either bow. Range was 7200 yards. The nearest destroyer had FL on her bow. [Here Lt. Kershaw must have made a mistake, destroyer Fulmine (FL) had been sunk on 9 November 1941.]
1310 B/5 - Commenced a retiring turn. The target was on a steady course with the destroyers zigging independently. The target was a vessel of about 7000 tons and fully laden.
1320 B/5 - Decided to attack with a salvo of four torpedoes. It was a large vessel, fully laden with a heavy escort. It must be a very valuable ship for the enemy.
1321 B/5 - Went to 40 feet as the sea was flat calm and the periscope would otherwise be sighted. Decided to attack using Asdic.
1323 B/5 - Fired four torpedoes from 780 yards. All hit. H.E. of the target ceased. P 31 increased to full speed and retired to the north.
1327 B/5 - Went to slow speed.
1329 B/5 - Five depth charges were dropped, fairly close.
1331 B/5 - Four depth charges exploded, very close.

1333 B/5 - Settled on the bottom at 240 feet. By 1345 hours a total of thirty depth charges had been dropped and minor damage was received. The destroyers were overhead, stopping and starting their engines. Decided to remain on the bottom. It was some comfort that the chart 'from the latest Italian Government Surveys' gave the greatest depth as 180 feet.
1600 B/5 - The final depth charges were dropped and the destroyers were heard proceeding to the North.
1700 B/5 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight. Went deep again to reload. (21)

5 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 1943 B/5, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) and ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.K.S. Koziolkowski, ORP); My signal timed 1944 B/2 to P 31, most likely route for northbound traffic appears direct from position 34°53'N, 11°43'E to position 36°47'N, 11°54'E being made during daylight. Shift patrols as follows Sokol to 35°32'N, 11°51'E - 355° - 70 - 20 and P 31 to 35°00'N, 11°10'E - 000° - 30 - 70 and concentrate as circumstances permit on route given until reconnaissance indicates otherwise. Important southbound traffic may be expected on route in signal quoted [1944 B/2 to P 31]. (25)

7 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 2050 B/7, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN); Leave patrol through positions 35°20'N, 12°35'E and 35°40'N, 14°08'E to enter Marsamxett [Malta] at 0645 B/11. (25)

9 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 1745 B/9, the Vice Admiral Malta informed HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.K.S. Koziolkowski, ORP) of the following; Force K will sweep north from position 36°05'N, 11°50'E between 0200 B/10 and 0300 B/10.

In a follow up signal the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to these three submarines; My 1745 B/9. P 31 patrol in position 35°27'N, 12°23'E from 0400 B/10 to intercept a 14 knot ship on approximate course 140° then return to arrive Grand Harbour.
Sokol keep west of 11°40'E until 0400 B/10 then return to northern route.
Una remain on that route south of 35°30'N.

[The 14-knot ship referred to was the 14-knot transport Luciano Manara (8396 GRT, built 1942) escorted by the destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare and the torpedo boats Centauro and Polluce which sailed from Trapani at 1600 B/9-1700 B/9. Shortly after leaving Trapani, Centauro had defects which forced her to return to Trapani at 1730 B/9. From 2125 B/9, the convoy came under air attack and Luciano Manara was damaged by RAF torpedo bombers (three Swordfish and three Albacores, one Albacore cashed on landing on return to Malta) at 2310 B/9. She dropped anchor at Pantelleria at 0053 B/10 and then sailed again at 0950 B/10 to return to Palermo where she arrived at 0135 B/11. (ULTRA source, see DEFE3/939/2, pp. 1091-1092)]

[Force K, made up of the light cruisers HMS Cleopatra (Capt. M.S. Slattery, RN), HMS Penelope (Capt. A.D. Nicholl, RN) and the destroyer HMS Kingston (Cdr. P. Somerville, DSO, DSC and Bar, RN) departed Malta around 2000 B/9 to intercept the above convoy. As the convoy broke off its passage they failed to find it and they returned to Malta around 0900 B/10.] (26)
10 Mar 1942 At 0306 B/10, east-south-east of Lampedusa, near position 35°27'N, 12°23'E, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), sighted a destroyer carrying out an A/S sweep in the area.
This was perhaps either one out of the Italian destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi or Fuciliere which sailed at 1855 B/9-1946 B/9 from Tripoli and arrived at Palermo at 1450 B-1500 B/10.
At 0837 B/10, a destroyer and float plane were seen carrying out an A/S sweep in the vicinity.
At 0905 B/10, nine depth charges and at 0930 B/10, fifteen depth charges were dropped. None were close.
Aircraft were seen patrolling throughout the day and between 1230 B/10 and 1341 B/10, sixteen bombs were dropped. The destroyer was heard to use it's Asdic at 0923 B/10.

The Italian destroyer Antonio da Noli and the Italian torpedo boat Pallade sailed from Tripoli at 1917 B/9-2034 B/9 escorting thr Italian transport Monviso (5322 GRT, built 1941). At 0030 B/10, they received a signal from Supermarina that a naval force had sailed from Malta on a westerly course. To observe radio silence, the destroyer communicated by megaphone his intention to turn back for an hour to reduce the probability of an encounter. This was done at 0145 B/10 and at 0245 B/10 they resumed their course toward Lampedusa. At 0900 B/10, they sighted Lampione and ten minutes later, the torpedo boat Pallade reported a submarine dead ahead and dropped depth charges. At 0935 B/10, she signalled that she had sunk the submarine; she was recovering debris and would rejoin later. At 1000 B/10, an Italian reconnaissance aircraft dropped a green flare 3000 metres astern, and this was interpreted as a torpedo attack, and the convoy turned 40 degrees to starboard. At 1038 B/10, a similar signal was made, the convoy turned to port and Antonio da Noli dropped four 30-kg depth charges for intimidation purposes. It was later realised that the aircraft had dropped only the first part of a reconnaissance signal (the other was a white flare) and not a signal for a torpedo attack. At 1415 B/10, the convoy was off Pantelleria, and at 1515 B/10, Pallade rejoined them and reported that a submarine had not been sunk, as the debris recovered was from a merchant ship wreck (later identified as the German Preussen, sunk by air attack on 22 July 1941). Antonio da Noli informed Pallade that the torpedo boat was required to reinforce the Manara-convoy (Manara had been torpedoed by an enemy aircraft at 23260 B/9) but at 1705 B/10, this order was cancelled. At 1940 B/10, they were off Marsala and Pallade was detached to scout ahead. The convoy escort was reinforced by the torpedo boat boat Centauro off Trapani. The convoy arrived off Palermo at 0130 B/11, however they were told they could not enter harbour (no reason given, perhaps magnetic mines had been dropped by enemy aircraft ?) and the four ships dropped anchor off Lungomare. At 1120 B/11-1200 B/11, they finally arrived at Palermo.

At 1500 B/10, smoke of a steamer was sighted. This steamer approached close round Cape Ponente and then altered course to 120° close inshore of Lampedusa. The steamer was out of range. P 31 closed at high submerged speed. A course was set to intercept the steamer when it would clear Lampedusa. The vessel was however lost from sight and not seen again.
Possibly this was the Italian tug Instancabile (former-Yugoslav Spasilac, 740 tons, built 1930).

A convoy consisting of the German transport Wachtfels (8467 GRT, built 1928) and the Italian tug Instancabile escorted by the Italian destroyer Bersagliere had sailed from Tripoli at 1900 B/9. The Italian torpedo boat Castore sailed from Tripoli at 2034 B/9 and rejoined the convoy at 2240 B/9. At 0830 B/10, they were off Kerkennah Buoy No.4 and at 1150 B/10, Instancabile was detached to Lampedusa. At 1156 B/10, Bersagliere signalled the presence of a submarine and dropped depth charges. Castore was detached to deal with it and obtained an echo on her sonar. She dropped a first pattern of eight depth charges followed by another of four. At 1300 B, the torpedo boat rejoined the convoy. At 0040 B/11, off Marittimo, they were illuminated by flares but no air attack ensued. At 0730 B/11, Bersagliere was detached to Palermo where she arrived at 1120 B/11. Castore assumed command of the convoy and, at 1725 B/11, they arrived at Naples.
At 2040 B/10, a fully lit hospital ship was seen in the anchorage off Lampedusa.
At 2110 B/10, the hospital ship switched off it's lights as did the harbour lights which had been turned on 15 minutes before.

At 2118 B/10, HE was heard to start, very close. At first this was thought to be a motor torpedo boat and P 31 dived. The HE changed bearing very slowly and was considered to be a submarine charging. Position was about 10 miles south-east of Lampedusa. P 31 surfaced and tried to stalk without result.

The Italian hospital ship Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1926) had sailed from Tripoli at 1815 B/8 with 724 wounded and arrived at Naples at 0750 B/11 and thus could not be the hospital ship seen. We could find no other hospital ships in the area. (21)

12 Mar 1942 At 0645 B/12, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) ended her 8th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. At 0435 B/12, while approaching Malta through the south-east swept channel, HMS P 31 had to dive for an approaching aircraft which had been seen to have dropped a bomb ten minutes earlier. HMS P 31 surfaced at 0510 B/12 and continued her passage up the swept channel. (21)
23 Mar 1942 At 1945 B/23, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) departed Malta for her 9th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean). She is ordered to patrol in the Tyrrhenian Sea.

She was to proceed through position 37°15'N, 13°05'E after 0001 B/25, through QBB 65 and then keep north-west of the line Marettimo-Naples to patrol in the northern Tyrrhenean Sea. The following schedule was ordered; Marittimo 25/3, Ischia 28/3, Ponza 29/3, Giglio 31/3, Civita Vecchia l/4, Ischia 3/4 to arrive at Malta A.M./7.

25 Mar 1942 At 1040 B/25, south of Marettimo Island in position 37°45'N, 12°05'E, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) sighted smoke and masts of a merchant vessel in the swept channel 225° from Favignana Island.
[We have been unable to identify this vessel.}
At 1400 B/25, a large single funnel destroyer passed Marettimo Island on a northerly course.

[We have been unable to identify this destroyer, The destroyer Ugolino Vivaldi and the torpedo boats Circe, Giuseppe Dezza and Generale Carlo Montanari operated from Trapani during this day, but either before or after this time.] (21)

27 Mar 1942 At 1414 B/27, in position 40°42'N, 13°57'E, sighted two schooners. One was patrolling and one was proceeding southwards. No attack was made on this southbound schooner, she appeared light and was not considered worth a torpedo and the sea state was not suitable for gun action. (21)

29 Mar 1942 At 2000 B/29, south-south-east of Ponza Island, in position 40°49'N, 13°01'E, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) sighted a hospital ship bearing 160° at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 270°.
This was the Italian hospital ship Virgilio (11,718 GRT, built 1926) which sailed from Naples at 1500 B/29 and arrived at Genoa on 30 March (time not known) for a refit. (28)
1 Apr 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) attacked but missed a tanker [or engine aft merchant vessel] with three torpedoes near Civitavecchia, Italy.
[We have been unable to identify this vessel.]
0845 B/1 - Sighted two trawlers leaving Civitavecchia harbour. When clear they streamed their sweeps and proceeded down the searched channel for 14 miles, after which they turned 180° to starboard.
0925 B/1 - Sighted a merchant vessel and four schooners leaving the harbour of Civitavecchia.
0955 B/1 - When the trawlers, that had been sighted earlier, were clear P 31 was able to start closing the merchant vessel. Three of the schooners were ahead of her and one was close astern.

1014 B/1 - In position 42°02'N, 11°31'E fired three torpedoes from 3000 yards. The merchant vessel, thought to be a 2000 ton tanker [or engine aft merchant vessel], turned away to comb the tracks and then returned to the harbour at high speed. (21)
2 Apr 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) attacked an Italian auxiliary A/S schooner with one torpedo about 10 nautical miles east-south-east of Ponza Island. The torpedo missed.
[We have been unable to identify this schooner.]

1750 B/2 - Sighted a large 3-masted schooner in position 40°49'N, 13°07'E. She was laying stopped. Started attack in which one torpedo was fired from 1400 yards. The torpedo missed just ahead. The schooner started her engines and turned down track. An aircraft circled HMS P 31 but dropped no bombs. (21)

3 Apr 1942 While patrolling off Ischia, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) sighted a large number of schooners patrolling within 5 miles from Imperatore Light. All remained in the vicinity the whole day.

P.M. on this day, HMS P 31 left patrol to return to Malta. She was routed through position 40°20'N, 13°50'E to intercept two escorted 14-knot northbound merchant vessels as had been ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1019 B/3.

This referred to the Italian transport Vettor Pisani (6339 GRT, built 1940) and the German transport Reichenfels (6339 GRT, built 1940) escorted by the Italian destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare and the Italian torpedo boat Lince which had sailed from Tripoli at 1855 B/2-1859 B/2. At 0430 B/3, the Italian destroyer Premuda sailed from Messina and joined the escort at 1725 B/3, At the same time, the torpedo boat Lince was detached to Messina, arriving at 0635 B/4. The convoy reached Naples at 0830 B/4-0920 B/4 [see file DEFE3/840/1 pp. 995 and 1025].
After proceeding through the above position P 31 was also routed through position 38°30'N, 12°44'E (to the north of Cape San Vito, Sicily) on her way back to Malta. (21)
8 Apr 1942 At 0100 B/8, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) ended her 9th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean) at Malta (Marsamxett).
She had earlier entered Grand Harbour at 2015 B/7 where she fuelled before proceeding to Marsamxett.
The former crew of HMS P 36 temporarily took charge of the submarine until 13 April to give her crew a rest.

[No logs of HMS P 31 are available for the months of April, May and June 1942. Some details for these months may thus be missing. These logs were most likely lost when HMS Medway was torpedoed and sunk.]
While submerged in Malta on 8 April, the submarine was shaken by a bomb which exploded nearby and developed a small leak. (21)

13 Apr 1942 Around 0510 B/13, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) departed Malta for her 10th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the south of the Strait of Messina in area 'M', between latitude 36°55'N, and the Italian coast and longitude 16°40'E and the coast of Sicily.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (21)

14 Apr 1942 At 0155 B/14, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) received the Vice Admiral Malta's signal timed 2335 B/13, which stated the following; Position of two large merchant ships, one destroyer and one other enemy vessel is 37°48'N, 15°20'E. Enemy course 210° speed 16 knots.

This referred to a convoy made up of the Italian transport Vettor Pisani (6339 GRT, built 1940) and the German transport Reichenfels (6339 GRT, built 1940) escorted by the Italian destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Nicolò Zeno and the Italian torpedo boat Pegaso (operation APRILIA). They had sailed from Naples at 0730 B/13-0925 B/13. They crossed the Straits of Messina at 2055 B/13. At 0940 B/13-1146 B/13, the Italian transport Ravello (6142 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the Italian destroyers Mitragliere and Turbine sailed from Brindisi. At 0835 B/14, Turbine was detached to Catania where she arrived at 1352 B/14. At 1212 B/13, the Italian transport Reginaldo Giuliani (3837 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the Italian destroyer Freccia sailed from Taranto. At 0600 B/14, the Vettor Pisani and the Ravello convoys joined up. At 0945 B/14, they merged with the Reginaldo Giuliani convoy. At 1545 B/14, they were attacked by eight Beaufort torpedo bombers. One torpedo exploded near Reichenfels causing minor damage. At 1615 B/14, an escorting aircraft signalled a submarine and Pegaso dropped depth charges but the aircraft corrected that the “submarine” was a dolphin. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 0932 B/15-1050 B/15. (29)

15 Apr 1942 At 0633 B/15, in position 37°30'N, 15°50'E, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) sighted masts and funnels of a medium merchant vessel proceeding on course 090° art 8 knots. HMS P 31 closed at high speed, but the vessel passed well out of range. She was escorted by a two-funnel torpedo boat. This vessel was considerably south of the usual route which was thought to be the result of an attack by HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) on 5 April. (21)

17 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1602 B/17 the Capt. S.10 informed HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) of the following; You were sighted this forenoon by the enemy, move position, spending 24 hours off Sicilian Coast. (29)
18 Apr 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) hoped to fire a Mark IV torpedo at the base of the viaduct north of Taormina but the bad weather prevented the attempt. (21)

19 Apr 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) developed a leak which became progressively worse most likely also due to the bad weather conditions. Lt. Kershaw decided to leave patrol and return to Malta to effect repairs. The leak was in the area where the bomb had fell while P 31 was dived in Lazeretto Creek at Malta on 8 April. (21)

20 Apr 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) spent the day near Cape Murro di Porco. Between 1230 B/20 and 1930 B/20 a destroyer and two aircraft were seen carrying out an A/S sweep. Hopes were high that something bigger might come to the area but nothing further happened.

The Italian torpedo boat Circe had sailed from Messina at 0154 B/20 and from 1355 B/20 to 0600 B/21 carried out an A/S search between 37°28'N / 37°54.5'N and 16°00'E and 16°23'E. She returned to Messina at 0913 B/21. This does not quite match HMS P 31’s description as Circe was nearly 30 miles north of Cape Murro di Porco but nothing else could be found in the Italian records.
Also two schooners were seen in the area, most likely patrolling though they may also have been fishing. (21)

21 Apr 1942 At 2030 B/21, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) ended her 10th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. She arrived through the north-east swept channel running her mine detector unit (M.D.U.) while making the passage. (21)

26 Apr 1942 At 0530 C/26, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) departed Malta for Alexandria. Due to heavy enemy air attacks it had been decided to retire the small number of submarines currently based at Malta to Alexandria.
On board was 1,5 tons of essential Flotilla spare gear and two officers and ten ratings from the spare submarine crew were on board.
HMS P 31 was to leave Malta through the north-east swept channel proceeding on course 071° from St. Elmo Light to position 36°00'N, 14°53'E thence direct to position 35°51'N, 15°15'E.
Further routing for HMS P 31 was through 35°01'N, 19°59'E and 32°12'N, 29°06'E to arrive Alexandria searched channel at 0530 C/5.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (30)

28 Apr 1942 At 1736 B/28, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) sighted a large, single funnel, destroyer and an aircraft carrying out an A/S sweep. The destroyer did not pass within range. [No position is given in the passage report.] (30)

4 May 1942 In a signal timed 1745 C/4, the Capt. S.1 informed HMS Porpoise (Cdr. E.F. Pizey, DSO, RN) and HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) of the following; HMS Jackal (Cdr. C.T. Jellicoe, DSC, RN) on A/S Patrol at end of searched channel. HMS P 611 (Lt. P.S. Skelton, RN) sailed 1830 C/4 to the north-east. One trawler E.T.A. A.M. from westward. HMS Coventry (Capt. R.J.R. Dendy, RN) and destroyers leave harbour 0630 C/5 for exercises.

[In the end HMS Coventry did not sail for the exercises until around 1345 C/5. The destroyers HMS Jervis (Capt. A.L. Poland, CB, DSO and Bar, DSC, RN), HMS Janus (Cdr. J.A.W. Tothill, DSC, RN), HMS Jackal (which apparently had not been out on A/S patrol after all, it appears she had been replaced on A/S patrol by HMS Kelvin (Cdr. M.S. Townsend, OBE, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Kipling (Cdr. A. St. Clair-Ford, DSO, RN), HMS Sikh (Capt. St.J.A. Micklethwait, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Lively (Lt.Cdr. W.F.E. Hussey, DSO, DSC, RN) and the escort destroyer HMS Airedale (Lt.Cdr. A.G. Forman, DSC, RN) did sail for exercises at 0630 C/6.]
5 May 1942 Around 0730 C/5, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) arrived at Alexandria from Malta. (30)
18 May 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) departed Alexandria for Port Said. (31)
20 May 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) arrived at Port Said from Alexandria. Most likely she was docked here but as there is no log available we are not sure of this. (31)
3 Jun 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) departed Port Said for Alexandria. (31)
4 Jun 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) arrived at Alexandria from Port Said. (31)

6 Jun 1942 At 1800 C/6, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) departed Alexandria for her 11th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Ionian Sea to provide cover during 'Operation Vigorous'.

She was to patrol in position 'B' (35°43'N, 18°55'E) of a patrol line 230° - 050° - 12 miles apart together with HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), in position 'A', HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), in position 'C' and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) in position 'D'.
13 Jun 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) arrived in her assigned patrol position on the patrol line, position 'B' which was in 35°43'N, 18°55'E.'
At 0608 C/13, she tried to establish S.S./T. communication with HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) but failed to do so.
At 1700 C/13, S.S./T. communication was established with HMS P 34. (30)
14 Jun 1942 At 0307 B/14, in position 35.42'N, 18.58'E, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) sighted an unidentified submarine.
At 1530 B/14, HMS P 31 was in S.S./T. communication with HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN). (30)

15 Jun 1942 At 0604 C/15, in position 35°46'N, 18°50'E, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) sighted two battleships and three cruisers bearing 045° at extreme range against the dawn sky. HMS P 31 was on their starboard beam.

At 0613 C/15, aircraft were seen going into attack and two torpedo hits, followed by bombs or depth charges were heard. The enemy fleet altered course and the cruisers drew ahead. Both enemy units proceeded to the south-south-east.
At 0620 C/15, the only ship in sight was a cruiser which appeared to be damaged. She was steering north-west at slow speed. HMS P 31 altered course to intercept.

At 0916 C/15, another air attack developed and a series of sharp explosions were heard. The cruiser was burning gloriously and the crew had gathered on the quarterdeck. Two destroyers were circling and laying a smokescreen.

At 1020 C/15, while P 31 was running at 60 feet a dull rumbling explosion was heard. On coming to periscope depth, two minutes later, there was no sign of the cruiser. When last seen, her position was 35°42'N, 18°59'E. P 31 withdrew at 3 knots on course 150° to comply with the orders in the Capt. S.1's signal timed 0601 C/15. [From 0701 C/15 steer 150° at 3 knots while submerged.]
[The damaged cruiser, which was the Italian heavy cruiser Trento had indeed been sunk, the culprit was HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN).]

At 1730 C/15, HMS P 31 received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1315 C/15 and the Vice-Admiral Malta's signal timed 1515 C/15. Lt. Kershaw decided to comply with the former, in spite of the time or origin. P 31 surfaced and set course to 070°.

[Capt. S.1's signal timed 1315 C/15 gave the following; Proceed with all desparch to form new patrol lines. 1st Submarine Flotilla, 5 - 35°32'N, 20°10'E - 12 miles. 10th S/M Flotilla, 4 - 35°22'N, 19°01'E - 10 miles. All submarines with relative positions as before. Remain on the surface. Commanding Officers gave discretion to move to intercept.]
At 1800 C/15, HMS P 31 sighted HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) steering 270°.

At 1825 C/15, the Commander-in-Chief's signal 1701 C/15 was received which made it clear that HMS P 31 had obeyed the correct signal. Also the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1625 C/15 was received which contained the following orders for the submarines of the 10th Flotilla; Move patrol line 050° - 20 miles. (32)

16 Jun 1942 At 0410 C/16, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2321 C/15 which ordered the four submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla to return to their positions in the A - D patrol line. HMS P 31 thus set course to return to position 'B' (35°43'N, 18°55'E). (32)

17 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 1833 C/17, the Capt. S.1 ordered HMS Porpoise (Lt. L.W.A. Bennington, DSC, RN), HMS Thorn (Lt.Cdr. R.G. Norfolk, DSO, RN), HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) to proceeded towards Taranto with despatch and that additional orders would follow.
In a follow up signal timed 2015 C/17 the following orders were signalled; My 1835 C/17 adjust speed and time leaving present patrol area so as to arrive by dawn 20th.

In yet another signal timed 2101 C/17 the following orders were signalled; Establish patrol by dawn 20th as follows; HMS Thorn in the vicinity of position 40°04'N, 17°13'E HMS Thrasher in the vicinity of position 40°07'N, 17°26'E. HMS Porpoise in the vicinity of position 35°01'N, 16°29'E. 10th Submarine Flotilla was to patrol line; 4 - 39°47'N 17°17'E - 7 miles - 055°. HMS Una in position 'A', 39°47'N, 17°17'E. Remainder in present relative positions. [thus HMS P 31, HMS P 34 and HMS P 35 in positions 'B', 'C' and 'D' respectively.] Proceed direct to new position diving at dawn 20th on line 247° from Cape Maria di Leuca. (33)
19 Jun 1942 At 2230 C/19, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2049 C/19. It was not possible for HMS P 31 to reach the position ordered in time.

Lt. Kershaw decided that it was important to remain unseen to avoid giving away the presence of submarines. Further the signal did not give the composition, position, course and speed of the enemy which made appreciation of the the situation difficult. Lt. Kershaw decided that the line of advance of the enemy was expected to be 330° or 150° so course was altered to get onto this track. (30)

19 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 1109 C/19, the Capt. S.1, ordered HMS Porpoise (Lt. L.W.A. Bennington, DSC, RN), HMS Thorn (Lt.Cdr. R.G. Norfolk, DSO, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) in position 'C' and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) to return to Alexandria forthwith through following positions to arrive as stated; HMS Porpoise through positions 35°22'N, 18°11'E and 32°21'N, 28°01'E to arrive at dawn on the 22nd. HMS Thorn through positions 37°21'N, 20°30'E, 35°42'N, 21°57(?)'E and 32°08'N, 28°57'E to arrive at dawn on the 25th. The four boats of the 10th submarine flotilla were to cross Latitude 34.59'N in the following Longitudes; HMS Una in 21°28'E, HMS P 31 in 22°04'E, HMS P 34 in 22°40'E and HMS P 35 in 23°18'E. [More positions to proceed through are given in the signal but it contains several errors]. The four boats were to arrive off the boom before 2030 C/27 or after 0730 C/28. (33)

19 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 2059 C/19, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) and HMS Thorn (Lt.Cdr. R.G. Norfolk, DSO, RN); Establish patrol line forthwith 5 - 38°16'N, 19°47'E - 8 - 150° in the order HMS Una, HMS P 31, HMS P 34, HMS P 35, HMS Thrasher. HMS Una in position 38°16', 19°47'E. HMS Thorn proceeding as ordered in my signal timed 1109 C/19 is not to cross 37°N before 2200 C/20.

In a signal timed 2205 C/19, the above listed submarines were informed of the following by Capt. S.1; On reaching position in my 2059 C/19 proceed submerged at 3 knots on course 145° until 2130 hours. HMS Thrasher then return to patrol area keeping at least 10 miles to north east above course, and the four boats of the 10th S/M Flotilla proceed in accordance with my 1109 C/19. It is important that no submarine will be seen on the surface on the 20th.

In a signal timed 2259 C/19, the Capt. S.1 informed the submarines listed above of the following; My 2059 C/19. It is important that error in longitude should be reduced to a minimum. HMS Thrasher is to obtain accurate land fix at dawn, dive to position ordered to replace by 1200 hours or earlier if possible and then establish communication by SS/T to enable the other submarines to adjust their position. (33)
20 Jun 1942 At 1559 C/20, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1211 C/20 ordering her to return to Alexandria. Course was set accordingly. (30)

20 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 0401 C/20, the Capt. S.1, sent the following orders to HMS Thorn (Lt.Cdr. R.G. Norfolk, DSO, RN), HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN); My 2059 C/19 shift patrol line 22 miles from 267°. My 2205 C/19 for 145° read 134°. HMS Thorn conform.

In a follow up signal timed 0455 C/20 sent by the Capt. S.1 to HMS Thorn, HMS Una and HMS P 31 and repeated to the other submarines the following orders were given; My 0401 C/20, HMS Thorn patrol along a line between positions 37°22'N, 20°15'E and 36°50'N, 20°59'E. HMS Una and HMS P 31 return to position 37°55'N, 19° 35'E and then steer 134°.

In a signal timed 1211 C/20, the following orders were sent by Capt. S.1; HMS Thorn, HMS Una, HMS P 31, HMS P 34 and HMS P 35 return to Alexandria. Expected in accordance with previous orders. HMS Thrasher return to patrol area through 37°56'N, 20°03'E. (34)

21 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 2135 C/21, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the four submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla; Area 33°00'N, 47°00'E, 23°14'E - 310° - 65 miles is not to be crossed during night 22nd/23rd. HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) pass to southward of area. HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) passing to the south and HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) to the north of Gavdo Island. (34)

23 Jun 1942 In a signal timed 1117 C/23, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla; Proceed to following positions from whence you are expected end of searched channel at the approx. time given but submarines are not to arrive off the boom after 2030 hours or before 0730 hours. HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) through position 32°19'N, 29°08'E to arrive at (the end of the searched channel) 1401 C/27. HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) through position 31°39'N, 29°02'E to arrive at 0459 C/28. HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) through position 31°53'N, 28°55'E to arrive at 0801 C/28. HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) through position 32°01'N, 28°59'E to arrive at 0501 C/29. (34)
28 Jun 1942 Around 0750 C/28, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) ended her 11th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean) at Alexandria. (30)
29 Jun 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) departed Alexandria for Haifa. (31)
3 Jul 1942 Shortly before 1700 C/17, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, RN) arrived at Haifa from Alexandria. (35)

17 Jul 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) departed Haifa for her 12th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to proceed to Malta carrying out a short patrol to the south-west of Crete.
P 31 had one officer and ten ratings on board for passage to Malta as well as about five tons of cargo.
For the daily positions of HMS P 31 during this patrol see the map below.
18 Jul 1942 In a signal timed 1254 C/18 [or 1259 C/18], the Capt. S.1, ordered HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) to proceeded through position 34°05'N, 25°00'E en-route to her patrol area.

23 Jul 1942 In a signal timed 0937 C/23, the Senior Officer Submarines Alexandria, ordered HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) to patrol in the vicinity of Elaphonisi after having passed through position 34°18'N, 23°27'E. P 31 was also ordered not to cross longitude 23°30'E before 2200 C/24. She was also ordered to leave the area at dawn 26th to arrive in position 35°21'N, 16°19'E at about 0400 C/30.
HMS P 31 received this signal at 1420 C/23. (36)

25 Jul 1942 At 0600 C/25, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) arrived in position 34°18'N, 23°27'E. Course was altered to patrol to proceed towards Elaphanisi (near the south-east tip of Crete).
26 Jul 1942 At 0600 C/26, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) left patrol and set course for Malta in accordance with the Senior Officer Submarines Alexandria's signal timed 0937 C/23.
She was to proceed through position 35°21'N, 14°12'E on 1 August.
1 Aug 1942 Around 1250 C/1, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) ended her 12th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
Around 0947 C/1, she had surfaced in the submarine sanctuary off Malta and made rendezvous with HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which then swept her into the harbour. (37)
Convoy WS 21S and the concentration of the escort forces

Convoy WS 21S departed the Clyde on 2 August 1942. The convoy was made up of the following ships; American freighters; Almeria Lykes (7773 GRT, built 1940), Santa Elisa (8379 GRT, built 1941), British freighters; Brisbane Star (12791 GRT, built 1937), Clan Ferguson (7347 GRT, built 1938), Deucalion (7516 GRT, built 1930), Dorset (10624 GRT, built 1934), Empire Hope (12688 GRT, built 1941), Glenorchy (8982 GRT, built 1939), Melbourne Star (11076 GRT, built 1936), Port Chalmers (8535 GRT, built 1933), Rochester Castle (7795 GRT, built 1937), Waimarama (12843 GRT, built 1938), Wairangi (12436 GRT, built 1935), and the American tanker; Ohio (9264 GRT, built 1940).

These ships were escorted by light cruisers HMS Nigeria (Capt. S.H. Paton, RN, flying the flag of the Rear-Admiral 10th C.S., H.M. Burrough, CB, DSO, RN), HMS Kenya (Capt. A.S. Russell, RN) and the destroyers HMS Wishart (Cdr. H.G. Scott, RN), HMS Venomous (Cdr. H.W. Falcon-Stewart, RN), HMS Wolverine (Lt.Cdr. P.W. Gretton, OBE, DSC, RN), HMS Malcolm (A/Cdr. A.B. Russell, RN), HMS Amazon (Lt.Cdr.(Emgy) Lord Teynham, RN), HMS Derwent (Cdr. R.H. Wright, DSC, RN) and HMS Zetland (Lt. J.V. Wilkinson, RN).

A cover force made up of departed Scapa Flow on the same day. This force was made up of the battleships HMS Nelson (Capt. H.B. Jacomb, RN, flying the flag of Vice-Admiral E.N. Syfret, CB, RN) and HMS Rodney (Capt. J.W. Rivett-Carnac, DSC, RN). They were escorted by the destroyers HMS Ashanti (Cdr. R.G. Onslow, DSO, RN), HMS Eskimo (Cdr. E.G. Le Geyt, RN), HMS Somali (Cdr. E.N.V. Currey, DSC, RN), HMS Tartar (Cdr. St.J.R.J. Tyrwhitt, DSC, RN), HMS Pathfinder (Cdr. E.A. Gibbs, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Penn (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Swain, RN) and HMS Quentin (Lt.Cdr. A.H.P. Noble, DSC, RN). They were to rendez-vous with convoy WS 21S at sea on 3 August. HMS Penn was delayed by a defect and after topping off with fuel at Moville, Northern Ireland overtook the force and joined at sea.

The aircraft carrier HMS Victorious (Capt. H.C. Bovell, CBE, RN, flying the flag of Rear Admiral A.L.St.G. Lyster, CB, CVO, DSO, RN) and the light cruiser HMS Sirius (Capt. P.W.B. Brooking, RN) meanwhile had already left Scapa Flow on 31 July 1941 to rendez-vous with the convoy. They were escorted by the destroyers HMS Intrepid (Cdr. C.A.deW. Kitcat, RN), HMS Icarus (Lt.Cdr. C.D. Maud, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Fury (Lt.Cdr. C.H. Campbell, DSC and Bar, RN) and HMS Foresight (Lt.Cdr. R.A. Fell, RN). These ships were joined at sea on 1 August 1942 by the aircraft carrier HMS Argus (Capt. G.T. Philip, RN), loaded with spare fighter aircraft for the operation, and her two escorts the destroyers HMS Buxton (Lt.Cdr. I.J. Tyson, RD, RNR) and HMS Sardonyx (Lt.Cdr. A.F.C. Gray, RNR). HMS Argus and her two escorting destroyers had departed the Clyde on 31 July. HMS Buxton later split off and proceeded towards Canada and HMS Sardonyx proceeded to Londonderry.

The last ships to take part in the operation to depart the U.K. (Clyde around midnight during the night of 4/5 August) were the aircraft carrier HMS Furious (Capt. T.O. Bulteel, RN), loaded with Hurricane fighters for Malta, and her escorts, the light cruiser HMS Manchester (Capt. H. Drew, DSC, RN) and the Polish destroyer ORP Blyscawica (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) L. Lichodziejewski). They were joined at sea, around dawn, by HMS Sardonyx coming from Londonderry. The destroyers parted company around midnight during the night of 5/6 August. They arrived at Londonderry on 7 August. HMS Furious and HMS Manchester then joined convoy WS 21S around midnight of the next night but HMS Manchester parted company shortly afterwards to proceed ahead of the convoy and fuel at Gibraltar.

On 1 August 1942 the aircraft carrier HMS Indomitable (Capt. T.H. Troubridge, RN), light cruiser HMS Phoebe (Capt. C.P. Frend, RN) and the destroyers HMS Laforey (Capt. R.M.J. Hutton, RN), HMS Lightning (Cdr. H.G. Walters, DSC, RN) and HMS Lookout (Lt.Cdr. A.G. Forman, DSC, RN) departed Freetown to proceed to a rendez-vous position off the Azores.

On 5 August 1942, the aircraft carrier HMS Eagle (Capt. L.D. Mackintosh, DSC, RN), light cruiser HMS Charybdis (Capt. G.A.W. Voelcker, RN) and the the destroyers HMS Wrestler (Lt. R.W.B. Lacon, DSC, RN), HMS Westcott (Cdr. I.H. Bockett-Pugh, DSO, RN) and HMS Vansittart (Lt.Cdr. T. Johnston, RN) departed Gibraltar also to the rendezvous position off the Azores.

The convoy conducted maneuvering and AA exercises with the escorts between the Azores and Gibraltar during the period of 6 to 9 August. (Operation Berserk). Also dummy air attacks were carried out by aircraft from the carriers.
Passage of the Straits of Gibraltar and organization of escort forces.
The convoy then passed the Straits of Gibraltar during the night of 9/10 August 1942 in dense fog but despite this the convoy was detected by German and Italian spies and reported.

After passing the Straits of Gibraltar the convoy was organized as follows; The actual convoy was protected a large force of warships until the whole force would split up before entering the Sicilian narrows after which ‘Force X’ under command of Rear-Admiral Sir H.M. Burrough, CB, DSO, RN was to accompany the convoy to the approaches to Malta where they would be met by the Malta Minesweeping Flotilla, which was then to sweep the convoy into the harbour. Force X was made up of the following ships: Licht cruisers: HMS Nigeria (flagship), HMS Kenya,, HMS Manchester. AA cruiser: HMS Cairo (A/Capt. C.C. Hardy, DSO, RN). Destroyers: HMS Ashanti, HMS Fury, HMS Foresight, HMS Icarus, HMS Intrepid, HMS Pathfinder and HMS Penn. Escort destroyers: HMS Derwent, HMS Bicester (Lt.Cdr. S.W.F. Bennetts, RN), HMS Bramham (Lt. E.F. Baines, RN), HMS Ledbury (Lt.Cdr. R.P. Hill, RN) and HMS Wilton (Lt. A.P. Northey, RN). Also the rescue tug HMRT Jaunty was to be part of this force.

After the escort was to be split up cover was provided by ‘Force Z’ under Vice-Admiral E.N. Syfret, CB, RN. This force was made up of the following ships: Battleships: HMS Nelson (flagship) and HMS Rodney. Aircraft carriers: HMS Victorious, HMS Indomitable and HMS Eagle. Light cruisers: HMS Phoebe, HMS Sirius and HMS Charybdis. Destroyers: HMS Laforey, HMS Lightning, HMS Lookout, HMS Eskimo, HMS Somali, HMS Tartar, HMS Quentin, HMS Ithuriel (Lt.Cdr. D.H. Maitland-Makgill-Crichton, DSC, RN), HMS Antelope (Lt.Cdr. E.N. Sinclair), HMS Wishart and HMS Vansittart. Escort destroyer: HMS Zetland. Also attached were the aircraft carrier HMS Furious (for Operation Bellows, the launching of Hurricane fighters for Malta.
