HMS Furious only carried four Albacore aircraft for A/S searches after the Hurricanes had been launched) and the ‘spare’ destroyers HMS Keppel (Cdr. J.E. Broome, RN), HMS Malcolm, HMS Venomous, HMS Vidette (Lt.Cdr. E.N. Walmsley, DSC, RN), HMS Westcott, HMS Wolverine, HMS Wrestler and HMS Amazon. These ‘spare’ destroyers were to take the place of destroyers in the screen ‘Force Z’ if needed, escort HMS Furious during her return passage to Gibraltar after she had completed Operation Bellows and / or strengthen the escort of ‘Force R’.

Then there was also ‘Force R’, the fuelling force. This force was made up of the following ships: Corvettes: HMS Jonquil (Lt.Cdr. R.E.H. Partington, RD, RNR), HMS Spiraea (Lt.Cdr. R.S. Miller, DSC, RNR), HMS Geranium (T/Lt. A. Foxall, RNR) and HMS Coltsfoot (T/Lt. the Hon. W.K. Rous, RNVR). Rescue tug: HMS Salvonia. RFA tankers: RFA Brown Ranger (3417 GRT, built 1941, Master D.B.C. Ralph) and RFA Dingledale (8145 GRT, built 1941, Master R.T. Duthie).

Before we give an account of the passage of the main convoy we will now first describe the operations taking place in the Eastern Mediterranean (Operations MG 3 and MG 4), the launching of the Hurricane fighters for Malta by HMS Furious (Operation Bellows) and the return convoy from Malta (Operation Ascendant) as well as on submarine operations / dispositions.
Diversion in the Eastern Mediterranean.

As part of the plan for Operation Pedestal the Mediterranean Fleet had to carry out a diversion in the Eastern part of the Mediterranean. Before we go to the operations in the Western Mediterranean we will first give an account of the events in the Eastern Mediterranean.

It was at this time not possible to sent any supplies from Egypt to Malta as all supplies and forces were much needed for the upcoming land battle at El Alamein it was agreed that ‘a dummy convoy’ would be sent towards Malta with the object of preventing the enemy to direct the full weight of their air and naval power towards the Western Mediterranean.

In the evening of 10 August 1942 a ‘convoy’ (MG 3) of three merchant ships departed Port Said escorted by three cruisers and ten destroyers. Next morning one more merchant ship departed Haifa escorted by two cruisers and five destroyers. The two forces joined that day (the 11th) and then turned back dispersing during the night. The Italian fleet however did not go to sea to attack ‘the bait’.

The forces taking part in this operation were: From Port Said: Merchant vessels City of Edinburgh (8036 GRT, built 1938), City of Lincoln (8039 GRT, built 1938) and City of Pretoria (8049 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the light cruisers HMS Arethusa (Capt. A.C. Chapman, RN, flying the flag of Rear-Admiral I.G. Glennie, RN), HMS Euryalus (Capt. E.W. Bush, DSO, DSC, RN), the AA cruiser HMS Coventry (Capt. R.J.R. Dendy, RN) and the destroyers HMS Jervis (Capt. A.L. Poland, DSO and Bar, DSC, RN), HMS Kelvin (Cdr. M.S. Townsend, OBE, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Pakenham (Capt. E.B.K. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Paladin (Cdr. A.F. Pugsley, RN) and the escort destroyers HMS Dulverton (Lt.Cdr. W.N. Petch, OBE, RN), HMS Hurworth (Lt.Cdr. J.T.B. Birch, RN), HMS Eridge (Lt.Cdr. W.F.N. Gregory-Smith, DSC, RN), HMS Hursley (Lt. W.J.P. Church, DSC, RN), HMS Beaufort (Lt.Cdr. S.O’G Roche, RN) and HMS Belvoir (Lt. J.F.D. Bush, DSC and Bar, RN).

From Haifa: Merchant vessel Ajax (7797 GRT, built 1931) escorted by the light cruisers HMS Cleopatra (Capt. G. Grantham, DSO, RN, flagship of Rear-Admiral P.L. Vian, KBE, DSO and 2 Bars, RN), HMS Dido (Capt. H.W.U. McCall, RN), the destroyers HMS Sikh (Capt. St.J. A. Micklethwait, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Zulu (Cdr. R.T. White, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Javelin (Cdr. G.E. Fardell, RN) and the escort destroyers HMS Tetcott (Lt. H.R. Rycroft, RN) and HMS Croome (Lt.Cdr. R.C. Egan, RN).

After dark on 11 August 1942 the force turned back and the City of Pretoria returned to Port Said escorted by HMS Eridge and HMS Hursley. The City of Edinburgh, escorted by HMS Beaufort and HMS Belvoir proceeded to Haifa. The City of Lincoln escorted by HMS Dulverton and HMS Hurworth proceeded to Beirut and finally the Ajax, escorted by HMS Tetcott and HMS Croome returned to Haifa. HMS Dido had to return to Port Said with hull defects. She was escorted by HMS Pakenham, HMS Paladin and HMS Jervis.

HMS Cleopatra, HMS Arethusa, HMS Sikh, HMS Zulu, HMS Javelin and HMS Kelvin then proceeded to carry out another diversion (Operation MG 4). They bombarded Rhodes harbour and the Alliotti Flour Mills during the night of 12/13 August but did little damage. On the way back HMS Javelin attacked a submarine contact in position 34°45’N, 31°04’E between 0654 and 0804 hours. She reported that there was no doubt that the submarine was sunk but no Axis submarines were operating in this area so the attack must have been bogus. This force returned to Haifa at 1900 B/13.
Operation Bellows.

During operation Bellows, the aircraft carrier HMS Furious, started 37 Spitfire which were to proceed to Malta, when south of the Balearic Islands. The Admiralty had decided to carry out this operation at the same time as Operation Pedestal.

HMS Furious remained with the convoy until 1200 B/11. She then launched the Spitfires for Malta in 5 batches between 1230 and 1515 hours. During these flying off operations she acted independently with the destroyers HMS Lookout and HMS Lightning. After having launched the last batch of Spitfires she briefly re-joined to convoy until around 1700 hours when she split off and set course for Gibraltar escorted by the destroyers HMS Malcolm, HMS Wolverine and HMS Wrestler. These were joined shortly afterwards by HMS Keppel and HMS Venomous.

Around 0100 B/12, HMS Wolverine, rammed and sank the Italian submarine Dagabur which was trying to attack HMS Furious. Around 0200 hours, HMS Wolverine reported that she was stopped due to the damage she had sustained in the ramming. HMS Malcolm was detached to assist her.

At 1530 B/12, the destroyer HMS Vidette joined the screen. The force then entered Gibraltar Bay around 1930 B/12. The damaged HMS Wolverine arrived at Gibraltar at 1230 B/13 followed by HMS Malcolm around 1530 B/13.
Operation Ascendant

On 10 August 1942 the empty transports Troilus (7648 GRT, built 1921) and Orari (10107 GRT, built 1931) departed Malta after dark for Gibraltar. They were escorted by the destroyer HMS Matchless (Lt.Cdr. J. Mowlam, RN) and the escort destroyer HMS Badsworth (Lt. G.T.S. Gray, DSC, RN). They first proceeded to the south of Lampedusa, then hugged the Tunisian coast as far as Galita Island. Near Cape Bon they encountered the Italian destroyer Lanzerotto Malocello that was laying a minefield. They had a brief gunfight but this was soon ended as both sides were thinking the enemy was Vichy-French. The remained of the passage to Gibraltar was uneventful and the convoy arrived at Gibraltar shortly before noon on 14 August 1942.

Submarine operations / dispositions. Eight submarines took part in the operation; these were HMS Utmost (Lt. A.W. Langridge, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, RN), HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN), HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN), HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN), HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN). Two of these were to carry out normal dived patrol to the north of Sicily, one off Palermo, the other off Milazzo which is futher to the east. The other six submarines were given alternative patrol lines south of Pantelleria, one od which they were to take up at dawn on 13 August 1942, according to the movements of enemy surface ships that might threathen the convoy from the westward. When the convoy had passed the patrol line, which it should have done by that time, the submarines were to proceed on the surface parallel to the convoy as a screen and to dive away clear of the convoy at noon. It was expressly intended that they should be seen on the surface and reported by enemy aircraft in order to deter enemy warships from attacking the convoy.

Enemy warships did go to sea but as soon as it was clear that the enemy ships could not reach the convoy the sunmarines were ordered to dive and retire. These six sumarines had no contact with the enemy. One of the the two submarines off the north coast of Sicily, HMS P 42, managed to torpedo two Italian cruisers near Stromboli on the morning of 13 August 1942.
Now we return to the main convoy to Malta.
Passage eastwards after passing the Straits of Gibraltar.
10 and 11 August 1942.

After passing through the Straits of Gibraltar in the early hours of 10 August 1942, in dense fog, the convoy was first sighted by an Italian passenger aircraft, which sighted the convoy in the afternoon of the same day. German reconnaissance aircraft started shadowing the convoy from dawn on the 11th, and thereafter they or Italian aircraft kept the convoy under continuous observation, despite the effort of the fighters from the carriers to shoot them down or drive them off. At 1315 hours, HMS Eagle, was hit an sunk by torpedoes from the German submarine U-73 which had penetrated the destroyer screen. At that moment there were thirteen destroyers in the screen, the remainder was away from the main convoy, escorting HMS Furious during the flying off operations of the Hurricane fighters for Malta or oiling from and screening ‘Force R’ which was several miles away. Between 1430 B/10 and and 2030 B/11 no less then three cruisers and twenty-four destroyers fuelled from the two oilers of ‘Force R’.

At the time of the torpedoing of HMS Eagle the convoy was in four columns, zigzagging at 13 knots, with the heavy ships stationed close round it and a destroyer screen ahead. HMS Eagle was on the starboard quarter of the convoy. She was hit on her starboard side by four torpedoes which had dived through the destroyer screen and the convoy columns undetected and then torpedoed and sank the Eagle in position 38°05’N, 03°02’E (Another source gives 03°12’E but this might be a typo). The carrier sank quickly in about 8 minutes, 926 of her crew, including the Commanding Officer, were rescued by the destroyers HMS Laforey and HMS Lookout and the rescue tug HMS Jaunty. At the time of her sinking, HMS Eagle had four aircraft on patrol. These landed on the other carriers. All other aircraft were lost with the ship. The survivors picked up were later transferred to the destroyers HMS Keppel, HMS Malcolm and HMS Venomous that were to escort HMS Furious back to Gibraltar. The tug HMS Jaunty that had been involved in picking up survivors was never able to rejoin the convoy due to her slow speed.

Late in the afternoon air attacks were expected so Vice-Admiral Syfret ordered the destroyer to form an all-round screen. Indeed the air attacks started around sunset, 2045 hours. The last destroyers had just returned from oiling from ‘Force R’. The enemy aircraft that were attacking were 36 German bombers and torpedo aircraft, Ju 88’s and He 111’s, most of which attacked the convoy but a few attacked ‘Force R’ to the southward. The Junkers arrived first, diving down from 8000 feet to 2000 / 3000 feet to drop their bombs. They claimed to have hit an aircraft carrier and one of the merchant ships. Then the Heinkels attacked, they claimed to have torpedoed a cruiser but during the attacks no ship was hit. The British fighter cover was unable to attack / find the enemy in the failing light. Four enemy aircraft were claimed shot down by the ships AA fire but it appears only two JU 88’s were in fact shot down.
12 August 1942

At 0915 B/12 another wave of German aircraft attacked the convoy. Some twenty or more JU 88’s approached the convoy out of the sun ahead. They were intercepted by fighters about 25 miles from the convoy. About a dozen got through to the convoy, making high-level or shallow dive-bombing attacks individually but without any result. Eight German aircraft were claimed to be shot down by the fighters and two more by AA guns from the ships. The fighters meanwhile were also busy dealng with shadowers, three of which are claimed to have been shot down before the morning attack. Around this time destroyers were also busy with numerous submarine contact which were attacked by depth charges.
Around noon the enemy launched heavy air attacks from the Sardinian airfields. Seventy aircraft approached which were heavily escorted by fighters. They attacked in stages and employed new methods.

First ten Italian torpedo-bombers were each to drop some sort of circling torpedo or mine a few hundred yards ahead of the British force, while eight fighter bombers made dive-bombing and machine-gun attacks. The object at this stage was clearly to dislocate the formation of the force and to draw anti-aircraft fire, making the ships more vulnerable to a torpedo attack which soon followed with over forty aircraft. They attacked in two groups, one on either bow of the convoy. The next stage was a shallow dive-bombing attack by German aircraft, after which two Italian Reggiane 2001 fighters, each with a single heavy armour-piercing bomb were to dive bomb on one of the aircraft carriers, whilst yet another new form of attack was to be employed against the other carrier, but defects in the weapon prevented this attack from taking place.

The enemy attack went according to plan besides that the torpedo attack was only made half an our after the ‘mines’ were dropped instead of five minutes. British fighters met the minelaying aircraft, they shot down one of them as they approached. The remaining nine aircraft dropped their ‘mines’ at 1215 hours in the path of the force, which turned to avoid the danger. The mines were heard to explode several minutes later. Only three of the fighter-bombers of this stage of the attack appear to have reached as far the screen, but HMS Lightning had a narrow escape from their bombs.

The torpedo-aircraft appeared at 1245 hours. Their number were brought down a bit due to British fighters. The remaining aircraft, estimated at 25 to 30 machines, attacked from the port bow, port beam and starboard quarter. They dropped their torpedoes well outside the screen some 8000 yards from the merchant ships which they had been ordered to attack. The force turned 45° to port and then back to starboard to avoid the attack.

In the next stage, around 1318 hours, the German bombing attack, the enemy scored their one success. These aircraft were also intercepted on their way in but about a dozen of about twenty aircraft came through. They crossed the convoy from starboard to port and then dived to 3000 feet. They managed to damage the transport Deucalion which was leading the port wing column. More bombs fell close to several other ships.

Finally, at 1345 hours, the two Reggiane fighters approached HMS Victorious as if to land on. They looked like Hurricanes and HMS Victorious was at that time engaged in landing her own fighters. They managed to drop their bombs and one hit the flight deck amidships. Fortunately the bomb broke up without exploding. By the time HMS Victorious could open fire both fighters were out of range.

The Deucalion could no longer keep up with the convoy and was ordered to follow the inshore route along the Tunisian coast escorted by HMS Bramham. Two bombers found these ships late in the afternoon, but their bombs missed. At 1940 hours, however, near the Cani Rocks, two torpedo aircraft attacked and a torpedo hit the Deucalion. She caught fire and eventually blew up.

The convoy passed some 20 miles north of Galita Island and spent the afternoon avoiding enemy submarines which were known to be concentrated in these waters. There were innumerable reports of sightings and Asdic contacts and at least two submarines proved dangerous. At 1616 hours, HMS Pathfinder and HMS Zetland attacked one on the port bow of the convoy and hunted her until the convoy was out of reach. HMS Ithuriel, stationed on the quarter, then attacked, forced the enemy to surface and finally rammed her. She proved to be the Italian submarine Cobalto. Meanwhile HMS Tartar, on the starboard quarter, saw six torpedoes fired at close range at 1640 hours, and the next destroyer in the screen, HMS Lookout sighted a periscope. Together they attacked the submarine, continuing until it was no longer dangerous. There was no evidence this submarine was sunk.

At 1750 hours, HMS Ithuriel, which was on her way back to the convoy after sinking the Italian submarine Cobalto was attacked by a few dive-bombers, when still a dozen miles astern of the convoy. At this time the convoy came under attack by aircraft stationed on Sicily. This force numbered nearly 100 aircraft. Ju.87 dive-bombers as well as Ju.88’s and SM-79’s all with a strong escort of fighters. The enemy started attacking at 1835 hours, the bombers attacking from both ahead and astern which last was the direction of the sun. The torpedo aircraft came from ahead to attack on the starboard bow and beam of the convoy.

The Italian SM-79’s torpedo bombers dropped their torpedoes from ranges of about 3000 yards outside the destroyer screen, and once again the convoy turned away to avoid them. However the destroyer HMS Foresight was hit by a torpedo and disabled. The bombers chose HMS Indomitable as their main target. She was astern of HMS Rodney at the time on the port quarter of the convoy. Four Ju.88’s and eight Ju.87’s came suddenly out of the sun and dived steeply towards HMS Indomitable from astern. Some of the Ju.87 came down to 1000 feet and the carrier received three hits and her flight deck was put out of action. Her airborne fighters eventually had to land on HMS Victorious. HMS Rodney meanwhile had a narrow escape when a bomber attacked from ahead. One enemy aircraft was claimed to have been shot down by AA fire from the ships while the fighters claimed nine more although there were about twice as much enemy fighters in the air then British.

HMS Tartar took the damaged HMS Foresight in tow and proceeded westward for Gibraltar. Next day, as they were shadowed by enemy aircraft, and enemy submarines were known to be in the area, it was decided to scuttle the cripple before both ships might be lost. HMS Tartar then torpedoed HMS Foresight a few miles from Galita Island.
Passage through the narrows, 12-13 August 1942, and the loss off HMS Manchester.

These last air attacks took place about 20 nautical miles west of the Skerki Channel and at 1900 hours, when the attacks were clearly over, Vice-Admiral Syfret turned away with ‘Force Z’. It was now up to Rear-Admiral Burrough with ‘Force X’ to take the convoy to Malta.

At 2000 hours, when the convoy was changing it’s formation from four to two columns, the convoy was attacked by Italian submarines. The submarine Dessiè attacked a freighter with four torpedoes and claimed three hits. The sound of the torpedo hits was however not caused by her attack but by an attack by the Axum which hit three ships, HMS Nigeria, HMS Cairo and the tanker Ohio.

HMS Nigeria had to turn back to make for Gibraltar escorted by the escort destroyers HMS Derwent, HMS Wilton and HMS Bicester. Rear-Admiral Burrough transferred his flag to the destroyer HMS Ashanti. The stern of HMS Cairo had been blown off and she had to be sunk as she was beyond salvage with both engines also out of action. She was scuttled by HMS Pathfinder. The Ohio meanwhile managed to struggle on.

At this time the convoy was still trying to form up the the submarine attacks messed things up and right at thus time the convoy was once more attacked from the air in the growing dusk at 2030 hours. About 20 German aircraft, Ju-88’s made dive bombing and torpedo attacks, hitting the Empire Hope with a bomb and the Clan Ferguson and Brisbane Star with torpedoes. The first of these ships had to be sunk (by HMS Bramham, the second blew up but the last eventually reached Malta. Soon after this attack, at 2111 hours, HMS Kenya was torpedoed by the Italian submarine Alagi. She was able to evade three of the four torpedoes but was hit in the bow by the fouth. She was however able to remain with the convoy.

The situation was then as follows. HMS Kenya and HMS Manchester with two merchant ships, and with the minesweeping destroyers HMS Intrepid, HMS Icarus and HMS Fury sweeping ahead, had passed the Skerki Channel and were steering to pass Zembra Island on the way to Cape Bon. HMS Ashanti, with Rear-Admiral Burrough on board was fast overhauling these ships. The other two destroyers HMS Pathfinder, HMS Penn and the escort destroyer HMS Ledbury, were rounding up the remaining nine merchant ships. The escort destroyer HMS Bramham was also catching up after having escorted the single Deucalion until she sank.

On learing about the fate of HMS Nigeria and HMS Cairo, Vice-Admiral Syfret detached HMS Charybdis, HMS Eskimo and HMS Somali to reinforce Rear-Admiral Burrough. It would take these ships several hourse to catch up with the convoy.

The main body of the convoy passed Cape Bon around midnight. Fourty minutes later enemy Motor Torpedo Boats appeared and started to attack. Their first victim was HMS Manchester which was torpedoed at 0120 B/13 by the Italian MS 16 or MS 22. She had to be scuttled by her own crew. Many of her ships company landed in Tunisia and were interned by the Vichy-French but about 300 were picked up by destroyers (first by HMS Pathfinder, and later by HMS Eskimo and HMS Somali. These last two destoyers then set off towards Gibraltar.)

Four and possibly five of the merchant ships were also hit by the Motor Torpedo Boats. These were the Wairangi, Rochester Castle, Almeria Lykes, Santa Elisa and probably the Glenorchy. They were attacked between 0315 and 0430 hours about 15 nautical miles south-east of Kelibia whilst taking a short cut to overhaul the main body of the convoy. Four were lost, only the Rochester Castle survived and she managed to catch up with the main body of the convoy at 0530 hours. The Glenorchy was sunk by the Italian MS 31, the other four, of which the Rochester Castle survived as mentioned earlier, were hit by the German S 30 and S 36 as well as the Italian MAS 554 and MAS 557.

Shortly before 0530 hours HMS Charybdis, HMS Eskimo and HMS Somali had joined the main body of the convoy making the force now two cruisers and seven destroyers with the transports Rochester Castle, Waimarama and Melbourne Star. The damaged tanker Ohio was slowly catching up. With her was the escort destroyer HMS Ledbury. Astern of the main body was the Port Chalmers escorted by the destroyer HMS Penn and the escort destroyer HMS Bramham. The destroyers recued the crew of the Santa Elisa when the passed by the abandoned ship which was afterwards finished off by a German bomber. The Dorset was proceeding without escort and lastly the damaged Brisbane Star was still keeping close to the Tunisian coast independently, intending to steer towards Malta after nightfall.

At 0730 hours, Rear-Admiral Burrough, sent back HMS Tartar and HMS Somali to Kelibia to assist HMS Manchester and then go to Gibraltar. When they arrived they found out that the Manchester had been scuttled several hours earlier so they rescued those of her crew that had not reached the shore yet and then made off to Gibraltar as ordered. Besides crew of the Manchester they also picked up survivors from the Almeria Lykes and Wairangi.

The next encounter with the enemy was an air attack on the main body of the convoy at 0800 hours by German bombers. About 12 Ju.88’s made a shallow diving attack coming down from 6000 feet to 2000 feet to drop their bombs. Two dived on the Waimarama hitting her several times and she blew up immediately, one of the bombers was even destroyed in the explosion. HMS Ledbury saved some of her crew out of the blazing sea. At 0925 hours, when the Ohio, Port Chalmers and Dorset where with the main body again, a few Ju.87’s escorted by Italian fighters attacked. They dived down to 1500 to 1000 feet. HMS Kenya leading the port column, and the Ohio last ship but one in the starboard column, had narrow escapes. One of the enemy aircraft crashed on board the Ohio just after having released it’s bomb after being damaged by gunfire from the Ohio and HMS Ashanti. Another aircraft was claimed to have been shot down by fighters from Malta that had been patrolling overhead since daybreak.
Arrivals at Malta 13-15 August 1942.

At 1050 hours, about 20 bombers, mostly Ju.88’s with a few Ju.87’s, came in to attack. Target was the Ohio and she received four or five near misses and her engines were disabled. At the same time the Rochester Castle in the port column was near-missed and set on fire but she continued with the convoy. The Dorset which was astern of her was hit and stopped. The convoy went on leaving the Dorset behind with the Ohio and two destroyers.

At 1125 hours the last air attack on the main body took place. Five Italian SM.79’s attacked with torpedoes and almost hit the Port Chalmers as the torpedo got stuck in the paravane. Further attacks on the main body were held of by fighters from Malta. At 1430 hours, four minesweepers from Malta joined the main body of the convoy, these were HMS Speedy (Lt.Cdr. A.E. Doran, RN, with the group’s commander A/Cdr. H.J.A.S. Jerome, RN on board), HMS Hebe, HMS Rye and HMS Heyte. Also with them were seven Motor Launches; ML 121, ML 126, ML 134, ML 135, ML 168, ML 459 and ML 462. HMS Rye and two of the ML’s were sent towards the damaged Ohio which was ‘vital for Malta’, according to A/Cdr. Jerome.

At 1600 hours, Rear-Admiral Burrough, set course to the west with his two cruisers and with five destroyers. The Port Chalmers, Melbourne Star and Rochester Castle arrived in Grand Harbour around 1800 hours with the force of A/Cdr. Jerome. The Rochester Castle was by that time very low in the water, she had just made it into port on time.

Out were still the Ohio, Dorset and the Brisbane Star. The valuable Ohio had been helpless with HMS Penn and HMS Bramham. When HMS Rye arrived at 1730 hours, HMS Penn took the Ohio in tow. Meanwhile HMS Bramham was sent to the Dorset but soon afterwards German bombers came again and the ships were attacked repeatedly until dark. Both merchantman were hit around 1900 hours and the Dorset sank.

At daylight on the 14th HMS Ledbury arrived to help bringing the Ohio to Malta. HMS Speedy also soon arrived on the scene with two ML’s. The rest of his force he had sent to search for the Brisbane Star. At 1045 hours, enemy aircraft made their last attempt, causing the parting of the tow. Fighter from Malta shot down two of the attackers. The tow was passed again and the slow procession went on and in the morning of the 15th the vital tanker finally reached Malta.

The Brisbane Star had by then also arrived. She left the Tunisian coast at dusk on the 13th. Aircraft had attacked her unsuccessfully and one of the attackers was shot down by a Beaufighter escort that had been sent from Malta. She arrived at Malta in the afternoon of the 14th.
Italian surface ships to operate against the convoy ?

The convoy had experienced the violence of the enemy in every shape except that of an attack by large surface ships. Yet Italian cruisers and destroyers had been at sea to intercept and attack it. Two light cruiser had left Cagliari in the evening of 11 August 1942 and the heavy cruisers Gorizia and Bolzano from Messina, and a light cruiser from Naples had sailed on the morning of the 12th. That evening reconnaissance aircraft reported one heavy and two light cruisers with eight destroyers about 80 nautical miles to the north of the western tip of Sicily and steering south. It would have been possible for this force to meet the convoy at dawn on the 13th so the shadowing aircraft was therefore ordered in plain language to illuminate and attack. This apparently influenced the Italians as they had limited air cover and they turned back at 0130 B/13 when near Cape San Vito. At 0140 hours the aircraft reported that it had dropped its bombs but no hits had been obtained. Similar orders were signalled, in plain language, to relief shadowers and to report the position of the enemy force to the benefit of imaginary Liberator bombers in case the Italians would change their minds and turn back. They however held on to the eastward.

The submarine HMS P 42 sighted them around 0800 B/13 off Stromboli and attacked with four torpedoes claiming two hits. She had in fact hit the heavy cruiser Bolzano which was able to proceed northwards and the light cruiser Muzio Attendolo which managed to reach Messina with her bows blown off. The other cruisers went to Naples. Following the attack P 42 was heavily depth charged by the destroyers but managed to escape.

In fact the following Italian ships had been at sea; heavy cruisers Gorizia, Trieste, Bolzano, light cruisers Eugenio di Savoia Raimondo Montecuccoli, Muzio Attendolo. They were escorted by eleven destroyers; Ascari, Aviere, Camicia Nera, Corsaro, Fuceliere, Geniere, Legionaro, Vincenzo Gioberti, Alfredo Oriani, Grecale and Maestrale.
The return to Gibraltar.

The British ships returning to Gibraltar had better fortune. Having left the convoy off Malta in the afternoon of the 13th, they rounded Cape Bon around 0130 B/14 and from that point until past Zembra Island they successful ran the gauntled of E-boats laying in wait.

at 0450 B/14, near the Fratelli Rocks, a submarine fired torpedoes at HMS Ashanti from the surface. She was nearly rammed by HMS Kenya, which was next astern of the ‘flagship’ (Rear-Admiral Burrough was still in HMS Ashanti). The inevitable shadowers arrived soon after daylight to herald their air attacks that began at 0730 hours. They lasted until around 1315 hours. German bombers came in first with three attemps by a few Ju.88’s. This was followed by a more severe attack with about 30 bombers, Ju-88’s and Ju-87’s between 1030 and 1050 hours. An hour later 15 Savoia high-level bombers attacked followed until 1315 hours by torpedo-carrying Savoia’s. Around 20 aircraft attacking single or in pairs. Also aircraft are though to be laying mines ahead. Several ships were near missed, but no further damage was sustained. After these attacks the British were left alone and in the evening they joined ‘Force Z’.

Vice-Admiral Syfret had gone as far west as 01’E where he ordered the damaged carrier HMS Indomitable to proceed to Malta with HMS Rodney and a destroyer screen made up of HMS Ithuriel, HMS Antelope, HMS Amazon, HMS Westcott, HMS Wishart and HMS Zetland. He then turned back to the east to make rendez-vous with Rear-Admiral Burrough. HMS Rodney, HMS Indomitable, HMS Ithuriel, HMS Antelope, HMS Amazon, HMS Westcott, HMS Wishart and HMS Zetland arrived at Gibraltar in the evening of the 14th.

A few hours before they arrived the damaged HMS Nigeria and her escort had also entered port, as had HMS Tartar, HMS Eskimo and HMS Somali. On her way back HMS Nigeria had been attacked by torpedo-bombers and a submarine but she had not been hit.
Conclusion.

Out of the fourteen ships that had sailed only five arrived ‘safe’ at Malta. This was not a very high score also given the very heavy escort that had been provided also taken in mind that an aircraft carrier, a light cruiser, an AA cruiser an a destroyer had been lost and two heavy cruiser had been damaged. But the convoy had to meet very heavy air attacks by over 150 bombers and 80 torpedo aircraft, all in the space of two days. Also these aircraft were protected by fighter in much greater strength that the carriers and Malta could provide. And there were also the enemy submarines and E-boats.

The spirit in which to operation was carried out appears in Vice-Admiral Syfret’s report: ‘ Tribute has been paid to the personnel of His Majesty’s Ships, both the officers and men will desire to give first place to the conduct, courage, and determination of the masters, officers, and men of the merchant ships. The steadfast manner in which these ships pressed on their way to Malta through all attacks, answering every maneuvering order like a well trained fleet unit, was a most inspiring sight. Many of these fine men and their ships were lost. But the memory of their conduct will remain an inspiration to all who were privileged to sail with them.
Movements of the Italian Fleet during operation Pedestal.

The light cruisers Eugenio di Savoia and Raimondo Montecuccoli (7th Cruiser Division, Divisional Admiral Alberto Da Zara) escorted by the destroyers Ascari, Alfredo Oriani (leader) and Vincenzo Gioberti (10th Destroyer Squadron), sailed from Cagliari at 1605 B/13-1625 B/13. They were sighted by HMS P 211 at 2020 B/13 in position 38°42'N, 10°29'E. They were attacked by HMS P 43 at 1840 B/13 and missed in position 38°46.5'N, 09°49'E. They arrived at Palermo at 0726 B/14-0826 B/14.

At 1910 B/14-2006 B/14, the squadron sailed from Palermo escorted by Alfredo Oriani, Ascari, Ugolino Vivaldi, Vincenzo Gioberti and Lanzerotto Malocello. The destroyer Premuda sailed from Trapani at 1745 B/14 to join them. After midnight, Vincenzo Gioberti had turn back due to defects and returned to Palermo at 0500 B/15.

At 0532 B/15, in position 190° - Punta Sciaccazza (Pantelleria) - 18 miles, The Italian squadron sighted two enemy cruisers and several destroyers of the Tribal and J/K/N-classes, bearing 220°, course 135°, 15 knots, at a range of 22000 metres. At a distance from them was a convoy consisting of a 12000-ton troop transport, a tanker and three merchant ships of about 6,000 tons, escorted by four Tribal-class destroyers. Immediately, Eugenio di Savoia catapulted a RO 43 seaplane and at 0536 B/14 (it later crashed at sea, the pilot was recovered by a MAS boat but the navigating officer was missing), Raimondo Montecuccoli also launched a RO 43 seaplane but it was forced by engine defects to abort the mission and landed in Sicily. The two cruisers increased speed to 28 knot and a couple of minutes later to 32 knots and opened fire with their 152mm/53 guns, with the third salvo straddling the enemy. The British gunfire soon straddled the Italian cruiser. At 0543 B/15, five enemy destroyers closed to attack, the leading destroyer was of the Tribal-class, followed by a J/K/N-class, the other three could not be identified. At 0546 B/15, Eugenio di Savoia opened fire on the leading destroyer with her main armament and as the range has now decreased, the 100mm/47 also joined. The Italian destroyers also opened fire at 0551 B/15 at a range of 18000 metres (their extreme range). Three minutes later, they were ordered to attack the convoy which was observed to turn back while enemy destroyers laid a smoke screen. At 0555 B/15, Eugenio di Savoia was hit by an enemy round on the 70mm armoured belt on the starboard side, two ratings were killed and eight were wounded. In the meantime, at 0559 B/15, the Italian destroyers had closed the enemy convoy at 5800 metres and the enemy destroyers at 5000 metres. Ugolini Vivaldi and Lanzerotto Malocello each launched two torpedoes. At 0605 B/15, a merchant vessel appeared to be hit by a torpedo. Seven minutes later, an enemy destroyer was hit and the explosion was followed by a dense smoke, she withdrew from the action. At 0600 B/15, Eugenio di Savoia fired one torpedo. At 0609 B/15, the Italian cruisers resumed their fire on the enemy cruisers at a range of 17000 metres but observation was difficult due to the smoke from the enemy ships. At 0620 B/15, the destroyer Ugolino Vivaldi was hit by a round in the forward engine room which had to be evacuated, ten men were killed and eight wounded. Admiral da Zara ordered the destroyers to give her assistance. Alfredo Oriani and Ascari were unable to do so due to the enemy fire. Only Lanzerotto Malocello stood by the stricken destroyer, and laid smoke to cover her. At 0645 B/15, the enemy destroyers were now at 4500 metres and Ugolino Vivaldi fired two torpedoes at the destroyer with the letters H 68 clearly seen on her bows [This was HMS Foresight]. Shortly after, she managed to increase speed at 13 knots. The enemy cruiser was firing at a range of 19000 metres at the Italian destroyers which could not reply due to the distance. At 0659 B/15, the leading enemy cruiser appeared to have been seriously hit, and the escorting destroyers covered her with smoke. By 0717 B/15, the enemy formation had completely disappeared from view, but a big detonation was observed. At 0735 B/15, Ugolino Vivaldi sighted Premuda, Ascari and Alfredo Oriani and the former joined to escort her. At 0757 B/15, a violent fire developed in Ugolino Vivaldi's forward engine room the forward magazine had to be flooded, and ammunition thrown overboard as a precaution. At 0919 B/15, Lanzerotto Malocello took her damaged sistership in tow and they proceed to Pantelleria. The enemy appeared briefly at 0801 B/15, and the cruisers resumed fire for a minute before contact was definitely lost. At 0935 B/15, an enemy bomber of the Beaufort type attacked Eugenio di Savoia, one torpedo missed astern and the other sank in mid-course. In the meantime, the cruisers were still in action with the enemy and, at 1156 B/15, a large explosion was observed with a white column of smoke of over 1000 metres in height believed to be from an enemy ammunition ship. At 1223 B/15, next to the enemy merchant ship on fire, two destroyers were observed and twelve minutes later, fire was opened on one of them at the range of 19500 metres. It was certainly hit and masked by smoke from the other destroyer. Another two destroyers were then sighted next to another merchant ship on fire and fire was opened on one of them at 1259 B/15 at a range of 18000 metres. The destroyer blew up at 1325 B/15 and sank rapidly. In the meantime, Alfredo Oriani and Ascari opened fire at a tanker at a range of 12000 metres which was at repeatedly hit at 1302 B/15-1304 B/15 and set afire. Italian bombers. At 1343 B/15, Alfredo Oriani launched a torpedo at the tanker, but it was only a muffled hit. At 1423 B/15-1437 B/15, six enemy bombers attacked Alfredo Oriani and Ascari; they were not hit. In the action, Eugenio di Savoia fired 838 rounds of 152mm rounds, 690 rounds of 100mm, 978 rounds of 37mm and 973 rounds of 13.2mm, Raimondo Montecuccoli fired 704 rounds of 152mm rounds, 368 rounds of 100mm rounds and 567 rounds of 37mm. The cruisers came under air attack at 1428 B/15 by enemy bombers and at 1600 B/15 by three torpedo bombers. One of the latter was shot down by Italian fighters. Shortly after, the destroyers Lanzerotto Malocello and Premuda joined the cruisers. Premuda was detached and reached Trapani at 2105 B/15. The light cruisers Eugenio di Savoia and Raimondo Montecuccoli, escorted by the destroyers Alfredo Oriani, Lanzerotto Malocello and Ascari reached Naples at 1000 B/16-1154 B/16.

The tribulations of Ugolino Vivaldi. She was proceeding to Pantelleria, towed by Lanzerotto Malocello and escorted by Premuda. At 0927 B/15, the towing cable broke and Ugolino Vivaldi ordered Premuda to take over the towing and, nine minutes later, four Swordfish bombers attacked Lanzerotto Malocello and Ugolino Vivaldi, but they managed to avoid their torpedoes. At 0948 B/15, another Beaufort bomber missed astern the destroyer Ascari. At 1003 B/15, the destroyers Ascari and Alfredo Oriani rejoined the cruisers. At 1019 B/15, Premuda managed to pass a tow line to Ugolino Vivavli which was still on fire and were proceeding to Pantelleria, fifteen minutes later, they were sighted at long range by the Italian cruisers. At 1218 B/15, two minesweepers from Pantelleria joined them and were asked to provide all the available fire extinguishers and at 1400 B/15, Ugolino Vivaldi dropped anchor at Scauri (Pantelleria). At 1640 B/15, she ordered Premuda and Lanzerotto Malocello to leave the formation and rejoin the 7th Cruiser Division. At 2000 B/15, The damaged destroyer was joined by the tug Salvatore I and the fire was finally extinguished an hour later. At 0728 B/16, Ugolino Vivaldi departed from Pantelleria towed by Salvatore I, but the tug had engine defects and could not proceed at more than 5 knots. They were escorted by the torpedo boats Castore and Polluce and the tug Terracina and reached Trapani at 0154 B/17. (38)

11 Aug 1942 Around 1345 B/11, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) departed Malta for her 13th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean). She was to provide cover during 'Operation Pedestal'. Upon completion of this duty she was to proceed to Gibraltar as HMS P 31 was to return to the U.K. as she was due to refit.
She and HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) were swept out until around 1625 B/11, by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN).

[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed. Actually no logs are available for the months of August, September, October and November 1942 so some datails for there months may be missing.] (7)
12 Aug 1942 At 1900 B/12 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) reached her patrol position in position 'T' (35°51.5'N, 12°17'E).

She was part of patrol line with HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) (in position 'R'), HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN) (in position 'S'), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) (in position 'V'), HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) (in position 'W') and HMS Utmost (Lt. A.W. Langridge, RN) (in position 'X'). After midnight, the patrol line was moved and P 31 took up position in 36°18'N, 12°19.5'E. With the exception of aircraft, nothing was seen and in the evening of 13 August, she left her patrol for her onward passage to Gibraltar. (7)

12 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 2219 B/12, the Capt. S.10 ordered the submarines in the patrol line; HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN), HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. A.W. Langridge, RN) to take up positions on patrol line D as had been specified in their patrol orders. (39)
12 Aug 1942 At 2330 B/12, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2219 B/19 ordering the submarines in the patrol line to take up patrol line 'D'.
At 0001 B/13, HMS P 31 set course to proceed to position 36.18'N, 12.19.5'E which was her assigned patrol position on line 'D' (7)

13 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 0345 B/13, HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN), HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. A.W. Langridge, RN) were informed of the following; Convoy two hours late. Delay easterly movement on the surface until 0800 B/13 and dive at 1400 B/13.

In a signal timed 0826 B/13, the Capt. S.10 informed the above submarines of the following; Enemy cruisers retired. Dive immediately course 040° convoy escort will attack submarines south of 36°10'N after 0900 B/13. (39)
13 Aug 1942 At 2102 B/13, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) surfaced and left patrol commencing the passage to Gibraltar. She was to proceed through position 38°25'N, 09°01'E. (7)

14 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1604 A/14, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders to HMS P 211 (Cdr. B. Bryant, DSC, RN), HMS P 222 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. MacKenzie, RN) and HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN); P 211, P 222 and P 31 are to proceed diving by day until dawn on the day ofarrival thence on surface at the Commanding Officers discretion by the following routes. P 211 through position 'S.P.' (38°57'N, 02°54'E) at 0100 B/18 or on the 21st, position 'S.Q.' (38°33'N, 01°34'E) at 1100 B/18 or on the 21st, position 'S.R.' (180° - Cape de Gata - 6 miles) at 0400 B/20 or on the 23rd, arriving at Gibraltar 0830 B/21 or on the 24th. P 222 through position 'S.P.' at 2330 B/18, position 'S.Q.' at 0500 B/19, position 'S.R.' at 0200 B/21 arriving at Gibraltar at 0830 B/22. P 31 through position 'S.P.' at 0330 B/20, position 'S.Q.' at 0001 B/21, position 'S.R.' at 0130 B/23 arriving at Gibraltar at 0830 B/24. Bombing restrictions are arranged. If unable to comply within two hours report position, course and speed. (39)

20 Aug 1942 At 1715 B/20, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) passed her signal timed 1630 B/20 in which she reported being over three hours astern of her expected station. She reported her position as being 38°50'N, 02°32'E.
In a response to the signal the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) ordered HMS P 31 to arrived at Gibraltar at 1500 B/24. (7)
24 Aug 1942 Around 1200 B/24, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) ended her 13th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean) at Gibraltar. On arrival D/G trials were carried out. (7)
27 Aug 1942 Around 1500 A/27, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) departed Gibraltar for passage to Portsmouth.

She was to pass though position 'A' (43°02'N, 13°29'W) at 0030 A/31. Further routing provided by the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) was as follows; Position 'B' (45°01'N, 12°59'E), Position 'C' (47°01'N, 12°31'E), Position 'D' (49°08'N, 11°59'E), Position 'E' (49°22'N, 09°01'E), Position 'F' (49°36'N, 05°02'E), Rendezvous position off Portland Bill A.M. on 9 September as will be ordered by the Capt. S.5.

Bombing restrictions were arranged, 25 miles either side line joining positions as follows; 1) Between 'A' and 'B' from 2200 A/30 to 0800 A/2. 2) Between 'B' and 'C' from 0200 A/2 to 2000 A/3. 3) Between 'C' and 'D' from 1200 A/3 to 1200 A/5. 4) Between 'D' and 'E' from 0001 A/5 to 2000 A/6. 5) Between 'E' and 'F' from 0100 A/6 to 1200 A/8. 6) Between 'F' and 'the rendezvous position' will be ordered by the Capt. S.5.

In a signal timed 1743 A/27 from F.O.S. the times were amended as follows as a mistake had been made in the earlier schedule; 1) Between 'A' and 'B' from 2200 A/30 to 0800 A/1. 2) Between 'B' and 'C' from 0200 A/1 to 2000 A/2. 3) Between 'C' and 'D' from 1200 A/2 to 1200 A/4. 4) Between 'D' and 'E' from 0001 A/4 to 2000 A/5. 5) Between 'E' and 'F' from 0100 A/5 to 1200 A/7. 6) Between 'F' and 'the rendezvous position' will be ordered by the Capt. S.5.
Due to a shortage of torpedoes in the Mediterranean and at Gibraltar, HMS P 31 was carrying only two Mark IV and two Mark II torpedoes.

31 Aug 1942 In a signal timed 1906 A/31, the Flag Officer Submarines sent the following orders to HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN); Proceed with all despatch towards Cape Vilan, Spain. Total bombing restrictions have been arranged.
This was to intercept a German transport that had been reported in position 40°40'N, 17°20'W which was making for Cape Finisterre. (40)

1 Sep 1942 At 0800 A/1, in position 43°18'N, 10°52'W, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) left patrol as nothing had been sighted. Course was set to comply with the orders in the signal timed 1906 A/31 from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) which had contained the following orders; 1) Estimate your approximate position at 0800 A/1 will be ('X') 43°21'N, 10°28'W. 2) If nothing is sighted by this time proceed to dive by day through position 'B' to 'F' inclusive as given in my signal 1024 A/26 [see the event for 27 August 1942 for this route] to R/V with escort off Portland Bill A.M/1O as ordered by Capt. (S) 5. 3) H.Q.C.C., is requested to institute new submerged bombing restriction 25 miles either side of line joining the positions as given earlier. 4) Total bombing restrictions as in my signal 1610 A/31 will be cancelled as from 0800 A/1. (39)

4 Sep 1942 In a signal timed 1037 A/4, the Capt. S.5 sent the following orders to HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN); From position 'F' (49°36'N, 05°02'E) proceed through the following positions; Position 'G' (180° - Start Point - 20 miles). Position 'H' (180° - Portland Bill - 16 miles to make rendezvous with the escort in Position 'J' (140° - Portland Bill - 6 miles at 0700 A/10 for passage to Portsmouth. H.Q.C.C. (Headquarters Coastal Command) is requested to arrange submerged bombing restrictions. (41)

6 Sep 1942 At 0049 A/6, in position 48°59'N, 11°35'W, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) sighted a schooner which had one while light burning, was seen to fire a green Very Light when about 2 miles on the submarines starboard beam. HMS P 31 turned away and dived for 45 minutes. No HE was heard. It was though extremely unlikely that the submarine had been observed as the schooner's sails were barely visibly. On surfacing the schooner was no longer in sight. (7)

8 Sep 1942 At 0203 A/8, in position 49°31'N, 06°49'W, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) had a narrow escape when she was just able to avoid a floating mine. The Officer of the Watch, Lt. D.R. Wilson, RANVR was able to order a course change just in time. (7)
10 Sep 1942 At 0700 A/10, HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) was in position 140° - Portland Bill - 6 miles but these was no sign of her escort, the Free French submarine chaser Chasseur 10.
At 0750 A/10, Chasseur 10 was sighted in position 117° - Portland Bill - 7.5 miles and rendezvous was made.
At 1400 A/10, HMS P 31 ended her 14th war patrol at Portsmouth (Gosport / Fort Blockhouse). (7)
13 Sep 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) departed Portsmouth for Sheerness. She made the passage in convoy CE 119.
[For more info on this convoy see the event 'Convoy CE 119' for 13 September 1942. Info on this convoy is incomplete though.]
This convoy departed St. Helens Roads around 1400 A/13.
[Details for this convoy are lacking.] The submarine HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) was taking passage in this convoy.
[Only one merchant vessel is listed on the convoyweb website (offsite link. We have, so far, been unable to find a list of merchant vessels in this convoy from primary sources.]
The convoy was escorted by the escort destroyer HMS Pytchley (Lt.Cdr. H. Unwin, DSC and Bar, RN) and the (river) gunboat HMS Locust (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) W.J. Stride, RN).
The convoy arrived at Southend on 14 September 1942. (42)
14 Sep 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) arrived at Sheerness.
15 Sep 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.de B. Kershaw, DSO, RN) proceeded from Sheerness to the Chatham Dockyard where she is to be refitted.
24 Dec 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) completed her refit at the Chatham Dockyard. (43)
26 Dec 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) proceeded from the Chatham Dockyard to Sheerness.
In the afternoon she conducted trials off Sheerness. These included diving and D/G trials. (43)
27 Dec 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted gunnery trials off Sheerness. (43)
28 Dec 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted trials off Sheerness. (43)
30 Dec 1942 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Sheerness for Portsmouth. She made the passage in convoy CW 145.
[For more info on this convoy see the event 'Convoy CW 145' for 30 December 1942. Info on this convoy is incomplete though.] (43)
This convoy departed Southend around 1300 A/30.
[Details for this convoy are lacking.] The submarine HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) was taking passage in this convoy.
[Only three merchant vessels are listed on the convoyweb website (offsite link. We have, so far, been unable to find a list of merchant vessels in this convoy from primary sources.]
The convoy was escorted by the escort destroyer HMS Atherstone (Lt. E.N. Wood, DSC, RNVR). Several minor warships were also part of the convoy escort.
The convoy arrived in St. Helens Roads around 1500 A/31. (37)
31 Dec 1942 Around 1615 A/31, HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) arrived at Portsmouth (Gosport / Fort Blockhouse). (43)
2 Jan 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Portsmouth. (44)

3 Jan 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Portsmouth for passage to Holy Loch. She made the passage together with HMS P 229 (Lt. R. Gatehouse, DSC, RN) and HMS Oberon (Lt.Cdr. J.W. McCoy, RN). They were escorted HMS Unst (T/Lt. J.R. Smith, RNR) until 0830 A/04 when the armed yacht HMS Star of India (T/A/Lt.Cdr. R.S. Keating, RNR) took over the escort. (44)
6 Jan 1943 Around 1300 A/6, HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) arrived at Holy Loch to begin a post refit work-up / training period. (44)
7 Jan 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted independent exercises in the Clyde area. (44)
8 Jan 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted independent exercises in the Clyde area. (44)
10 Jan 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted independent exercises in Loch Ranza. (44)
11 Jan 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) proceeded from Loch Ranza to Holy Loch. (44)
13 Jan 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted torpedo firing trials in lower Loch Long. (44)

18 Jan 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area. Also depth charge trials were carried out. She proceeded to Rothesay upon completion of these trials / exercises. (44)

19 Jan 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted S.T.U. trials in the Clyde area. Also W/T calibraition trials were carried out. She proceeded to Holy Loch upon completion of these trials. (44)
20 Jan 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted gunnery and attack exercises in the Clyde area. (44)
21 Jan 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted torpedo firing trials in the lower Loch Long. (44)
23 Jan 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area on completion of which she proceeded to Campbeltown. (44)
24 Jan 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Campbeltown with HMS St. Modwen (Lt.Cdr. F.J. Webster, RNR). (44)
25 Jan 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted radar exercises off Campbeltown. Upon completion of these exercises she proceeded to Rothesay. (44)
26 Jan 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted attack exercises and a bombardment exercise in the Clyde area. (44)
27 Jan 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) proceeded from Rothesay to Holy Loch. (44)
28 Jan 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted D/G trials off Helensburgh. (44)
29 Jan 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted sound trials in Loch Goil. (44)
30 Jan 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted sound trials in Loch Goil. These were followed by exercises with HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN). (44)
31 Jan 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted sound trials in Loch Goil. (44)
1 Feb 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted attack exercises in Loch Goil during which HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN) acted as target. (45)
2 Feb 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) proceeded from Loch Goil to Holy Loch. (45)

4 Feb 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) and HMS P 229 (Lt. R. Gatehouse, DSC, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area with each other and also with HMS Ambuscade (Lt.Cdr. D.E. Mansfield, RN). (45)

5 Feb 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) returned to Holy Loch upon completion of yesterday's exercises. While doing so a practice attack was made on HMS Cardiff (A/Capt. A.G. Davidson, RN). (45)

6 Feb 1943 Around 1400 A/6, HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Holy Loch for Scapa Flow. On departure compass adjustment trials were carried out. The passage north through the Minches was made together with HrMs O 19 (Lt.Cdr. H.F. Bach Kolling, RNN(R)). They were escorted by HMS Pennyworth (T/Lt. R.A. Mason, RNVR). (45)
8 Feb 1943 Around 1245 A/8, HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) arrived at Scapa Flow where she was to participate in A/S exercises in the coming period. (45)
9 Feb 1943 HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) participated in A/S exercises at / off Scapa Flow. (45)

10 Feb 1943 HMS Uganda (Capt. W.G. Andrewes, RN) conducted torpedo firing, A/S and underway refuelling exercises at Scapa Flow. The A/S exercises were with HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) and the underway refuelling exercises with HMS Quail (Lt.Cdr. R.F. Jenks, RN). (46)

16 Feb 1943 HMS Ullswater (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) participated in A/S exercises at / off Scapa Flow. (45)
17 Feb 1943 HMS Ullswater (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) participated in A/S exercises at / off Scapa Flow. (45)
18 Feb 1943 HMS Ullswater (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) participated in A/S exercises at / off Scapa Flow. (45)
19 Feb 1943 HMS Ullswater (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) participated in A/S exercises at / off Scapa Flow. (45)

20 Feb 1943 HMS Ullswater (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) participated in A/S exercises at / off Scapa Flow. HMCS Athabaskan (Cdr. G.R. Miles, DSO, OBE, RCN) was most likely one of the ships participating in the A/S exercises. (47)
22 Feb 1943 HMS Ullswater (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted independent exercises at / off Scapa Flow. (45)

24 Feb 1943 HMS Ullswater (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises at Scapa Flow with HMS Newfoundland (Capt. W.R. Slayter, DSC, RN) and HMS Kenya (Capt. D.P. Evans, RN).
Following these A/S exercises, HMS Kenya and HMS Newfoundland conducted gunnery exercises to the west of Scapa Flow. (45)

25 Feb 1943 HMS Ullswater (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises at / off Scapa Flow with HMS Chiddingfold (Lt.Cdr. L.W.L. Argles, RN) and HMS Eggesford (Lt.Cdr. D.W. Austin, RN). These exercises were however soon cancelled. (45)
26 Feb 1943 HMS Ullswater (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) participated in A/S exercises at / off Scapa Flow. (45)
27 Feb 1943 HMS Ullswater (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) participated in A/S exercises at / off Scapa Flow. Also a boarding party exercise was carried out with HMS Meynell (Lt. B.M.D. I'Anson, RN).
[No logs of HMS Ullwater / HMS Uproar are available for the months of March, April and May 1943. Some details for these months will thus be missing.] (45)

5 Mar 1943 HMS Ullswater (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Scapa Flow for Holy Loch. She joined the submarines HMS Universal (Lt. C. Gordon, RN) and ORP Dzik (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B.S. Romanowski) and their escort HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN) coming from Dundee and which were also on passage to Holy Loch.
7 Mar 1943 HMS Ullswater (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) arrived at Holy Loch from Scapa Flow.
HMS Ullswater was renamed HMS Uproar the following day.

22 Mar 1943 Around 1100 A/22, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Holy Loch for Gibraltar. Before departure compass adjustment trials had been carried out. During the day she carried out exercises with HMS Biter (Capt. E.M.C. Abel Smith, RN). Upon completion of these exercises she made rendezvous with ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.K.S. Koziolkowski, DSC). They were escorted for the passage south through the Irish Sea by HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR).

On parting company with the escort HMS Uproar was to proceed through positions 49°30'N, 09°01'W, 49°30'N, 12°26'W and 49°10'N, 12°54'W and then southwards to position 43°02'N, 12°54'W where she was to arrive at 1530 A/30.
While en-route HMS Uproar was ordered to patrol about 200 nautical miles north-west of Cape Finisterre, Spain to intercept an outbound German blockade runner making this passage her 15th war patrol.

24 Mar 1943 At 1800 A/24, in position 360° - Round Island - 7 miles, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) and ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.K.S. Koziolkowski, DSC) parted company with HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR). (7)

27 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 2301 A/27, the Flag Officer Gibraltar sent the following orders to HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN); After crossing 43°01'N at 1500 A/30 pass through the following positions at the times stated. Position 'T' (36°51'N, 09°13'W) at 2000 A/3. Position 'U' (36°37'N, 07°24'W) at 0630 A/4. Position 'V' (35°53'N, 06°15'W) at 1350 A/4. Position 'W' (35°49'N, 06°06'W) at 1420 A/4 thence to Gibraltar. (48)

29 Mar 1943 In the evening HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) was ordered to patrol in position 45°10'N, 12°50'W. For the next two days Uproar is ordered several times to take up patrol other positions. (7)

29 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 2229 A/29, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) informed USS Herring (T/Cdr. J. Corbus, USN), ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.K.S. Koziolkowski, DSC) and HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) of the following. Patrol orders were also included to try to intercept the reported enemy merchant vessel; Aircraft report at 1650 A/29, one enemy merchant ship escorted by five destroyers, one of which is a 'Narvik'-class, in position 45°50'N, 06°22'W, course 250°, speed unknown but possibly 12 knots. Proceed to reach the following positions by daylight on the 30th; USS Herring 44°46'N, 11°50'W. ORP Sokol 44°14'N, 12°26'W. HMS Uproar 45°10'N, 12°50'W.

This referred to the Italian blockade breaker Himalaya (6240 GRT, built 1929) which was on passage from Bordeaux to Japan. At the time that she was sighted by the aircraft she was escorted by the torpedo boats T 23, T 2, T 12, T 18 and Falke.

At 2130 B/29, the escort left Himalaya and turned back. They arrived at La Pallice at 1130 B/29. Himalaya proceeded alone but apparently had no been given the proper sailing orders and was way off her expected position. At the same time the 8th Z-Flottille (Z 23, Z 24, Z 32 and Z 37) had sailed to meet the Italian blockade runner Pietro Orseolo (6344 GRT, built 1939) to escort her in. At 2150 B/30, Himalaya signalled that she had been sighted by enemy warships (apparently, she had not been informed of the sortie of the 8th Z-Flottille) in Quadrat BE 9688 (43°51'N, 12°15'W), mistaking the German destroyers for British. She began the process of scuttling, destroying her secret papers and abandoning ship. Fortunately, the error was soon realised and the crew returned aboard, the scuttling cancelled but without her code books, she was forced to turn back and arrived at La Pallice at 1330 B/2 April.
In an amplifying report timed 2318 A/29 the course of the enemy was given as 255°.
[Multiple enemy blockade breakers were on the move both westwards and eastwards though the area at this time though.] (49)
30 Mar 1943 At 0433 A/30, HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) arrived in her ordered patrol position, 45°10'N, 12°50'W.
At 0638 A/30, HMS P 31 dived and patrolled within two miles of the ordered position.
At 1229 A/30, the signal from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) timed 1228 A/30 was received.
At 1302 A/30, the signal from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) timed 1239 A/30 was received.
At 1417 A/30, the signal from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) timed 1333 A/30 was received.

At 1459 A/30, the signal from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) timed 1348 A/30 was received. No attempt was made to comply with this signal ass the reported enemy vessel was at least 80 miles away which made it impossible for HMS Uproar to intercept her. Instead an attempt was made to intercept the ship reported in the signal timed 1228 A/30.

At 1556 A/30, HMS P 31 surfaced. Course was altered to the south and speed was increased to maximum in order to be on the expected enemy's route by 2030 A/30. Assuming the enemy was proceeding on course 250° at 11 knots.
At 1900 A/30, HMS P 31 dived in position 44°42'N, 12°42'W to avoid being sighted by a possible air escort of the expected enemy vessel.
At 2025 A/30, HMS P 31 was in position 44°40'N, 12°42'W. Course was altered to 070° to proceed along the expected route of the enemy vessel.
At 2055 A/30, HMS P 31 surfaced.
At 2152 A/30, the signal from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) timed 1959 A/30 was received.
At 2201 A/30, the signal from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) timed 1936 A/30 was received which ordered HMS P 31 to patrol in position 44°45'N, 11°25'W.
At 2248 A/30, having seen nothing of the enemy course was altered to comply with the signal timed 1936 A/30.
At 2257 A/30, the signal from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) timed 2232 A/30 was received.
[For the signals see the second 'event' listed for this day.] (50)

30 Mar 1943 The following signals were sent by the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) to USS Herring (T/Cdr. J. Corbus, USN), ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.K.S. Koziolkowski, DSC) and HMS P 31 (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) on this day;
Signal timed 1228 A/30; Aircraft report, position, course and speed of of one merchant ship, 45°20'N, 10°05'W, 250°, 11 knots at 0945 A/30.
Signal timed 1239 A/30; My 1228 A/30. To ensure interception in daylight shift patrol position 20 miles in direction 070°.

Signal timed 1333 A/30; Aircraft report at 1220 A/30, position, course and speed of one merchant ship 44°25'N, 11°04'E, 225°, 12 knots. Believed to be Tannenfels. This is probably a different ship to that reported in my 1228 A/30.
Signal timed 1348 A/30; Cancel my 1239 A/30. Endeavour to intercept ship reported in my 1333 A/30 which is now considered same vessel as that in my 1228 A/30.

Signal timed 1936 A/30; If nothing has been sighted by dark submarines proceed as follows; USS Herring to position 44°33'N, 10°55'W. ORP Sokol to position 44°16'N, 11°17'W. HMS Uproar to position 44°45'N, 11°25'W. Submarines are not to be attacked unless definitely identified as hostile. It is possible enemy destroyers may be encountered returning to the eastward.
Signal timed 1959 A/30; Aircraft report position, course and speed of one merchant vessel at 1910 A/30 as 44°10'N, 11°25'E, 220°, 10 knots.
Signal timed 2232 A/30; [We have been unable to find the text of this signal but apparently the submarines were ordered to report whether they had sighted anything.]

Signal timed 2301 A/30; A ship [this was Himalaya] made an S.O.S. at 1950 Z/30 from position 43°59'N, 12°18'E, stating she had been ordered to stop by a warship. It is uncertain whether this signal is genuine. (48)
31 Mar 1943 At 0024 A/31, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) passed her signal timed 2317 A/30 as had been ordered in the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) signal timed 2232 A/30.
At 0348 A/31, the signal from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) timed 0012 A/31 was received.
At 0640 A/31, HMS P 31 dived in her ordered patrol position, 44°45'N, 11°25'W.
During the day problems were encountered with the W/T set. It was feared that signals were being missed. [This was apparently not the case.]
At 2204 A/31, the signal from the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) timed 2009 A/31 was received which ordered her to leave patrol and continue the passage to Gibraltar.
At 2235 A/31, course was altered to comply with the orders in the signal timed 2009 A/31. (7)

31 Mar 1943 In a signal timed 2009 A/31, ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.K.S. Koziolkowski, DSC) and HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) were ordered to leave patrol forthwith for Gibraltar proceeding on the surface. ORP Sokol pass through position 43°01'N, 12°26'E at 0700 A/1. HMS Uproar pass through position 43°01'N, 12°54'E at 1500 A/1. Furher routing will be signalled. (48)

1 Apr 1943 In a signal timed 0349 A/1, the Flag Officer Gibraltar sent the following orders to HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN); Amended times schedule to my signal timed 2301 A/27. After crossing 43°01'N at 1500 A/1 pass through the following positions at the times stated. Position 'T' (36°51'N, 09°13'W) at 0400 A/6. Position 'U' (36°37'N, 07°24'W) at 0410 A/7. Position 'V' (35°53'N, 06°15'W) at 1110 A/7. Position 'W' (35°49'N, 06°06'W) at 1200 A/7 thence to Gibraltar.
After passing position 'U' proceeding on the surface at your discretion at 10 knots. Do not cross meridian of 06°40'W before 0630 A/7. (48)

3 Apr 1943 At 1220 A/3, while submerged off the west coast of Portugal, in approximate position 40°10'N, 11°20'W, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) sighted a British hospital ship proceeding on course 358° [or 350°, difficult to read in the patrol report] at 13 knots. The range was closed to 3000 yards. She looked similar like the Lady Drake. No information had been received of this ship's movements.
This must have been the British hospital ship Newfoundland (6791 GRT, built 1925) on passage from Algiers (departed 31 March) to Belfast (arrived 06 April). (51)

5 Apr 1943 At 0836 A/5, while submerged well to the north-west of Cape St. Vincent, Portugal, in approximate position 37°37'N, 09°44'W, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) sighted a small sailing schooner proceeding on course 360° (approximately). (7)

6 Apr 1943 At 0855 A/6, while submerged south-south-west of Portimao, Portugal, in approximate position 36°47'N, 08°38'W, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) sighted a Spanish merchant vessel of about 6000 tons proceeding on course 310°. She passed within two miles.
Numerous fishing vessels were sighted throughout the day.
At 2331 A/6, the signal timed 2251 A/6 from the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) was received with ordered HMS Uproar to keep west of 07°W until 0630 A/7. (7)
7 Apr 1943 At 1830 A/7, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) ended her 15th war patrol at Gibraltar. On entering harbour she ran over the D/G range.

HMS Uproar arrived at Gibraltar with some defects, including the her Asdic and there was also a problem with her starboard screw. Most likely she was docked at Gibraltar before she left for her next patrol. (7)
15 Apr 1943 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for her 16th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol of Marseilles.
On departure from Gibraltar Asdic trials were carried out with ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.K.S. Koziolkowski, DSC) before the submarines parted company at 1800 A/15 and proceeded on patrol.

HMS Uproar was to proceed through the following positions; Position 'S' (180° - Cape Calaburra - 5 miles at 2100 A/15. Position 'T' (180° - Cape de Glance - 5 miles unless otherwise ordered by Capt.(S). Position 'U' - 135° - Cape Palos - 5 miles at 0001 A/18th. Position 39°57'N, 01°37'E at 2000 A/19th. Position 42°19'N, 04°20'W at 1200 A/21 and then patrol north of 42°19'N between 04°00'E and 05°50'E.
At 2141 A/15, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1620 A/15 was received. This signal contained information on the patrol orders but we were unable to find the text of this signal.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (52)
16 Apr 1943 At 0610 A/16, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived in position 36°33'N, 03°18'W.
At 1033 A/16, HMS Uproar sighted a Spanish merchant vessel proceeding on course 090°. The vessel was showing the national colours and ensign.

At 1610 A/16, a Spanish merchant vessel proceeding on course 315° was sighted. This vessel was also showing the national colours and ensign. At 2048 A/16, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 36°36'N, 02°44'W. (7)
17 Apr 1943 At 0012 A/17, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) sighted an illuminated merchant vessel proceeding on course 300°. Most likely this would be a Spanish vessel.
At 0206 A/17, another illuminated merchant vessel was sighted proceeding on course 240°. Most likely another Spanish ship.
At 0603 A/17, HMS Uproar dived in position 36°56'N, 01°38'W.
At 1807 A/17, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 37°09'N, 01°19'W.
At 2000 A/17, another illuminated merchant vessel was sighted proceeding on course 230°. Most likely another Spanish ship.
At 2200 A/17, yet another illuminated merchant vessel was sighted proceeding on course 230°. Most likely another Spanish ship. (7)
18 Apr 1943 At 0140 A/18, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) passed a Spanish merchant vessel which was proceeding on course 060°. She was showing illuminated colours.
At 0555 A/18, HMS Uproar dived in position 38°01'N, 00.10'W.
At 1115 A/18, while submerged, HMS Uproar passed a Spanish merchant vessel proceeding on course 034°. This vessel was showing national colours and ensign.
At 1300 A/18, while submerged HMS Uproar passed another Spanish merchant vessel also proceeding on course 034°. This vessel too was showing national colours and ensign.
At 2041 A/18, HMS Uproar dived to avoid being sighted by a Spanish merchant vessel proceeding on course 230°. She was showing illuminated colours.
At 2117 A/18, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 38°21'N, 00°11'E. (7)
19 Apr 1943 At 0100 A/19, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) passed two illuminated merchant vessels which were proceeding on course 230°. Presumably this were Spanish ships.
At 0236 A/19, HMS Uproar passed a Spanish merchant vessel proceeding on course 230°. She was showing illuminated colours.
At 0558 A/18, HMS Uproar dived in position 39°20'N, 01°12'E.
At 2021 A/18, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 39°36'N, 01°17'E. (7)
21 Apr 1943 At 1830 A/21, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) entered the sink at sight area. (7)
22 Apr 1943 At 0200 A/22, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) entered her patrol area.
At 2013 A/22, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 0942 A/22 was received which contained orders for leaving patrol (presumably on the 25th). We have been unable to find the text of this signal. (7)
25 Apr 1943 At 0215 A/25,HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) left her patrol area to proceed to Algiers. No enemy shipping at all had been sighted in the patrol area. (7)

28 Apr 1943 At 0556 A/28, two Allied fighter aircraft, thought to be Mustangs, passed overhead of HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) in position 36°59'N, 03°02'E. The leading aircraft machine gunned HMS Uproar. Investigation showed that the pilots had not been informed of the presence of HMS Uproar
Around 0800 A/28, HMS Uproar ended her 16th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean) at Algiers.
She was escorted in by HMS Juliet (T/Lt. T. Paxton, RNR) which had joined around 0630 A/28. (7)
5 May 1943 Around 1500 A/5, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Algiers for Malta where she was to join the 10th Submarine Flotilla.
On departure exercises were carried out off Algiers with ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.K.S. Koziolkowski, DSC) which also departed Algiers for Malta.

HMS Uproar was routed as follows; Position 36°58'N, 03°14'E at 1930 A/5. Position 38°06'N, 03°56'E at 0500 A/5. Position 38°14'N, 11°18'E at 0300/10th. Position 'P' (?) at 0500 A/10. Further routing would be signalled by the Capt. S.10.

7 May 1943 In a signal timed 1209 B/7, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.K.S. Koziolkowski, DSC) and HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN); Onward passage from position 'P' (?), through positions 'Q' (?) and 'R' (?) thence direct for the end of the Malta searched channel. Both to arrive at 0830 B/12 to rendezvous with the minesweeper escort. Keep to starboard of route.

In a signal timed 1053 B/8, the Capt. S.10 sent new orders; Cancel my 1209 B/7. Onward routeing from position 'P' through positions 13°15'N, 35°57'E, 37°07'N, 13°56'E and 35°41'N, 14°23'E. Both submarines to arrive at 0830 B/12 to rendezvous with the minesweeper escort at the southern end of the searched channel. (53)
12 May 1943 At 1135 A/12, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) arrived at Marsamxett (Malta) from Algiers.
She was escorted in (identity of the escort not known). The escort had joined at 0835 A/12. (7)
31 May 1943 At 1600 B/31, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 17th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol of the East Calabrian coast in area 'Q'.
She was to proceed through the following positions 35°14'N, 15°35'E and 37°20'N, 16°52'E.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS Uproar during this patrol see the map below.

5 Jun 1943 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) attacked the Italian transport Monte Cengio (former German KT 16, 834 GRT, built 1943) with 4 torpedoes about 5 nautical miles East of Capo Colonna. she was escorted by the torpedo boat Clio. They were on passage from Messina to Taranto.

Monte Cengio reported that three of the four torpedoes passed under her. An Italian escorting aircraft dropped two bombs on the submarine but only one exploded. Clio dropped seven 50kg depth charges and eight depth charges of the intimidation type.
0400 B/5 - Dived in position 054° - Cape Colonne - 7 miles.
0700 B/5 - While patrolling about 5 miles from Crotone harbour a small yacht was seen behind the breakwater. Most likely this was a naval auxiliary.
0705 B/5- A schooner was seen to leave Crotone. The enemy proceeded to the northward.

0750 B/5 - Two small motor minesweepers were seen leaving Crotone. They appreared to be sweeping on course 085°. They then turned to the southward down the coastal route and disappeared round Cape Colonne on course 170°. Both were painted light grey, two masts, small deckhouse aft and amidships. They were proceeding in line abreast
1000 B/5 - Sighted smoke bearing 210°.
1007 B/5 - In position 39°03'N, 17°17'E commenced attack on a 1500 tons motor tanker escorted by one Avio-class minelayer, two small motor minesweepers and one seaplane.

1059 B/5 - Fired four torpedoes from about 5000 yards. One explosion was heard 2 minutes and 40 seconds after firing the last torpedo. A counter attack followed in which 9 depth charges were dropped but these did no damage.
1134 B/5 - The last depth charges were dropped.
1150 B/5 - Returned to periscope depth. No escorts in sight. Thought to see the target outside Crotone harbour. Withdrew to seaward to reload.
2136 B-5 - Surfaced in position 040° - Cape Colonne - 19 miles. (7)
6 Jun 1943 At 0357 A/6, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived in position 054° - Punta Alice - 7 miles. She then close Punta Alice and proceeded south along the coastal convoy route.
At 1053 A/6, HMS Uproar went deep for a patrolling aircraft and then closed Crotone to investigate the harbour but no shipping was seen.
At 1500 A/6, two schooners were seen close inshore proceeding northwards.
At 2153 A/6, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 054° - Cape Colonne - 10 miles. (7)

7 Jun 1943 At 0002 A/7, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) received an enemy report from a patrolling aircraft. This must have referred to the following report from aircaft 'GDK/H' which started the following; Two enemy destroyers and two small craft in position 38°59'N. 17°15'E, Course 210°, speed 12 knots.
HMS Uproar was not in a position to intercept this reported convoy.
At 0359 B/7, HMS Uproar dived in position 093° - Cape Colonne - 8 miles. She then closed the coastal route between Capes Rizzuto and Cape Colonne.
At 1415 B/7, a motor schooner passed at a range of 2 miles. Enemy course was 030°.
At 1420 B/7, a seaplane was seen circling round Cape Rizzuto. A small coaster, of about 300 tons, rounded the cape and proceeded to the northwards along the coastal route escorted by the seaplane.
It was decided not to attack this coaster as there were only four torpedoes left on board and it was hoped bigger game may come eventually.
At 2210 B/7, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 138° - Cape Rizzuto - 8 miles. She then patrolled across the direct convoy route from Cape Stilo to Cape Rizzuto. (7)

7 Jun 1943 In a signal timed 1503 B/7, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) J.K.S. Koziolkowski, DSC) and HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN); Sokol leaves patrol to pass position 37°23'N, 16°00'E at 2000 B/10 then through positions 36°26'N, 15°43'E and 36°26'N, 14°21'E to arrive west sanctuary rendezvous at 0830 B/12 keeping south of 38° 00'N after 0001/10.
Uproar leaves zone 'Q' at 0001/1O to operate in zone 'M'. north of 38°N and leaves this area for Malta by same route as Sokol but all times 24 hours later. (55)
