8 Jun 1943 At 0400 B/8, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived in position 177° - Cape Rizzuto - 6 miles and then commenced to patrol one mile to seaward of the coastal route to Cape Rizzuto and Cape Colonne.
At 0800 B/8, a minesweeper passed to the southward.
At 0830 B/8, two F lighters passed to the southward close inshore. They were flying the German ensign.

At 1000 B/8, two small craft, similar to large British LTC's with catwalks, passed northwards close inshore. The were escorted by a Fiat RS 14 seaplane. HMS Uproar was forced to go deep by this seaplane three times.
At 1430 B/8, a minesweeper passed northwards.
At 1745 B/8, a barquentine of about 200 tons passed to the north. She had an auxiliary motor.

At 1711 B/8, an aircraft report was received of one destroyer and two merchant vessel which must have passed out 5 miles to the south of HMS Uproar whilst the Fiat seaplane had forced the submarine to go deep. [At 1040 B/8 in position 090° - Cape Rizzuto - 5 miles, one destroyer, speed 15 knots, two medium merchant ships, speed 8 knots, course 270°.]
At 2111 B/8, an amplification of the enemy report that had been recieved at 1711 B8 was received. Considered this to have been two F-boats and one minesweeper.
At 2153 B/8 - HMS Uproar surfaced in position 150° - Cape Rizzuto - 7.5 miles and commenced to patrol on the direct convoy route from Punta Stilo to Cape Rizzuto. (54)

9 Jun 1943 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) attacked the Italian transport Sezze (former French Espiguette, 1109 GRT, built 1920) with four torpedoes of Capo Stilo. No hits were obtained. She was escorted by the torpedo boat Francesco Stocco. They were on passage from Messina to Crotone and Taranto.

On Francesco Stocco the alarm was given by a lookout who observed a periscope and shortly after three torpedo tracks. The torpedo boat turned to port and they passed 200 metres astern. The torpedoes did hit a beach south of Soverato. At the same time an escorting aircraft sighted four torpedo tracks and gave the alarm, it dived on the submarine and released two bombs. Francesco Stocco dropped immediately intimidation depth charges (30kg) and combed the tracks and released 100kg depth charges set at a depth of 100 metres. She carried out a second run and released another seven 100kg depth charges but she had only two left. She dropped a few more intimidation depth charges and rejoined her charge. The hunt was taken over by submarine chasers from the 8th A/S Group.

0024 B/9 - Received an enemy report of an aircraft of one unknown vessel. This ship should pass Cape Rizzuto shortly after 0430 B/9. As the night was very dark it was decided to proceed to a position where a submerged day attack could be carried out so proceeded toward Punta Stilo. [Position, course and speed of an unknown vessel is 39°26 N, 17°12'E, 265°, 10 knots. Aircraft 'FDS/R'. Signal was timed 2350 B/8.]
0408 B/9 - Dived in position 058° - Punta Stilo - 11 miles.
0940 B/9 - Sighted smoke of Punta Stilo.
0945 B/9 - Sighted an aircraft patrolling of Punta Stilo.
1000 B/9 - Closed Capo Stilo at speed for 15 minutes.
1025 B/9 - Started attack on a 2500 tons merchant vessel escorted by a Generale-class torpedo boat and a seaplane.

1114 B/9 - In position 38°37'N, 16°39'E fired four torpedoes at the merchant ship from about 7500 yards. Four explosions were heard but were thought to be the torpedoes hitting the beach and not to be hits.
1122 B/9 - An ineffective counter attack followed in which twenty-one depth charges were dropped.
1220 B/9 - Returned to periscope depth. A seaplane was seen very close. Went to 80 feet. The area was patrolled by aircraft during the whole afternoon.
2153 B/9 - Surfaced in position 38°31'N, 17°11'E. (7)

10 Jun 1943 At 1957 B/10, in position 38°04'N, 16°40'E, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) sighted an Italian hospital ship, thought to be Toscana (9429 GRT, built 1923) or Sicilia (Italian, 9646 GRT, built 1924). This vessel passed at a range of one mile proceeding on course 240° at 10 knots.

The hospital ship was the Italian Toscana which had sailed from Bari at 1050 B/9 and arrived at Naples at 0725 B/12. This passage was rather long and she may have been diverted to the Syracuse area or perhaps toward Pantelleria (under heavy bombardment and soon to be occupied) on the way but we are not sure. (7)

11 Jun 1943 At 0615 B/11, in approximate position 37°33'N, 16°12'E, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) sighted an Italian escort vessel bearing 300°. The enemy was carrying out an A/S patrol. She passed 2.5 miles astern and disappeared on bearing 295°.
This was the Italian corvette Driade which sailed from Messina at 0055 B/11 for an A/S sweep at 1135 B/11. She sailed again at 1504 B/11 and returned at 0456 B/12.
At 0840 B/11, strong HE was picked up on bearing 030°. 210 Revs were counted.
At 0850 B/11, the HE faded out on the same bearing. No shipping was seen but two Cant flying boats were observed circling on this bearing. (7)
13 Jun 1943 At 1020 B/13, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) ended her 17th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in. The escort (unidentified) had joined around 0810 B/13. (7)

21 Jun 1943 At 1845 B/21, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 18th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to carry out a special operation, a periscope beach reconnaissance of the beaches to be used by the Canadian First Division for Operation 'Husky', the Allied landings on Sicily. Major J.M. Robinson was embarked to view the beaches and Lieutenant G.S. Sinclair, RNR, of COPP was to make sketches of these.
For the daily positions of HMS Uproar during this patrol see the map below.
22 Jun 1943 At 0407 B/22, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived in position 223° - Correnti Island - 9 miles. She then closed the coast and carried out a periscope reconnaissance of the beach.
At 2204 B/22, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 060° - Cape Passero - 10 miles. (7)
23 Jun 1943 At 0413 B/23, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived in position 132° - Cape Murro di Porco - 8 miles. The coast was closed to carry out a periscope reconnaissance.
At 2142 B/23, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 076° - Cape Passero - 10 miles. (7)

24 Jun 1943 At 0413 B/24, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived in position 36.30'N, 14.28'E. A small darkened boat was observed on diving. Asdic picked up the HE of two vessels proceeding at 300 revs. Avoiding action was taken. This were most probably motor torpedo boats.
At 0435 B/24, the HE faded on bearing 170°.
At 1300 B/24, HMS Uproar surfaced to the north of Gozo.
At 1705 B/24, HMS Uproar ended her 18th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (7)

2 Jul 1943 At 1835 B/2, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 19th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in/off the Gulf of Taranto to provide cover during Operation 'Husky', the Allied landings on Sicily.

5 Jul 1943 At 0830 B/5, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) sighted an unescorted coaster of 1500 tons. The enemy was northbound, passed Cape Colonne and entered Crotone. Owing to the restrictions on torpedo firing, no attack was made.
At 0950 B/5, HMS Uproar took avoiding action for two minesweepers which closed to within a mile of the submarine. The enemy then returned towards Cape Colonne. (7)
6 Jul 1943 At 0030 B/6, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) entered her patrol area. She closed Cape Maria di Leuca to obtain a land fix and identify land objects.
At 0830 B/6, Cape Maria di Leuca was sighted bearing 041°, 7.4 miles.
At 1320 B/6, an Italian hospital ship, thought to be the Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913) passed at a range of 4 miles. Enemy course was 120° at 11 knots.
The hospital ship sighted was indeed the Gradisca which sailed from Taranto at 0500 B/6 and arrived at Bari at 0930 B/7.

At 1711 B/6, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1204 B/6 was received which ordered the radar set to be turned off so the signals could not be picked up by the enemy so the positions of the submarine would not be given away. (7)

13 Jul 1943 In a signal timed 1401 B/13, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) and HMS Unbroken (Lt. B.J.B. Andrew, DSC, RN) to leave patrol P.M./15 routed along 18°E to 35°50'N passing this position at 1400 B/17. Thence course 270° to make rendezvous with HMS Swona (T/Lt. E.C. Davids, RNR) at the end of eastern swept channel at 1000 B/18. Surface passage on 17th and 18th as necessary. (56)
14 Jul 1943 At 1320 B/14, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) received the capt. S.10's signal timed 1053 B/14 in which the torpedo firing restrictions were cancelled.
[We have been unable to find the text of this signal.] (7)

15 Jul 1943 At 0800 B/15, west-south-west of Cape Santa Maria di Leuca, in position 39°44'N, 18°06'E, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) sighted a coaster of about 1000 tons escorted by a Navigatori-class destroyer and one seaplane passing to the westward close inshore. The range was too great to make an attack.
At 1820 B/15, HMS Uproar passed out of her patrol area to return to Malta. (7)
18 Jul 1943 Around 1345 B/18, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) ended her 19th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She had been escorted in from 0910 B/18 to 1245 B/18 by HMS Beryl (T/Lt. H. Olsberg, RNR). (7)

31 Jul 1943 Around 2000 B/31, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 20th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean). She is ordered to patrol in the Adriatic within an arc of a 15 miles radius from Bari.
She was to leave Malta by the eastern channel and then trough positions 35°56'N, 16°31'E at 2000 B/1, 39°44'N, 18° 52'E at 1200 B/3 and 40°40'N, 18°52'E at 0230 B/4.
5 Aug 1943 At 0420 B/5, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) entered her patrol area. She dived in position 010° - Mola di Bari - 12 miles.
At 1930 B/5, she arrived off Bari harbour. About 6 or 7 small and medium sized merchant ships were seen inside the harbour as well as numerous schooners, etc.
At 2135 B/5, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 050° - Cataldo Lighthouse - 4 miles and commenced to patrol half a mile on either side of this position, with the Asdic and RDF closed up. (7)

6 Aug 1943 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian naval auxiliary D 15/Brindisi (1977 GRT, built 1931) 7 nautical miles north-east of Bari, Italy in position 41°11'N, 16°56'E.

This was the first attack carried out by a submarine of the Tenth Flotilla with the use of radar. Brindisi and the torpedo boat Rosolino Pilo were escorting the transport Italia (Italian, 5203 GRT, built in 1905) from Bari to Teodo.

Upon the torpedoing of Brindisi the Rosolino Pilo escorted the Italia to Bari, then returned with a number of small vessels to assist the survivors who had taken to three lifeboats. A tug towed the three boats back to Bari, there were 8 missing from a crew of 112.
0001 B/6 - Retired to seaward to charge the battery.
0426 B/6 - Dived in position 080° - Cataldo Lighthourse - 8 miles.
0600 B/6 - Bari harbour could not be seen due to fog.
0900 B/6 - Minesweepers were seen to pass to seaward on course 070°.
0940 B/6 - Visibility was now slightly improved. Eight merchant vessels of about 3000 to 5000 tons were seen inside the harbour.
1130 B/6 - The minesweepers first seen at 0900 B/6 were seen to return to harbour.
2127 B/6 - Surfaced in position 051° - Cataldo Lighthouse - 3.6 miles with the engines stopped. Closed up radar and Asdic.

2129 B/6 - Obtained a radar contact bearing 230°, range 5000 yards. It was at first thought to be a false echo as many had been obtained before but in view of later events it was thought to be a destroyer [if this was indeed correct this might have been the torpedo boat Rosolino Pilo.]
2148 B/6 - Obtained a radar contact bearing 230°, range 7000 yards.
2149 B/6 - Sighted a darkened ship on the same bearing. Uproar was almost ahead so ran out for 3 minutes.
2155 B/6 - As Uproar would still be too close to fire torpedoes went full astern.
2158 B/6 - In position 41°11'N, 16°56'E fired three torpedoes from 500 to 600 yards. One hit was obtained.
2159 B/6 - Dived and took avoiding action. A few depth charges were dropped during the next hours but no damage was done to Uproar.
Depth charges were heard exploding until 0038 B/7. (7)
7 Aug 1943 At 0407 B/7, dived in position 029° - Cataldo Lighthouse - 19 miles. This due to a radar contact which proved to be a false echo. Remained submerged.

At 1000 B/7, the torpedo tubes were reloaded. [We find it odd that so much time elapsed before the torpedo tubes were reloaded but there is no remark on this in the narrative of Capt. S.10 with his remarks on this patrol.] Bari was then closed to within 4 miles. Minesweepers were seen sweeping the area of last night's attack. Also an aircraft was carrying out an A/S patrol in the area.
At 1630 B/7, HMS Uproar withdrew to seaward and patrolled at the end of the swept channel.

In a signal timed 1114 B/7, which was received at 2108 B/7, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN); 1) At 2000 B/9 pass through position 41°22'N, 16°52'E thence through position 41°22'N, 18°19'E at 0800 B/10 thence to patrol in the southern approaches to Valona. 2) At 2200 B/11 leave patrol passing through position 38°56'N, 18°33'E at 2200 B/12. Remainder of the route to Malta will be provided later.
At 2124 B/7, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 068° - Cataldo Lighthouse - 12 miles. (58)
8 Aug 1943 At 0426 B/8, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived in position 085° - Cataldo Lighthouse - 11 miles.
Bari was closed to inspect the harbour with a view to firing torpedoes through the entrance at ships inside. The weather conditions were suitable for such an attack.

HMS Uproar patrolled across the entrance to the harbour and closed to within 5 cables but all ships inside were screened by solid stone moles and a net was observed across the entrance. It was decided not to fire into the net. It was also noticed that all ships inside the harbour appeared to keep up steam and to be at immediate notice. One two-funnelled passenger liner of about 7000 tons camouflaged in light and dark grey was inside the harbour. This is thought to be an armed merchant cruiser.
At 1330 B/8, HMS Uproar withdrew to seaward and patrolled up and down the swept channel, 5 miles from the harbour.
At 2140 B/8, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 059° - Cataldo Lighthouse - 7.2 miles and patrolled across the swept channel 9 miles offshore. (7)
9 Aug 1943 At 0415 B/9, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived in position 063° - Cataldo Lighthouse - 6 miles. She patrolled along the swept channel, 8 miles from shore.
At 1700 B/9, HMS Uproar passed out of the patrol area. On account of a stoker with a poisoned hand, which was not responding to treatment, Lt. Herrick, decided to return to Malta.
At 2105 B/9, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 41°22'N, 17°15'E. (7)

9 Aug 1943 In a signal timed 1530 B/9, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) received the following orders from Capt. S.10 regarding her route to Malta after leaving patrol; Onward route from position 38°56'N, 18°33'E at 2200 B/12. Position 38°08'N, 18°01'E at 0500 B/13. Position 35°44'N, 16°39'E at 1200 B/14. Thence to rendezvous with a minesweeper 5 miles east of the end of eastern searched channel at 0800 B/15. Do not proceed west of the minesweepers position until in company. Ease passage as necessary on the 13th. (59)

10 Aug 1943 At 0037 B/10, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) passed her signal timed 2107 B/9 to Capt. S.10. This signal stated the following; Have one stoker with poisoned hand, poison still spreading. Am returning to Malta passing through position 41°22'N, 18°19'E at 0300 B/10 and 40°20'N, 19°00'E at 0100 B/11.
At 0443 B/10, the signal timed 0152 B/10 from the Capt. S.10 was received. This signal contained the following orders; Subtract 24 hours from all times in my 1530 B/9 to arrive Malta on 14 August.
[This signal was sent before HMS Uproar's signal timed 2107 B/9 was received.]
At 0332 B/10, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0142 B/10 was received but it could not be deciphered.
At 0700 B/10, the Admiralty's signal timed 0531 A/10, was received which contained instructions regarding treatment of the stokers poisoned hand. (58)

11 Aug 1943 At 0410 B/11, in position 39°47'N, 18°54'E, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) encountered HMS Unruly (Lt. J.P. Fyfe, RN). The two submarines passed only at a range of 100 to 200 yards.
HMS Unruly reported the encounter at 0409 B/11 in position 39°48'N, 18°53'E.
Both submarines realised the identity of the other submarines. HMS Uproar challenged and HMS Unruly replied with a signal grenade. (60)
14 Aug 1943 At 1120 B/14, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) ended her 20th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
The escort, HMS Seaham (Lt.Cdr. R.E. Brett, RNR), had joined at 0745 B/14. (7)
24 Aug 1943 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) participated in A/S exercises off Malta. (61)

27 Aug 1943 Around 1900 B/27, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 21st war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to act as a beacon off Kuriat, Tunisia in an exercise with transport aircraft. During these exercises she was escorted by HMS BYMS 2028 (Skr. J.R. Clark, RNR).

28 Aug 1943 At 1505 B/28, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) and her escort, HMS BYMS 2028 (Skr. J.R. Clark, RNR), were in position 165° - Kuriat Lighthouse - 13 miles. They remained within sight of land to obtain a land fix as late as possible.
Between 1730 B/28 and 1930 B/28, innumeralble 'Douglas' transport aircraft passed proceeding on course 100°.
At 1935 B/28, in position 35°40'N, 11°23.5'E, to act as beacon for rehearsal with aircraft of troop carrier command.
At 2000 B/28, HMS Uproar commenced flashing a red light. Aircraft commenced to pass on course 270° and continued passing until 2145 B/28.
At 2300 B/28, HMS Uproar ceased flashing the light and set course to return to Malta together with her escort, HMS BYMS 2028. (7)
29 Aug 1943 Around 1745 B/29, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) ended her 21st war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
HMS BYMS 2028 (Skr. J.R. Clark, RNR) also returned to Malta around the same time. (7)
1 Sep 1943 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted speed trials on the measured mile off Malta. (62)

15 Sep 1943 At 2145 B/15, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 22nd war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the north of Elba Island, Italy and off Bastia, Corsica, France.

She made the passage through the Tunisian War Channel together with HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN) and ORP Dzik (Lt.Cdr. B. Romanowski). They were escorted by HMS BYMS 2028 (Skr. J.R. Clark, RNR).

17 Sep 1943 In a signal timed 1004 B/17, the Capt. S.8 sent the following orders to HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN), HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN), ORP Dzik (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B.S. Romanowski) and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN);

Onward routeing my signal timed 1400 B/16 refers. Tactic to cross latitude 42°N. HMS Unseen in 10°15'E about 0230 B/20 thence to zone 'SR' north of 43°N. HMS Ultor in 10°43'E about 0430 B/20 thence to zone 'SQ' north of 043°N. ORP Dzik in 10°27'E about 1430 B/20 thence to zone 'SQ' south of 43°N. HMS Uproar in 11°01'E about 2130 B/20 thence zone 'SR' south of 43°N. Submarines may withdraw from zone if necessary as follows; HMS Unseen into zone 'SP'. HMS Ultor into zone 'SP' or to westward of zone 'SQ'. ORP Dzik and HMS Uproar to the south of 42°30'N. Submerged bombing restrictions are permanently in force east of 08°E and north of 42°N. (63)

17 Sep 1943 Around 1330 B/17, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN), HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN), ORP Dzik (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B.S. Romanowski) and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN) parted company with HMS BYMS 2028 (Skr. J.R. Clark, RNR).
HMS BYMS 2028 was to return to Malta.

The submarines then proceeded independently to their patrol areas routed as follows; They were to coss latitude 39°01'N in longitudes as follows: HMS Unseen in 10°15' at 0630 B/18. HMS Ultor in 10°43'E at 1200 B/18. ORP Dzik in 10°27'E at 2200 B/18. HMS Uproar in 11°01'E at 0001 B/19. All four submarines were then to proceed on course 360° as far as latitude 42°N. Futher orders would be signalled. (64)

20 Sep 1943 At 2307 B/20, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) received the signal timed 1438 B/20 from the Capt. S.8 which stated that all attack restrictions were lifted and all targets may be attacked.
At 2345 B/20, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1400 B/20 was also received which ordered her to patrol to the north and north-east of Elba Island in the Gulf of Genoa. (7)
21 Sep 1943 At 0315 B/21, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) entered her patrol area.
At 0555 B/21, HMS Uproar dived in position 073° - Cape Enfola - 12 miles and proceeded along the northern coast of Elba eastward keeping three miles from the shore.

At 1310 B/21, she carried out a dummy attack on three Siebel Ferries which were proceeding westwards on course 270° at a speed of 9 knots. They passed at a range of 1000 yards. As it was the first day in the patrol area it was decided not to attack them as all three were empty and owing to the fact that the Germans were evacuating Corsica it was anticipated that many more such targets would be seen and could be attacked later if no merchant ships had been seen.
These were most probably the Germans SF 110, SF 172 and KSF 176 which arrived at Bastia early in the evening.
At 1415 B/21, Six F boats were seen to pass close inshore. [Their course and speed are not given in the patrol report or the logbook.]
These may well have been the Germans F 360, F 420, F 450, F 587, F 616 and F 749 which arrived at Bastia early in the evening.
At 2053 B/21, HMS Ultor surfaced in position 358° - Cape Enfola - 5 miles. Withdrew 8 miles to the northward to charge. (65)

22 Sep 1943 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the small Italian passenger ship (under German control) Andrea Sgarallino (731 GRT, built 1930) north of Elba Island, Italy in position 42°50'N, 10°21'E. Losses were heavy as there were about 300-330 victims and only 4 or 5 survivors. For more info see this website (in Italian language).
0550 B/22 - Dived in position 352° - Cape Enfola - 5 miles. Closed Portoferraio.
Entered the bay to see if there was any shipping present. Nothing but coal barges and two A/S schooners were seen.
0905 B/22 - Withdrew to seaward.

0928 B/22 - While still in the entrance to the bay, sighted a ship bearing 040°. Started attack. Enemy course was 240°. Later this became 220°. The target was thought to be a naval auxiliary of about 500 tons.
0949 B/22 - Fired three torpedoes from about 2200 yards. The target was hit and sank quickly. Uproar withdrew to seaward.
1036 B/22 - One A/S schooner and one motor torpedo boat appeared off the entrance to the harbour. Decided to patrol the Piombino Channel.
1700 B/22 - Sighted seven F boats proceeding eastwards.

This may have been the following (eight); F 181, F 249, F 424, F 486, F 488, F 554, F 619 and F 703 which had departed Bastia around noon for Piombino. They were loaded with German troops and material which were being evacuated from Corsica to the Italian mainland.
2106 B/22 - Surfaced in position 340° - Cape Vita - 7.5 miles and commenced to patrol 2 miles to the westward of the deep minefields. (65)

23 Sep 1943 At 0550 B/23, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived in position 033° - Cape Enfola - 3 miles. Proceeded to patrol in the Piombino Channel. During the day, numerous Siebel Ferries, F-boats, motor torpedo boats (E-boats), motor minesweepers (R-boats) and Junkers 52 transport aircraft were seen proceeding east and westwards during the day. The majority of these vessels entered Porto Vecchio but a few proceeded north up the coast.
At 0950 B/23, a Siebel Ferry, thought to be no. 174 [could not have been SF 174], opened fire at the periscope. HMS Uproar withdrew to seaward in event of an A/S hunt developing.
At 1229 B/23, with no A/S hunt having developed, it was decided to return to patrol in the Piombino Channel.
At 1755 B/23, retired to the northward to patrol in the same area as last night, outside the deep minefields.
At 2052 B/23, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 304° - Cape Vita - 5.5 miles. (7)

24 Sep 1943 At 0218 B/24, to the north of Elba, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) sighted a merchant vessel escorted by two unknown vessels, possibly E-boats or R-boats, bearing 340°. Commenced attack.
At 0225 B/24, radar range and bearing were 325°, 6000 yards. Enemy course was estimated as 240°, speed 10 knots. Continued to close to attack.
At 0235 B/24, radar range and bearing were 298°, 4500 yards. Lt.Herrick considered firing torpedoes but decided not to do so given the unfavourable 140° track.
At 0239 B/24, course was altered to 220° to try to get ahead of the enemy and attack later.

At 0256 B/24, range and bearing were now 330°, 8100 yards. During the run out another radar plot was commenced, and course and speed estimation were 245°, 12.5 knots. Given the insufficient speed difference between the target and the submarine, Lt. Herrick reluctantly decided to break off the attack as no favourable attack position could be obtained.

The vessel sighted was most probably the German transport San Pedro (former French, 5947 GRT, built 1931) en-route from Livorno (departed 2200 B/23 - 2300 B/23) to Bastia (arrived around 0615 B/24 - 0700 B/24). She was escorted by the motor minesweepers R 200, R 192 and R 162.
At 0340 B/24, HMS Uproar set course to return to the patrol area.
At 0557 B/24, HMS Uproar dived in position 315° - Cape Vita - 6.5 miles and closed the Piombino Channel with view to passing through to investigate Porto Vecchio.
At 1340 B/24, as the sea was now flat calm, Lt. Herrick, decided to withdraw to seaward and patrol in the area of last night sighting.

At 1800 B/24, received an aircraft report of a large transport. The course given in the enemy report would take this transport along the same route as that taken by the ship sighted last night, and should thus pass through Uproar's present position between 1730 B/24 and 1800 B/24. Hopes ran high at 1815 B/24 for an interception of this transport when two aircraft were observed circling on the appropriate bearing, but these turned out to be part of a fighter escort for ten Ju-52 transport aircraft.

At 2052 B/24, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 325° - Cape Enfola - 8 miles and patrolled up and down the route taken by the merchant ship sighted last night. The sea was flat calm but visibility was poor. During the day again many Siebel Ferries and F-boats were seen proceeding to and from Corsica. (66)
25 Sep 1943 At 0549 B/25, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived in position 300° - Cape Vita - 6.5 miles and closed the Piombino Channel. Visibility was poor.

At 0810 B/25, when in position 42°56'N, 10°22'E, sighted a ship bearing 107°. Enemy course was 219°. Commenced attack and ran in at speed. From further estimations of the enemy course, it would appear that the ship had come from the north and was altering round to westward during the attack to take him along the north coast of Elba. If he had been coming from the north he would have been seen considerably earlier. It was therefore thought more probable that he came through the Piombino Channel. The ship was escorted by two motor torpedo boats (E-boats), one either quarter.

At 0832 B/25, HMS Uproar steadied on a 120° track. Own course 180°. Enemy course 240° at 9 knots. Range 4000 yards. It was intended to fire four torpedoes over two ships lengths when the nearest motor torpedo boat turned towards and increased speed. He turned away about 1000 yards short of Uproar.

At 0835 B/25, on returning to the target it was seen that the opportunity to fire had passed so the wheel was put hard over and Uproar steadied on course 190°. While waiting for the D.A. to come on Lt. Herrick began to doubt his estimation of the enemy's course and he later thought the enemy had altered at least 20° away.
At 0837 B/25, Lt. Herrick decided not to fire due to the broad track of 150°. The attack was broken off.

At 0845 B/25, Asdic impulses were picked up coming from one of the E-boats. The target was last seen stern on proceeding into the direction of Portoferraio. The target looked very similar to the German tender Strahl.

Perhaps the target was the German escort vessel SG 11 with the German motor minesweepers R 162 and R 198 which had sailed from Leghorn at 0400 B/25 on their way to Porto Ferraio (Elba) to pick up the German minelayer Elbano Gasperi (former Italian, later renamed Juminda, 742 GRT, built 1928), and escort her to Leghorn where they arrived at 0830 B/26.
At 0915 B/25, Asdic charges were now heard, so withdrew, suspecting that HMS Uproar had been detected.
At 1105 B/25, no A/S activity having developed Lt. Herrick decided to pass through the Piombino Channel. During this passage further Asdic charges were heard, but only F-boats were seen.

At 1335 B/25, HMS Uproar had passed through the Channel and was able to see into Porto Vecchio harbour. There was no shipping except for landing craft in the bay, and only one small vessel was seen behind a wall in the harbour. Course was set to return westwards through the Piombino Channel. During this passage HMS Uproar was put deep by seven westbound F-boats and later again by two small steam vessels. One A/S schooner passed very close astern, but showed no interest in the submarine. Again Asdic charges were heard.

By 1700 B/25, HMS Uproar was well clear of the Piombino Channel, north of Portoferraio, Motor torpedo boats (E-boats) now showed up on the scene. Five of them being seen eastbound and three westbound between Portoferraio and the Piombino Channel, stopping and circling off Portoferraio. HMS Uproar withdrew to the northward to patrol in the same area as last night.

2050 B/25, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 286° - Cape Vita - 8 miles and commenced to patrol along the route of the merchant ship seen on 24 September. The night was very dark with poor visibility. The sea was calm.
At 2126 B/25, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1438 B/25 was received which ordered HMS Uproar to patrol in zone 'S.Q.' south of 43°N, as of 1900 B/27.
During the day a total of 27 F-boats and 11 E-boats (and / or R-boats) had been sighted. (7)

26 Sep 1943 At 0336 A/26 [Clocks had been retarded one hour at 0001 hours], in position 316° - Cape Vita - 9.5 miles, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived for a motor torpedo boat (E-boat) seen stopped half a mile away. Avoiding action was taken but no HE was heard and no hunt developed. Lt. Herrick decided to remain dived.
At 0708 A/26, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 0504 A/26 was received which ordered HMS Uproar to switch patrol area forthwith. Course was set to patrol off Bastia, Corsica.
At 1045 A/26, two Wellington aircraft were seen circling over some F-boats. Each Wellington dropped a smoke float amongst the F-boats.
At 1500 A/26, HMS Uproar passed out of area 'SR' and entered area 'SQ'.
At 1950 A/26, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 061° - Bastia - 8.5 miles. The night was very dark. Patrolled near position 050° - Basta - 11 miles during the night.
During the day, three Siebel Ferries, 47 F-boats and 14 E-boats (and / or R-boats) had been sighted. (7)

27 Sep 1943 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) torpedoed and further damaged the German tanker Champagne (former French, 9946 GRT, built 1938). The German tanker was grounded off Bastia, Corsica, France after being torpedoed on the 24th by HMS Ultor. The torpedo from HMS Uproar hit the engine room and left the Germans no choice then to abandon their salvage attempt.

Late in the evening she attacked a merchant vessel with three torpedoes but no hits were obtained. This was most probably the German transport San Pedro (former French, 5947 GRT, built 1931) who had sailed from Bastia for Livorno escorted by four R-boats. These were R 200, R 198, R 192 and R 162.
0127 A/27 - Obtained a radar echo bearing 215° at 8000 yards.
0128 A/27 - Sighted a ship. Commenced attack.

0131 A/27 - Radar reported two more echoes, one on either side of the darkened ship. The night being very dark, and the target being against the land, it was was almost impossible to estimate the angle on the bow. Gave the enemy a probable course of 050°. Speed and radar plot on this course gave a speed of 24 knots. One E-boat (or R-boat) was seen ahead of the contact with high stern wave and gave the impression of having increased speed.

0135 A/27 - Radar now reported that the E-boat was rapidly closing. Contact was lost at 600 yards. The second E-boat was not seen. Lt. Herrick considered that HMS Uproar had either been seen against the light horizon or detected so HMS Uproar, not being in any firing position, dived. After diving it was however soon apparent that the submarines presence was unknown, as all HE drew ahead and eventually faded.

The ship sighted was most likely the German transport KT 14 (834 GRT, built 1943) which had departed Bastia around 0020 B/27 [note the one hour time difference compared to the time kept on board Uproar.] escorted by the motor minesweepers R 200, R 192 and R 162. This convoy arrived at Livorno around 0845 B/27.
0225 A/27 - Surfaced and patrolled in near the same area (059° - Bastia - 10 miles).
0450 A/27 - Dived in position 235° - Bastia - 10 miles.
0703 A/27 - Sighted a merchant vessel south of Bastia, close inshore. Commenced attack but soon sighted that the ship was beached. Patrolled four miles from the entrance to Bastia harbour.
1200 A/27 - Sighted the wreck of a bomber in the water.

1300 A/27 - The Officer of the Watch reported that the beached ship first sighted at 0703 A/27 had now swung round heading 100°. Closed the ship to one mile to ascertain whether at anchor or beached. Either rust mark or cable seen down the bow of the ship. Having 5 torpedoes remaining, Lt. Herrick decided to fire on torpedo at the stern of the ship. If ship already beached, it would become even more so. If it was at anchor, it was considered that a hit in the stern would disable ti until the arrival of Free French forces in Bastia, the fall of which, according to the BBC news bulletins, is imminent. This would leave HMS Uproar with a full salvo of four torpedoes.

1555 A/27 - Manoeuvred to gain position to fire at the ship. A number of Siebel Ferries and F-boats were waiting off the entrance of the harbour, which was the very spot from where Lt. Herrick wished to fire. As they showed no signs of leaving, manoeuvred into a firing position amongst them.

1659 A/27 - In position 42°42'N, 09°28'E fired one torpedo at a tanker that was either at anchor or beached. Range was 2400 yards. The torpedo hit the tanker in the stern. On firing two Siebel Ferries were less than 500 yards away on the quarter. After the torpedo was seen to hit, HMS Uproar went deep to run out under the F-boats and Siebel Ferries.
1730 A/27 - On return to periscope depth the tanker was seen stern down and listing to port. Retired to seaward.
During the day a continual stream of Siebel Ferries, all sorts of landing craft and self propelled barges were seen leaving Bastia, all filled high with war material of all descriptions.

2145 A/27 - Surfaced in position 252° - Bastia - 10.5 miles. Found Uproar to be between two lines of F-boats, one eastbound and one westbound. Retired to the north taken by the ship that had been seen last night.
2215 A/27 - Obtained a radar echo bearing 240°, range 9800 yards.
2219 A/27 - Commenced radar plot. Altered course to the north to run parallel to the target.
2226 A/27 - Altered course to close the target.
2248 A/27 - The plot gave an enemy course of 360°, speed 11 - 12 knots. Range was now 6700 yards.
2256 A/27 - Radar now gave a second echo ahead of the target. Range was now 4500 yards.
2302 A/27 - Sighted the target against the land. It was a merchant ship.
2306 A/27 - Target was now bearing 265°, range was 2400 yards. Radar now reported a third echo just astern of the target.
2308 A/27 - The enemy altered course to 010°. Speed was 11 knots. Commenced attack.

2312 A/27 - In position 43°00'N, 09°35'E fired three torpedoes from 1200 yards. No hits were obtained. Uproar dived after firing. No explosions were heard and no counter attack followed as well. It was thought the target was a merchant vessel of 6000 tons.
[The attack does not appear to have been observed.] (67)

28 Sep 1943 At 0023 A/28, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) received the signal timed 1530 A/27 from the Capt. S.8 which contained the following orders for her; Proceed to operate in zone 'SQ' north 43°N after 2000 A/29. Leave patrol through position 43°01'N, 09°01'E at 2000 A/30, 42°01'N, 06°50'E at 2200 A/1, thence proceed on surface at discretion 5 miles to eastward of the route given to HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN). Make rendezvous with the escort into Algiers in position 37°08'N, 03° 20'E at 1600 A/3. [The route given to HMS Unseen was the following; through positions 40°01'N, 05°33'E and 38°00'N, 03°36'E.]
At 0030 A/28, HMS Uproar surfaced and patrolled in the same position.
At 0219 A/28, HMS Uproar passed her signal timed 0134 A/28 in which she reported that she had only one torpedo left.
At 0450 A/28, the Capt. S.8's signal timed 0344 A/28 was received which contained the following orders; Leave patrol. Advance all times as given in my signal timed 1530 A/27 by 24 hours.

At 0459 A/28, HMS Uproar dived in position 040° - Bastia - 10 miles. Closed Bastia to inspect the damage done to the anchored merchant ship (tanker) fired at yesterday. She was well down by the stern with a heavy list to port with her stern obviously on the bottom, still pointing in the same direction as yesterday. It was also observed that one merchant ship seen yesterday in the harbour had disappeared and it was considered possible that this had been the target of last night's attack. She was similar to the French San Antonio (6013 GRT, built 1930). Having one torpedo left it was hoped to find a fully laden Siebel Ferry. The weather however refused to cooperate fur such an attack, the sea being too rough to set a torpedo to run at 2 feet. Anyway, also no Siebel Ferries were seen. About a dozen F-boats and three trawlers, which arrived at Bastia during the forenoon were seen. In total 38 F-boats were seen on this day.
At 1650 A/28, HMS Uproar retired to the northward to patrol in area 'SQ' north of 43°N as ordered in the Capt. S.8's signal timed 0344 A/28.
At 1950 A/28, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 42° - Bastia - 8 miles.
At 2200 A/28, HMS Uproar entered area 'SQ' north of 43°N and commenced to patrol to the westward of Capraia Island. (68)

29 Sep 1943 At 0454 B/29, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived in position 43°06'N, 09°43'E. Owing to the weather she remained at 80 feet all day while proceeding westwards to leave patrol for Algiers.
At 1953 B/29, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 43°07'N, 09°11'E and continued to passage to Algiers. (69)
2 Oct 1943 Around 1830 A/2, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) ended her 22nd war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean) at Algiers.
She was escorted in by HMS Staffa (T/Lt. R.L. Roat, RNVR) which had joined around 1640 A/2. (7)

8 Oct 1943 Around 1845 A/8, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Algiers for Malta. She made the passage together with HMS BYMS 2074 (T/Lt. I.C. Hughes, RNR), HMS BYMS 2190 (T/Lt. J. McDowell, RNVR), HMS BYMS 2191 (???), HMS BYMS 2212 (T/Lt. R.A.L. Viner, DSC, RNVR) and HMS BYMS 2229 (T/A/Lt.Cdr. G.H. Clark, DSC, RNVR).
For the daily positions of HMS Uproar during this passage see the map below.
11 Oct 1943 Around 1600 A/11, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) arrived at Malta from Algiers.
She is taken in hand for repairs to her generators at the Malta Dockyard. (7)
25 Oct 1943 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) is docked in No.1 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (70)
2 Nov 1943 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) is undocked. (71)
This convoy departed Port Said on 22 November 1943.

This convoy was made up of the following merchant vessels; City of Hongkong (British, 9609 GRT, built 1924), Crista (British, 2590 GRT, built 1938), Hardingham (British, 7269 GRT, built 1942), Mandalay (British, 5529 GRT, built 1911), Recorder (British, 2276 GRT, built 1902), Socotra (British, 7840 GRT, built 1943) and Spero (British, 1589 GRT, built 1922).
On departure from Port Said the convoy was escorted by the corvettes HMS Coltsfoot (T/Lt. G.W. Rayner, RNVR) and HMS La Malouine (Lt. W.A. Ives, RNR).

On 23 November 1943 the following merchant vessels departed Alexandria to join the convoy; Athelviking (British (tanker), 8779 GRT, built 1926), Empire Forest (British, 7025 GRT, built 1942), Fort Capot River (British, 7128 GRT, built 1943), Newbrough (British, 5255 GRT, built 1941), Ocean Trader (British, 7178 GRT, built 1942), Samaritan (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Samaye (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Talma (British, 10000 GRT, built 1923) and Trevelyan (British, 7292 GRT, built 1943).

They were escorted by the frigate HMS Cuckmere (Lt.Cdr. A. Johnson, VRD, RNVR) (S.O. 46th Escort Group) and corvettes HMS Anemone (Lt. J.B. Sparkes, RNR) and HMS Hyderabad (T/Lt. T. Cooper, RNR) which also joined the convoy.

On the 27th the following merchant vessels departed Augusta to join the convoy; Andrew Hamilton (American, 7191 GRT, built 1942), Betty Zane (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Celtic Monarch (British, 5824 GRT, built 1929), George H. Dern (American, 7181 GRT, built 1943), John C. Breckinridge (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Noah Webster (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Patrick Henry (American, 7191 GRT, built 1941), Richmond Mumford Pearson (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942) and Salmon P. Chase (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942). The escort destroyers RHS Kriti (?) and RHS Pindos (?) also joined the convoy coming from Augusta. These two escort destroyers arrived at Algiers on 29 November 1943 having parted company with the convoy having parted company at an unknown time and date. Besides these two escort destroyers the minesweeper HMS Romney (Lt. W.E. Halbert, RNR) was also part of the convoy escort but we have been unable to find out the dates and times.

On the 28th the following merchant vessels arrived at Augusta after having parted company with the convoy; Athelviking, Crista, Empire Forest, Fort Capot River, Newbrough, Ocean Trader, Samaritan, Samaye, Spero, Talma and Trevelyan.

On the 27th the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Malta; Fort Liard (British, 7133 GRT, built 1942) and Gryfevale (British, 4434 GRT, built 1929). Also joining from Malta were the submarine HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) and the corvette HMS Convolvulus (A/Lt.Cdr. R.F.R. Yarde-Buller, RNVR) which joined the escort.

In the morning of the 29th the following merchant vessels were detached to Bizerta; Gryfevale, John C. Breckinridge, Noah Webster, Patrick Henry, Richmond Mumford Pearson and Salmon P. Chase while the following merchant vessels joined coming from Bizerta; Audun (Norwegian, 1304 GRT, built 1925), Benedict (British, 4949 GRT, built 1930), Empire Lightning (British, 6942 GRT, built 1940), Errington Court (British, 4913 GRT, built 1925), Fort Carillon (British, 7129 GRT, built 1943), Fort Fork (British, 7134 GRT, built 1942), Francis Marion (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942)), Shirrabank (British, 7274 GRT, built 1940) and Stanford (British, 5969 GRT, built 1941). Also joining from Bizerta were the AA cruiser HMS Delhi (Capt. A.T.G.C. Peachey, RN) and the landing ship HMS LST 324 (T/A/Lt.Cdr. A.J. Bell, RNR).
At 1400 A/29, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) to proceed on patrol off southern France.

On 29 November 1943, the following merchant vessels Fort Carillon and Shirrabank were detached to Bone while the merchant vessel Empire Copperfield (British, 6013 GRT, built 1943) joined coming from Bone.
On 30 November 1943, the following merchant vessels Empire Copperfield and Fort Fork were detached to Philippeville.
On 30 November 1943, the merchant vessel Benedict was detached to Bougie.

On 30 November 1943, HMS LST 324 was detached to Algiers, while the following ships joined the convoy coming from Algiers; Bactria (British, 2407 GRT, built 1928), Empire Derwent (British, 4026 GRT, built 1930), Empire Gareth (British, 2847 GRT, built 1942), Empire Gold (British (tanker), 8028 GRT, built 1941), Empire Scout (British, 2185 GRT, built 1936), Jade (British, 930 GRT, built 1938), Parame (French, 2337 GRT, built 1918), Robert Jordan (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943) and William Patterson (American, 7191 GRT, built 1942).

On 2 December 1943, the following ships were detached to Oran; Andrew Hamilton, Audun, Betty Zane, Empire Gareth, Errington Court, Francis Marion, George H. Dern, Jade, Parame, Robert Jordan and William Patterson.

The merchant vessel Capitaine Paul Lemerle (French, 4945 GRT, built 1925) joined the convoy coming from Algiers as did the landing ships USS LST 5 (Lt. J.F. Lambden Jr., USNR), USS LST 7 (Lt. F. O'Neil, USN), USS LST 306 (Lt. R.H. Kimball, USNR), USS LST 307 (Lt. J.B. Markham, USNR), USS LST 331 (Lt. J. Mazotta, USCG), USS LST 335 (Lt. H.R. Roberts, USNR), USS LST 336 (Lt. D.L. Biberman, USNR), USS LST 369 (Lt. J.P. O'Riorden, USNR), USS LST 388 (Lt. R.S. Browning, USNR), USS LST 391 (Lt. I. Ehrensall, USNR), USS LST 392 (Lt. L.R. Lemaire, Jr., USNR) and USS LST 393 (Lt. W.C. Knight, USNR).
The corvette HMS La Malouine arrived at Algiers on 2 December 1943 after having parted company with the convoy.

On 3 December 1943, the following merchant vessels arrived at Gibraltar after having detached from the convoy; Bactria, Celtic Monarch, Empire Lightning, Fort Liard and Stanford. Also the current escort, HMS Delhi, HMS Cuckmere, HMS Anemone, HMS Coltsfoot, HMS Convolvulus and HMS Hyderabad were detached to Gibraltar.

From Gibraltar the following merchant vessels joined the convoy; Alresford (British, 2472 GRT, built 1922), Arawa (British, 14462 GRT, built 1922), Ashbury (British, 3901 GRT, built 1924), Blackheath (British, 4637 GRT, built 1936), Clan Cameron (British, 7243 GRT, built 1937), Cochrane (British, 7203 GRT, built 1923), Dalcross (British, 4557 GRT, built 1930), Dalemoor (British, 5835 GRT, built 1922), Empire Wolfe (British, 2888 GRT, built 1941), Explorer (British, 6235 GRT, built 1935), Hartbridge (British, 5080 GRT, built 1927), Iris (British (cable ship), 1479 GRT, built 1940), Itinda (British, 6619 GRT, built 1938), Parklaan (Dutch, 3807 GRT, built 1911) and Porjus (Swedish, 2965 GRT, built 1906). Also joining from Gibraltar were the armed merchant cruiser HMS Ranpura (Capt.(Retd.) H.T.M. Pawsey, RN) and the rescue ship Bury (British, 1686 GRT, built 1911).

A new escort now also joined the convoy. It was made up of the sloops HMS Rochester (Cdr. H.V. King, OBE, DSO, RN) (S.O. 39th Escort Group), HMS Scarborough (Lt.Cdr. E.B. Carnduff, RN), frigate HMS Tavy (T/A/Lt.Cdr. F. Ardern, RNR) and the corvettes HMS Balsam (Lt. J.E.L. Peters, DSC, RNVR), HMS Geranium (T/Lt. A.R.J. Tilston, DSC, SANF(V)) and HMS Mignonette (Lt. H.H. Brown, DSC, RNR).
The convoy was now known as Convoy MKS 32(G).
On 4 December 1943, the convoy merged with convoy SL 141 coming from Freetown. (72)
24 Nov 1943 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted compass adjustment trials at Malta. (71)
25 Nov 1943 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Malta. These included A/S exercises. (71)

27 Nov 1943 Around 0600 A/27, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 23rd war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Southern France. She made the passage through the Tunisian War Channel in convoy MKS 32.
For more info on this convoy see the event convoy MKS 32 for 22 November 1943.
29 Nov 1943 At 1400 A/29, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) detached from convoy MKS 32.

She was to have parted company at 1000 A/29 [the convoy was late] and proceed to patrol in Zone 'SS'. Routed through position 40°05'N, 07°33'E at 0600 A/30 thence diving by day through position 43°12'N, 07°33'E at 0400 A/2.

In a signal timed 1735 A/2, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Uproar; Proceed on surface on the 30th to catch up routeing schedule. Total bombing restrictions arranged to position 40°39'N, 07°33'E. (73)
2 Dec 1943 At 0200 A/2, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) entered her patrol area (area 'SS'. This was off the French coast, apparently between 07°00'E and 08°10'E).
At 0400 A/2, she commenced to patrol 10 miles to the east of Cape d'Antibes. (7)

3 Dec 1943 At 1150 A/3, south-east of Cape d'Antibes, in position 43°32'N, 07°13'E, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) sighted a vessel, thought to be a Spica-class torpedo boat proceeding on course 050° at 9 knots. The enemy vessel passed at a range of 3000 yards.

This was most likely the German patrol vessel SG 15 which was on passage from Marseille (departed 0500 B/3) to Genoa (arrived 1800 B/3). SG 15 was a former French Chamois-class sloop / minesweeper captured by the Germans while still incomplete. (74)

5 Dec 1943 At 1524 A/5, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1227 A/5, which ordered her to patrol in the western part of her patrol area after 0200 A/6. [We have been unable to find the exact text of this signal which also included orders for other submarines.] (7)

6 Dec 1943 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) torpedoed and damaged the German troop transport Virgilio (former Italian hospital ship, 11718 GRT, built 1928) north-east of St. Tropez, southern France. She had been on passage from La Spezia (departed 2000 B/5) to Marseille escorted by the German patrol vessel SG 15.

Virgilio was hit in the stern and her rudder was disabled. Initially towed by SG 15, she dropped anchor in the Gulf of Fréjus. UJ 2210, UJ 2221, UJ 6076 (some sources refer to her as M 6076), M 6020, M 6027 and the tugs Faron, Thésée and Cépet were ordered to her assistance. But the bad weather forced some of them to turn back. Finally UJ 6076 towed her to Toulon (arrived in the morning of 9 December), screened by SG 15, UJ 2210 and UJ 2221. Virgilio was declared a constructive total loss and not repaired.
0750 A/6 - Sighted masts and funnel of a merchant ship to seaward escorted by one destroyer.
0751 A/6 - Commenced attack. Ran in for 13 minutes.
0807 A/6 - It was still difficult to estimate the range but it was thought to be 7000 yards. Enemy course was estimated to be 200° at a speed of 13 knots.

0810 A/6 - In position 43°23'N, 06°54'E fired four torpedoes at the merchant vessel. The target was a modern merchant vessel of about 6000 tons. It was thought to be the Virgilio. One explosion was heard 5 minutes and 53 seconds after firing this gave a range of 7500 to 8000 yards. HE ceased after the torpedo explosion. Uproar retired to seaward.
0828 A/6 - The 1st of 21 depth charges was dropped. All were dropped at random and none caused damage.
0850 A/6 - The merchant ship was heard starting and stopping its engines.
0855 A/6 - Went deep and altered course to the West again to close the target again for another attack.
1022 A/6 - The last depth charge was dropped.

1040 A/6 - HE of the target was getting louder. Retuned to periscope depth, the target was now about 1 nautical mile away. Uproar tried to get into a favourable attack position but in this she had not succeeded when visibility decreased and the target was lost out of sight about an hour later. (74)
7 Dec 1943 At 1310 A/7, south of Cannes, in approximate position 43°27'N, 07°04'E, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) sighted a trawler proceeding eastbound.

[We have so far been unable to positively identify this vessel. The German Netztender 11 and Netztender 44 should have proceeded from St. Raphael to Nizza (Nice) on this day, perhaps it was one of them.] (75)

8 Dec 1943 At 0820 A/8, when to the east of Cape d'Antibes in position 43°32'N, 07°16'E, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) sighted a convoy proceeding westwards close inshore. The convoy appeared to be made up of four small vessels escorted by an UJ boat and two aircraft. The biggest vessel was though to be not greater then about 800 tons.
No attack was made on such small vessels to not give away the submarines position as it was expected HMS Uproar was to spent a few more days in this area.

The convoy sighted was most likely the one made up of the transport Gonneville (German, 1724 GRT, built 1913), tanker Athylene (former French Éthylène, 1000 (?) GRT, built 1938) and Netztender 11. They were escorted by the auxiliary minesweepers M 6021, M 6023 and M 6025. The convoy had departed Nizza (Nice) at 0700 B/8 for Toulon where it arrived around 2015 B/8. (76)

9 Dec 1943 At 0830 A/9, when to the east of Cape d'Antibes in approximate position 43°21'N, 06°53'E, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) sighted a minesweeper leaving the Gulf of Frejus proceeding eastward keeping close inshore.
This may have been the German Netztender 43 which departed St. Raphael at 0830 B/9 for Nizza (Nice) where she arrived around 1400 B/9.

At 1430 A/9, An UJ boat and what is thought to be a former Italian Gabbiano-class corvette passed along the coast carrying out an A/S sweep. They passed at a range of about 1 to 1.5 miles. Enemy course was 040°.

This must have been the German auxiliary submarine chasers UJ 2210 and UJ 2221 [this was indeed a former Gabbiano-class corvette] proceeding from Toulon (departed 0845 B/9) to Genoa conducting an A/S sweep en-route. They entered Nizza (Nice) at 1640 B/9. They were to continue their passage east the following day. (76)

11 Dec 1943 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) attacked the Italian (in German service) transport Anagni (former French El-Mansour, 5818 GRT, built 1933) with four torpedoes about 10 nautical miles south-east of Cannes, France in position 43°26'N, 07°14'E. No hits were obtained. Anagni was escorted by the German patrol vessel SG 15 and the German motor minesweeper R 215. They were on passage from Genoa (departed 2145 B/10) to Marseille (arrived 1130 B/11).

The Germans did not sighted the torpedoes but two explosions were heard near the target and more much farther away which were probably torpedoes at the end of their run. SG 15 dropped six depth charges.
0413 A/11 - Sighted a darkened ship bearing 088°. Range was 5 to 6 nautical miles. Started attack.
0422 A/11 - Forced to dive as the moon broke through the clouds. Uproar was directly up moon from the target. Before diving a small escort was seen to the right of the target.
0426 A/11 - Sighted the target through the periscope.
0440 A/11 - In position 43°26'N, 07°14'E fired four torpedoes from a range 4000 to 5000 yards. It was thought that the target was a merchant vessel of about 3000 tons. No hits were obtained.

0452 A/11 - The first of eight depth charges was dropped. All were distant. Returned to periscope depth but nothing was in sight. Uproar then withdrew to seaward and to leave patrol as all torpedoes were expended.

1240 A/11 - Surfaced to transmit HMS Uproar's signal timed 1003 A/11 which contained the following; No recall received. All torpedoes expended. One hit on 6000 ton merchant ship 6th. Will pass 43°10'N, 07°45'E at 2000 A/11 then course 180° diving by day through position 43°23'N, 07°00'E (???) by day.
1305 B/11 - Dived upon completion of transmitting the signal.

At 1928 A/11 - the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1635 A/11 was received which gave the online route to La Maddalena; Yuor 1003 A/11 acknowledged. Proceed diving by day through position 43°10'N, 07°43'E'N and 42°09'N, 07°48'E to make rendezvous with a minesweeper in position 240° - Monachi Rock - 5 miles at 1200 A/13. (77)
13 Dec 1943 Around 1700 A/13, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) ended her 23th war patrol (18rd in the Mediterranean) at La Maddalena, Italy.
She had made rendezvous with the escort (unidentified) at 1200 A/13. (7)

24 Dec 1943 Around 1350 A/24, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed La Maddalena for her 24th war patrol (19th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Genoa and later off Southern France.
Routed Q.B.B. 301 thence through position 42°07'N, 08°08'E at 0001 A/24 and 45°49'N, 08°29'E at 0001 A/25 to patrol in zone 'SN', proceeding to submerge by day, except in Q.B.B. 301.
At 1915 A/24, a darkened convoy was seen leaving Ajaccio. HMS Uproar altered stern on to let the convoy pass.
At 1948 A/24, identities were exchanged with a French destroyer which had turned towards and was closing. [Most likely this was either Basque or the L'Alcyon.]
25 Dec 1943 At 2230 A/25, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) entered her patrol area (area 'SN'). (7)
27 Dec 1943 At 1246 A/27, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) received the signal timed 0841 A/27 from the Capt. S.10 which informed her that area 'SO' was now to be included in her patrol area.
At 1633 A/27, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1448 A/27 was received which cancelled the signal timed 0841 A/27 and ordered HMS Uproar to patrol in area 'SS'.
[We have been unable to find the text of these signals.] (7)

28 Dec 1943 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) attacked and damaged the Italian transport (in German control) Chisone (6168 GRT, built 1922) about 8 nautical miles east of the Gulf of Saint Tropez, France in position 43°16'N, 06°51'E. She was on passage from Genoa (departed around 1900 B/27) to Marseille, escorted by the German auxiliary submarine chaser UJ 2220, German patrol vessel SG 15 and the German motor minesweeper R 215.

Chisone was hit by one torpedo. She was towed to Toulon by SG 15 initially escorted by the other two escorts. They arrived at Toulon around 0015 B/29. R 215 had proceeded ahead and had already arrived at 1835 A/28. UJ 2220 claimed to have sunk the attacking submarine.
0625 A/28 - Dived in position 155° - Cape Drammont - 10.5 miles.
0840 A/28 - Heard HE and A/S transmissions. Closed to investigate.
0904 A/28 - Started attack on a 3000 tons motor vessel escorted by a 'destroyer' and an UJ boat. Range was 6500 yards.
0925 A/28 - In position 43°16'N, 06°51'E fired four torpedoes from 3000 to 4000 yards. All torpedoes missed. Uproar took avoiding action on firing and went deep.
0947 A/28 - The first of 25 very distant depth charges was dropped. A/S transmissions were no longer heard and HE was fading.
1000 A/28 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight.
1030 A/28 - Heard a pattern of six depth charges exploding.

1423 A/28 - Received the C-in-C Mediterranean's signal timed 1302 A/28 which stated that a 800 tons ship was reported near the position of the attack. Lt. Herrick considered this to be an UJ boat hunting in the area.

1530 A/28 - Sighted the mast of a ship and a seaplane circling on the bearing of the attack position. Both later disappeared to the southwest. UJ 2220 had remained behind to hunt the submarine and this was most likely her.
1900 A/28 - Surfaced in position 114° - Cape Drammont - 6.2 miles. (78)
30 Dec 1943 At 1000 A/30, when in approximate position 43°41'N, 07°32'E, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) sighted five Siebel Ferries proceeding westwards close inshore.

These were SF 113, SF 179, SF 190, SF 224 and SF 231 which had departed Genoa around 0700 A/29 for Toulon. They had paused at Imperia for the night had had continued their passage that morning. They arrived at Cannes at 1520 B/30 to pause for the night and continue their passage the following morning. (79)

31 Dec 1943 At 2226 A/31, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1816 A/31 which recommenced to patrol 7 miles to the east of Cape Drammont after dawn on 1 January. Nothing however was sighted on 1 and 2 January while patrolling in that vicinity. (7)

3 Jan 1944 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) attacked an enemy transport in a convoy about 10 nautical miles east of the Gulf of Saint Tropez, France. Four torpedoes were fired but no hits were obtained.

This was most probably the convoy made up of the German transports Avezzano (former Italian, former French Gouverneur Général Grévy, 4565 GRT, built 1922) and Siena (former Italian, former French Astrée, 2147 GRT, built 1921) escorted by the German patrol vessel SG 15 and the German motor minesweeper R 215 on passage from Genoa (sailed at 1200 A/2) to Marseille.
[The attack appears to have been unobserved.]
0330 A/3 - Heard A/S transmissions bearing 100°.
0338 A/3 - Sighted a darkened ship bearing 100°.
0340 A/3 - Obtained a radar contact bearing 090°. Range 9800 yards. The echo was however soon lost.
0350 A/3 - Regained radar contact bearing 080°, range 5500 yards. Commenced radar plot.
0416 A/3 - The target was observed to alter course. Started attack.
0452 A/3 - Now closed to attack. Five ships were now visible. The first ship sighted was the largest one and was chosen as the target.

0507 A/3 - In position 43°16'N, 06°56'E fired 4 torpedoes from 2200 yards. No hits were obtained. The convoy attack consisted of the target which was a 3000 tons merchant vessel, another smaller merchant vessel, a small tanker and two 'destroyers' were the escorts.
0510 A/3 - Dived and took avoiding action. Three depth charges were dropped following this attack but these were not close. Uproar meanwhile retired to seaward.

0914 A/3 - Surfaced to pass Uproar's signal timed 0810 A/3 informing the French submarine Aréthuse off this westbound convoy. Submerged at 0930 A/3 after having passed the signal. [Aréthuse was to patrol in area 'SU' but had been ordered to patrol in area 'SW' instead on 1 January, this was off Cape Couronne, west of Marseille.]
1930 A/3 - Surfaced in position 157° - Cape d'Antibes - 14 miles and left patrol to return to La Maddalena. (80)
5 Jan 1944 At 1245 A/5, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) ended her 24th war patrol (19th in the Mediterranean) at La Maddalena, Italy. (7)

13 Jan 1944 At 1500 A/13, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN) departed La Maddalena for Naples. They were escorted by HMML 121 (T/Lt. E.J. Bower, RNVR), HMML 134 (T/Lt. J.N. Barfield, SANF(V)) and HMML 558 (T/Lt. P.I. Fellows, RNVR). (81)
15 Jan 1944 Around 0930 A/15, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) arrived at Naples from La Maddalena. (81)

17 Jan 1944 Around 0900 A/17, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Naples for Salerno Bay. She was to exercise there for her beacon role during the upcoming Anzio landings (Operation Shingle). HMS Uproar was escorted by HMS Tetcott (Lt. A.F. Harkness, DSC, OBE, RD, RNR).
1430 A/17 - Parted company with the escort.
1434 A/17 - Dived in position 40°32'N, 14°50'E and closed the coast to identify the landmarks.
1750 A/17 - Surfaced in position 40°31'N, 14°48'E and from 1800 A/17 flashed the letter 'Baker' every two minutes between bearings 214° and 274°.
1830 A/17 - Other beach marking vessels passed and took up position inshore of HMS Uproar.
1930 A/17 - Ceased flashing the letter 'Baker' and proceeded to the south-west, now escorted by RHS Kriti (?), to keep clear of the approach route to the beach.
0200 A/18 - Set course for Capri. (81)
18 Jan 1944 At 0755 A/18, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) arrived at Capri from exercises. (81)

19 Jan 1944 Around 0730 A/19, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN) departed Capri together for Naples. They were escorted by HMML 560 (T/Lt. R. Edelsten-Pope, RANVR). They arrived at Naples around 1030 A/19. (81)

20 Jan 1944 At 1350 A/20, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Naples for her 25th war patrol (20th in the Mediterranean). She was to perform a special operation. She was to act as a beacon during the Anzio landings (Operation Shingle).
She departed Naples in company with HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN). The submarines were escorted by HMS Coverley (T/A/Lt.Cdr. L.R. Lord, RNVR).
The submarines parted company with the escort at 1730 A/20 and proceeded independently to carry out their patrol orders. (81)

21 Jan 1944 During the evening HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) successfully carries out her beacon duties during the Allied landings off Anzio. Early the next morning she departed the area for Capri.
0609 A/21 - Dived in position 41°10'N, 12°38'E and closed the port of Anzio to identify the land marks.
P.M./21 = Patrolled near position 'Baker' (41°12'15"N, 14°39'12"E).
1733 A/21 - Obtained the last fix before darkness.
1800 A/21 - Surfaced in position 158° - Cape d'Anzio - 4.6 miles.
1815 A/21 - Uproar was now in position 'Baker'. Stopped and commenced flashing the letter 'Baker' every two minutes between bearings 174° - 264°.

1820 A/21 - Ships of the Beach Identification Group were sighted. These ships subsequently passed Uproar and took up positions closer inshore. Whilst flashing the letter 'Baker' a little gunfire was heard coming from the shore.

1930 A/21 - Ceased flashing the light and set course for a position ten miles to the southward clear of the approach routes to the assault beaches. Uproar was now escorted by RHS Kriti (?). Remained in the position to the south of the landing area until 0450 A/22.
2346 A/21 - Heard very loud and heavy gunfire to the northward. (81)
22 Jan 1944 At 0450 A/22, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) and her escort, RHS Kriti (?), set course for Capri.
Around 1500 A/22, HMS Uproar ended her 25th war patrol (20th in the Mediterranean) at Capri. (81)

25 Jan 1944 At 0945 A/25, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) and HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN) departed Capri for Naples. They were escorted by HMML 560 (T/Lt. R. Edelsten-Pope, RANVR). They arrived at Naples around 1200 A/25. (82)

27 Jan 1944 At 0630 A/27, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Naples together with HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN). They were escorted by HMML 560 (T/Lt. R. Edelsten-Pope, RANVR). They arrived at Capri at 0900 A/27.
At 1700 A/27, HMS Uproar and HMS Ultor departed Capri for La Maddalena. They were escorted by HMS Hoy (T/Lt. G.H. McNair, MBE, RNVR). (81)
29 Jan 1944 At 1130 A/29, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) arrived at La Maddalena from Capri. (81)
5 Feb 1944 At 1230 A/5, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed La Maddalena for her 26th war patrol (21st in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off southern France, in zone 'ST'.
HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN) was in company on departure. The two submarines parted company at 1830 A/5.
6 Feb 1944 At 2130 A/6, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) entered her patrol area, zone 'ST'. (7)
7 Feb 1944 At 0600 A/7, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived in position 160° - Cape Camarat - 8.5 miles.
At 1030 A/7, two drifters were seen proceeding eastwards. They were close inshore.
At 1922 A/7, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 167° - Cape Camarat - 5.3 miles. During the night she patrolled about 5 miles to the south-east of Cape Camarat. (7)

8 Feb 1944 Near Cape Camarat, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) tried to attack an enemy convoy. As the submarine closed, gunfire was observed, the submarine dived but subsequently lost contact with the enemy.

This must have been the German auxiliary minelayer Niedersachsen (1794 GRT, bult 1934, former French Guyana) which was en-route to Toulon from coming Genoa with cable laying vessel Giasone (600 tons, built 1941).

0130 A/8 - Sighted two small vessels off Cape Camarat moving slowly to the southward. This were possibly E-boats (motor torpedo boats) on patrol. The sea was calm, there was a full moon and a cloudy sky.
0334 A/8 - Heard and felt two loud explosions. [This must have been HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN) making an attack.]
0335 A/8 - Two more loud explosions were heard which sounded like depth charges.
0336 A/8 - Sighted a darkened ship bearing 030°. Shortly afterwards a second ship was sighted.
0356 A/8 - Heard an aircraft passing overhead.

0400 A/8 - Obtained a radar contact at a range of 6400 yards. The plot gave an enemy course of 237° at 7 knots. Closed to attack. The target was seen to be a medium sized merchant vessel. The other vessel could not be seen closely but it looked like an escort vessel, former Italian Orsa-class. This vessel was heard to be transmitting on Asdics. The escort was astern of the merchant ship.

0409 A/8 - Bearing of the enemy was now 315°. Range was 5100 yards. Flashes from a medium sized gun, thought to be 4", were now seen on this bearing. These were followed by red and white tracers which crossed ahead of HMS Ultor.
0410 A/8 - Dived in position 140° - Cape Camarat - 6 miles to avoid being hit by the gunfire. No A/S hunt followed though.
0425 A/8 - Surfaced to give chase. Nothing was seen and no radar contact was obtained. HE and Asdic transmissions were last heard on bearing 295° just before surfacing.
0455 A/8 - Resumed patrol to the south-east of Cape Camarat.
0620 A/8 - Dived in position 125° - Cape Camarat - 4.5 miles.
2112 A/8 - Surfaced in position 112° - Cape Camarat - 7 miles. Nothing further had been seen all day. HMS Uproar had to hove too all night as a north-westerly gale had blew up in the meantime. (7)
9 Feb 1944 At 0230 A/9, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived in position 150° - Cape Camarat - 11 miles and patrolled to the south of Cavalaire Bay.
At 1900 A/9, a loud explosion was heard followed by heavy rumbling.
At 1920 A/9, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 170° - Cape Camarat - 5 miles.
At 2020 A/9, a red glow was seen on the horizon bearing 040°. It was thought HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN) had found a target but this was not the case. (7)

10 Feb 1944 At 0630 A/10, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived in position 125° - Cape Camarat - 4.5 miles. It appeared that during the night enemy aircraft were conducting A/S patrols in the area.
At 1830 A/10, several loud explosions were heard.
At 2105 A/10, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 145° - Cape Camarat - 7.5 miles. She had to hove too to the south-east of Cape Camarat until 0200 A/11 due to the bad weather conditions. (7)
12 Feb 1944 At 0629 A/12, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived in position 111° - Cape Camarat - 4 miles.

At 1432 A/12, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1015 A/12 was received which stated the following; Uproar is to leave patrol to pass through positions 42°47'N, 07°03'E at 0800 A/15, 42°30'N, 07°36'E at 1800 A15 thence by Q.B.B. 312 to arrive at La Maddalena at 1201 A/16.
At 1920 A/12, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 200° - Cape Camarat - 5.5 miles and patrolled 4 to 5 miles to the south and east of Cape Camarat during the night. (83)
13 Feb 1944 At 0330 A/13, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) set course to the west to patrol to the south of the Hyeres Islands.
At 0626 A/13 - HMS Uproar dived in position 185° - Titan Lighthouse (Levent Island) - 5.5 miles.
At 1929 A/13 - HMS Uproar surfaced in position 185° - Cape d'Armes (Hyères Island) - 3.75 miles.
At 2100 A/13 - Course was set to return to the Cape Camarat area. (7)
15 Feb 1944 At 0330 A/15, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) left patrol to return to La Maddalena.

At 0440 A/15, the lighthouse on Cape Camarat commenced to burn. This was a fair indication that enemy shipping might pass through the area within the next six hours so it was decided to return to the patrol area. Provided that HMS Uproar would leave the patrol area at 1000 A/15 at latest her time of arrival at La Maddalena would not be affected.
At 0530 A/15, HMS Uproar patrolled 4 to 5 miles to the east of Cape Camarat.
At 0624 A/15, HMS Uproar dived in position 063° - Cape Camarat - 4.5 miles.

At 0830 A/15, HE was heard on bearing 135°. Commenced to close to attack. It was thought a mast was sighted but after several looks it was thought it may be the periscope of HMS Ultor (Lt. G.E. Hunt, DSC, RN).
At 0859 A/15, the attack was broken off and S.S./T. contact was gained with HMS Ultor.
At 1000 A/15, HMS Uproar left patrol once more to return to La Maddalena. (7)
16 Feb 1944 Around 1115 A/16, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) ended her 26th war patrol (21st in the Mediterranean) at La Maddalena. (7)

26 Feb 1944 At 1300 A/26, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed La Maddalena for her 27th war patrol (22nd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off southern France in area 'SU'. She was routed as follows; Q.B.B. 312 thence through positions 42°08'N, 07°36'E at 0400 A/27 and 42°29'N, 06°01'E at 2358 A/27.
Before departure compass adjustment trials were carried out.
27 Feb 1944 At 1830 A/27, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) entered her patrol area, area 'SU'. (7)

28 Feb 1944 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) sighted what was thought to be a small enemy convoy near Hyères Island and an attack was started but this was broken off when it was thought the enemy vessels sighted were an A/S hunting group.

In fact the vessels sighted were the German auxiliary minelayer ML 6061 which was dropping 'sprengbojen' (explosive floats) in the area to prevent Allied minesweepers from sweeping the German minefields easily. Sprengbojen (explosive floats) were a mine like device with a small explosive charge which were to destroy the minesweeping gear of minesweepers. She was escorted by the auxiliary submarine chaser UJ 6075 and the auxiliary minesweepers M 6002, M 6022 and M 6023. They had departed Toulon around 0330 B/28 and returned around 1145 B/28.
0600 A/28 - Dived in position 241° - Cape d'Armes lighthouse (Hyères Island) - 4 miles.
0610 A/28 - Heard Asdic transmissions on bearing 055°. It was still too dark to see something through the periscope. Transmissions were weak.

0700 A/28 - The transmissions faded on bearing 065°. By now it was light enough but visibility was poor and nothing was seen. A gale blew up from the south with heavy rain squall making periscope watch very difficult.

1012 A/28 - Asdic transmissions were heard and four vessels were seen coming through Petit Passage. Commenced attack on what appeared to be a small coaster escorted by a trawler, an UJ-boat and an R-boat. Only the UJ-boat was transmitting on Asdic.
1036 A/28 - Broke off the attack as it was now considered that this was an A/S group carrying out an A/S sweep. The coaster was thought to be another UJ-boat. All ships later entered Toulon.
2124 A/28 - Surfaced in position 240° - Cape d'Armes lighthouse - 9 miles. Withdrew to seaward as a night surface attack would not be possible in the current weather conditions. (84)

29 Feb 1944 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the German transports Artésien (Former French, 3152 GRT, built 1921) off Toulon, southern France in position 43°03'N, 05°59'E. She had been on passage from Marseille to Toulon escorted by the auxiliary minesweepers M 6041, M 6043 and M 6044.

Artésien sank in two minutes after having sustained two torpedo hits. Twenty-two were killed (three Germans, including her captain, and nineteen Frenchmen), eighteen were rescued. One torpedo passed under M 6041 which was drawing only 2.5 metres.
Earlier HMS Uproar had spotted a U-boat and attempted to attack it but this failed due to the course changes of the enemy. This U-boat then entered Toulon.
This was U-371 which made a trial dive off Toulon.
0528 A/29 - Dived in position 185° - Cape Sicié - 7 miles.
0810 A/29 - Heard fast HE from the direction of Toulon. This HE moved across the entrance to the harbour and back but nothing was seen. This HE was heard again several times during the day.

1350 A/29 - Sighted the conning tower of a U-boat in position 43°02'30"N, 06°01'E. On sighting this U-boat bore Red 30° with Uproar already about 150° to 160° on his starboard bow. Range was 2000 yards. An attack was attempted but due to the frequent course changes of the enemy this failed.
1410 A/29 - The Officer Of the Watch sighted masts and a funnel off Cap Sicié. If an attack was to be made it had to be done before the ship rounded Cap Cépet to enter Toulon.
1430 A/29 - The target was seen to be a merchant vessel of 3000 yards. A 'chasseur' was seen to be escorting the ship and two seaplanes were patrolling the area. Started attack.
1440 A/29 - In position 43°03'N, 05°59'E fired four torpedoes from 2000 to 3000 yards. Four explosions were heard of which at least one was thought to be a hit on the target.
1444 A/29 - The first of three distant depth charges was dropped.
1523 A/29 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight except the two seaplanes.
1950 A/29 - Surfaced in position 150° - Cape Sicié - 11.5 miles.

2346 A/29 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1913 A/29 which contained the following orders HMS Universal (Lt. C. Gordon, RN) and HMS Uproar are to exchange patrol areas, both submarines crossing 06°10'E at 0030 A/2. HMS Universal is to keep south and HMS Uproar north of 42°40'N from 2330 A/1 until 0300 A/2. (85)
1 Mar 1944 At 0521 A/1, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived in position 134° - Cape Sicié - 5 miles.

At 1045 A/1, a trawler and an R-boat were seen leaving Toulon. The trawler was transmitting on Asdic. They conducted an A/S sweep half the way between Toulon and the Petit Pass, ran south for a mile and back to Toulon. They passed HMS Uproar at a range of 1800 yards but gained no contact.

These were probably vessels out of the following: The auxiliary submarine chaser UJ 6075 and the auxiliary minesweepers M 6022, M 6043 and M 6044 had left Toulon around 1015 B/1 for an A/S sweep but they returned around 1330 B/1 due to the bad weather conditions. [Note the one hour time difference !]
At 1650 A/1, two A/S vessels, two trawlers, were seen coming through Petit Pass. Four seaplanes were patrolling overhead. No Asdic transmissions were picked up. They subsequently entered Toulon.

These were probably vessels out of the following: The net tender Netztender 43 had departed Toulon around 0640 B/1. She was escorted by the auxiliary minesweepers M 6012, M 6023 and M 6041. They returned around 1800 B/1 after Netztender 43 had completed her tasks. [Note the one hour time difference !]
At 1840 A/1, HMS Uproar retired to seaward.

At 2009 A/1, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 125° - Cape Sicié - 7 miles. Shortly before HE had been picked up passing from the direction of the Petit Pass towards Toulon but nothing was sighted. (86)
2 Mar 1944 At 0030 A/2, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) left area 'SU' to proceed to area 'ST' in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1913 A/29.
At 0555 A/2, HMS Uproar dived in position 086° - Cape St. Tropez - 5.5 miles.
At 1948 A/2, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 105° - Cape St. Tropez - 5 miles. She patrolled about 7 miles to the east of Cape St. Tropez during the night. (7)

3 Mar 1944 At 0203 A/3, to the east of Cape St. Tropez, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) sighted a darkened ship bearing 335°. HMS Uproar closed to pick up the target with the radar. Contact was made at 3800 yards. By that time several more ships had been seen which had begun to signal to each other and to the signal station on Cape Camarat. The ships were moving southwards past Cape St. Tropez at at speed of 5 knots.
