HMS Uproar closed to 1800 yards and the targets were seen to be five F-lighters with something a little larger, probably a Siebel Ferry.
At 0250 A/3, Lt. Herrick decided not to attack as these targets were not suitable torpedo targets and certainly not at night. Besides that, they were also westbound and probably empty.
At 0300 A/3, HMS Uproar resumed her patrol about 7 miles to the east of Cape St. Tropez.
At 0548 A/3, HMS Uproar dived in position 068° - Cape St. Tropez - 7 miles and set course to patrol about four miles to the south-east of Cape St. Tropez.
At 1233 A/3, two vessels, thought to be a trawler and and R-boat, were seen leaving the Gulf of St. Tropez. The proceeded southwards close inshore and rounded Cape Camarat.
These were most probably the auxiliary minesweepers M 6025 and M 6031 which had left St. Tropez shortly after 1200 B/3 for Toulon where they arrived around 1900 B/3.
In the afternoon the harbour of St. Tropez was closed but no shipping was seen in the harbour.
At 1956 A/3, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 077° - Cape St. Tropez - 7.5 miles. Uproar patrolled 7 miles to the east of Cape St. Tropez during the night. (86)
6 Mar 1944 At 0548 A/6, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived in position 081° - Cape Camarat - 5 miles.
At 0810 A/6, a trawler and two drifters were seen passing southwards.
This must have been the German auxiliary minesweepers M 6009, M 6024 and M 6027 which had departed St. Tropez around 0640 B/6 for Toulon where they arrived around 1350 B/6.
At 1948 A/6, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 078° - Cape St. Tropez - 6 miles and commenced to patrol 6 miles to the east of Cape St. Tropez.
At 2025 A/6, two F-lighters escorted by a trawler were seen to leave the Gulf of St. Tropez. They proceeded to the north (and east) keeping close inshore. They were not attacked.

At 2156 A/6, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0957 A/6 was received which contained the following orders; Leave patrol to pass through position 42°32'N, 07°03'E at 0800 A/8 to arrive at La Maddalena at 1130 A/9. (86)
8 Mar 1944 At 0030 A/8, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) set course to return to La Maddalena.
At 0400 A/8, she passed out of area 'ST'. (7)
9 Mar 1944 At 1030 A/9, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) ended her 27th war patrol (22nd in the Mediterranean) at La Maddalena. (7)

21 Mar 1944 Around 1400 A/21, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed La Maddalena for her 28th war patrol (23rd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Genoa along the Italian Riviera.
She was routed through Q.B.B. 312, and positions 42°01'N, 08°18'E at 2359 A/21 and 43°40'N, 08°28'E at 2358 A/22 to patrol off Cape Noli.

22 Mar 1944 At 2026 A/22, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1137 A/22, informing her (and other submarines of the 10th Flotilla that were on patrol) that an enemy radar station was reported on Cape Noli.
At 2148 A/22, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1617 A/22 was received which ordered to move to area 'SS' at 0001 A/25. (87)
23 Mar 1944 At 0030 A/23, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) entered area 'SN'. (7)
25 Mar 1944 At 0100 A/25, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) passed out of area 'SN'. Nothing of interest had been sighted in this area.
At 0400 A/25, HMS Uproar entered area 'SS'.
At 0511 A/25, HMS Uproar dived in position 193° - Cape Mele - 10 miles and patrolled 5 miles to the south of Cape Berta.

At 1400 A/25, Oneglia harbour was closed to examine it for shipping. A ship was seen alongside the south wall. The M.D.U. (Mine Detection Unit) was run while closing the harbour but no contact were made. The ship was subsequently identified as a tanker of about 5000 to 6000 tons. She had light grey upperworks and a black hull. One schooner was seen alongside the wall ahead of the tanker and two more schooners were seen on slips on the north side of the harbour.
At 1530 A/25, HMS Uproar retired to seaward.

At 1956 A/25, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 178° - Cape Mele - 5.5 miles. Decided to remain in this area for another day in the hope that tomorrow the weather would be suitable for a gun action against the tanker which had been reported at Oneglia for about two weeks.
During the night HMS Uproar patrolled on the shipping route about 7 miles to the south of Port Maurizio. (7)

26 Mar 1944 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) damaged the German tanker Matera (former Italian, former French General Gassouin, 5011 GRT, built 1926) with gunfire inside the harbour of Oneglia, Liguria, Italy.
0510 A/26 - Dived in position 190° - Cape Mele - 10 miles.
1300 A/26 - Closed Oneglia for gun action against the tanker sighted in that port yesterday.

1455 A/26 - Surfaced in position 140 degrees, Oneglia outer breakwater light, 1 nautical mile and opened fire at the tanker, thought to be of 5000 tons that was moored alongside the south wall, Oneglia harbour. The third round hit the tanker at a range of 2000 yards.
1458 A/26 - Fire was returned from shore with small arms shortly afterwards followed by larger guns.
1500 A/26 - Dived and withdrew to seaward. In all 28 rounds had been fired. At least 16 hits were obtained on the tanker.
1644 A/26 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1147 A/26 which ordered HMS Uproar to move to area 'SU' during the night of 28/29 March.
2000 A/26 - Surfaced in position 206° - Cape Mele - 14 miles. (7)
27 Mar 1944 At 0458 A/27, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived in position 139° - Cape Ferrat - 6 miles.
At 0900 A/27, the harbour of Villafranche was investigated but no shipping was seen. Later no shipping was also seen to be present in the Gulf of Napoule and Gulf Juan.
At 2010 A/27 - HMS Uproar surfaced in position 204° - Antibes lighthouse - 11 miles and patrolled the shipping route to the south-east of Agay during the night. (7)

28 Mar 1944 At 0510 A/28, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived in position 147° - Cape Drammont - 7.5 miles and closed Rade d'Agay but no shipping was seen. Then patrolled three miles from the shore between Agay and the Gulf of Napoule.

At 1216 A/28, the signal timed 1047 A/28 from the Capt. S.10 was received which contained information on the expected arrival of an enemy U-boat at Toulon. At 1926 A/28, another signal (timed 1641 A/28) was received containing additional information.
At 2005 A/28, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 146° - Cape Drammont - 9 miles.
At 2200 A/28, HMS Uproar passed out of area 'SS' and into area 'ST' en-route to area 'SU'. (7)
29 Mar 1944 At 0300 A/29, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) passed out of area 'ST' and entered area 'SU'.
At 0450 A/29, HMS Uproar dived in position 185° - Cape Cépet - 5 miles and continued to close Toulon.

At 0625 A/29, the first ships of the 'Toulon escort group' were seen leaving harbour. It consisted of a modern looking escort vessel resembling the Italian sloop Diana [had been sunk in 1942], one UJ-boat, one R-boat, five Chasseurs and a small trawler. The modern escort vessel and the UJ-boat were the only ones transmitting on Asdic. They slowly swept towards the Petit Passage passing about 1500 yards from HMS Uproar. The modern escort vessel and UJ-boat later returned and at ...

0752 A/29, gained contact on HMS Uproar. For the next hour and a half HMS Uproar acted as Asdic target with these vessels intermittently losing and gaining contact. The rest of the 'party' joined in the hunt and dropped listening charges. The two Asdic equipped vessels passed overhead twice but no depth charges were dropped.
At 0952 A/29, contact was finally lost.

At 1015 A/29, HMS Uproar returned to periscope depth. Only the modern escort vessel was in sight, to the south of Cape Sicié. HMS Uproar remained to seaward as the Asdic equipped vessels were heard to be sweeping to the eastward. A Chasseur was seen to be patrolling off Cape Sicié during the afternoon assisted by two aircraft.
By keeping out of the way it was hoped the enemy would 'settle down' before the U-boat was expected to arrive tomorrow.
At 1830 A/29, the Asdic transmission ceased.
At 2036 A/29, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 220° - Cape Sicié - 11 miles.

The vessels out on A/S patrol were the German UJ-boats UJ 6073, UJ 6075, the auxiliary minesweeper M 6010 and eight vessels of the harbour defence flotilla. They had departed Toulon around 0600 B/29 and returned around 1910 B/29 except for UJ 6075 which had returned around 1410 B/29 with a defective Asdic [note the one hour time zone difference.] No depth charges had been dropped as the Germans were under the impression to be in contact with a known wreck.
The auxiliary minesweepers M 6002 and M 6003 had sailed at 0600 B/29 to meet a U-boat (this was U-466) and escort her in.

Netlayer Netztender 38 and the auxiliary minesweepers M 6023 and M 6027 sailed from Toulon at 0640 B/29 to work on the net near Hyères Island (T1022/3510,) and may also have been among those vessels seen. The tug Carcaran and auxiliary mineweeper M 6012 also sailed at 0700 B/29 for Les Salines. (88)
30 Mar 1944 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) fires four torpedoes at the German U-boat U-466 off Toulon, southern France. All four torpedoes however missed their target.

U 466 reported being missed by three torpedoes. The auxiliary submarine chaser UJ 6075 and the auxiliary minesweepers M 6002 and M 6003 sailed from Toulon to hunt the submarine and attacked a contact at 1613-1641 hours.]

During the night HMS Uproar charged her battery well to seaward to avoid being picked up by enemy radar. On completion of the battery charge Toulon Bay was closed on the probable approach route of the expected enemy U-boat.
0439 A/30 - HMS Uproar was challenged by the signal station on Cape Sicié. Uproar dived in position 192° - Cape Cépet - 6 miles.
0715 A/30 - Two F-lighters were seen leaving Toulon and proceeding eastwards.

0750 A/30 - Two barges were seen leaving Toulon and proceeding westwards round Cape Sicié escorted by two Chasseurs and an old trawler which was belching clouds of black smoke. These were followed by seven more vessels, which included another old trawler, two R-boats, three Chasseurs and one drifter. The vessels seen proceeding westwards were most likely from convoy '5435' which departed Toulon for Marseille around this time. It was made up of two barges (another one was unable to sail) escorted by M 6041, M 6044, M 6045 and FMa 02. The other vessels seen to spread out in Toulon Bay were ships of the harbour defence flotilla.

0815 A/30 - This motley collection of ships spread out across the bay and began to sweep southwards. HMS Uproar was about 2 miles to the south-south-east of Cape Cépet and altered course to the south-west to let this enemy flotilla pass astern.

0840 A/30 - Asdic reported HE on bearing 260°. Two seaplanes were seen circling to seaward but nothing else but black smoke was seen. This was thought to emanate from the trawler which had proceeded to the west with the barges so it was not given very much attention. By this time two Chasseurs had closed HMS Uproar to within 500 yards and as the sea was calm it was only possible to show a bare minimum of periscope.
0850 A/30 - The two Chasseurs turned away.
0904 A/30 - Asdic reported the former HE to have increased and the the conning tower of a U-boat was sighted bearing 307°. Range was about 2 nautical miles. Started attack.

0918 A/30 - In position 43°03'N, 05°57'E fired four torpedoes from 5000 yards. No hits were obtained. Two torpedoes were heard to explode on the beach. The U-boat, escorted by the two Chasseurs, were seen to enter the harbour. [The auxiliary minesweepers, M 6002 and M 6003 were tasked with escorting the U-boat in and were most likely these two 'Chasseurs'.]

0927 A/30 - An aircraft dropped two bombs. Also a depth charge was dropped but none were close. Uproar meanwhile withdrew to seaward. During the remainder of the day the area was patrolled by aircraft
1248 A/30 - A/S transmissions were heard.

1614 A/30 - Thirty depth charges were dropped in three patterns. No damage to Uproar was done. [UJ 6075 dropped 36 depth charges in three patterns. M 6002 and M 6003 also dropped four depth charges each.]
2046 A/30 - Surfaced in position 210° - Cape Sicié - 11 miles and patrolled 10 miles to the seaward.
2209 A/30 - Dived for 10 minutes for a patrolling aircraft. (89)
31 Mar 1944 At 0457 A/31, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) dived in position 140° - Cape Sicié - 8 miles and commenced to close Toulon.

At 0605 A/31, on coming to periscope depth, two vessels, thought to be an R-boat and another small vessel, were seen patrolling in Toulon Bay. HMS Uproar retired to the south to study what more would be coming out.

At 0630 A/31, two vessels, throught to be a modern destroyer type vessel and an UJ boat left Toulon and swept down towards HMS Utmost's position. Lt. Herrick decided to retire to the south-west at 120 feet.
At 0735 A/31, both these vessels obtained Asdic contact which they lost again around 0759 A/31.
At 0835 A/31, a depth charge exploded.

At 0900 A/31, HMS Uproar returned to periscope depth. Nothing was in sight but visibility was poor. Until noon Asdic transmissions were still heard and the vessels were occasionally seen. Also a seaplane was seen searching the area.
At 1200 A/31, HMS Uproar closed west of Cape Sicié to patrol on the 100 fathom line.
At 1400 A/31, HMS Uproar withdrew to seaward. A strong westerly gale now having blown up and visibility had closed down.
At 2045 A/31, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 225° - Cape Sicié - 9 miles. A night surface attack would be out of the question due to the weather conditions.

The German auxiliary submarine chasers UJ 6073 [which was probably mistaken for a 'destroyer'] and UJ 6075, the auxiliary minesweeper M 6010 and eight vessels of the harbour defence flotilla were conducting A/S operations off Toulon in the morning and early afternoon. Late in the morning they were joined by the auxiliary submarine chaser UJ 2216 which had been on passage from Marseille to Toulon. (88)

1 Apr 1944 At 0038 A/1, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) received the signal timed 1613 A/31 from the Capt. S.10 which contained the following orders (for her); HMS Uproar is to leave patrol to pass through positions 42°27'N, 06°01'E at 0001 A/2 and 42°02'N, 07°57'E at 2300 A/2 to arrive at La Maddalena at 1030 A/3.

At 0445 A/1, HMS Uproar dived in position 347° - Cape Cépet - 13 miles. From D/F bearings it was expected an enemy U-boat would arrive at Toulon during the afternoon from the south-east. Lt. Herrick decided to remain to seaward for now and study the A/S measures taken by the enemy and was hoping to close Toulon in the afternoon. During the early part of the forenoon seaplanes were seen patrolling in the area.

At 0750 A/1, a 'destroyer' and an UJ-boat were seen leaving Toulon, presumably for an A/S sweep / hunt. Asdic transmissions were picked up. Patrolling conditions were good now, the gale having blown itself out.
At 1030 A/1, five sources of Asdic transmissions were now reported. An UJ boat was seen to the north and the 'destroyer' to the east.
At 1130 A/1, the 'destroyer' was seen to close the position of HMS Uproar.
At 1149 A/1, the enemy 'destroyer' obtained contact and held it for five minutes. Another vessel was also in contact. They left the area after about an hour not having dropped depth charges.
At 1500 A/1, HMS Uproar commenced to patrol two miles to the south of the Giens Peninsula.
At 1800 A/1, two Chasseurs were seen leaving Toulon and proceeding towards the Petit Pass.
At 1930 A/1, HMS Uproar retired to seawards as it was getting too dark to make a periscope attack.
At 2038 A/1, HMS Uproar surfaced in position 132° - Cape Cépet - 9.5 miles and course was set to leave patrol and return to La Maddalena.

The German auxiliary submarine chasers UJ 6073 [which was again probably mistaken for a 'destroyer'], UJ 6075, UJ 2216, the auxiliary minesweeper M 6010 and eight vessels of the harbour defence flotilla were conducting an A/S operations off Toulon during morning and early afternoon.
The expected German U-boat was U-421 but she actually arrived at Toulon in the morning being escorted in by M 6003. (90)
3 Apr 1944 At 0950 A/3, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) ended her 28th war patrol (23rd in the Mediterranean) at La Maddalena. (7)
This convoy departed Port Said on 4 April 1944.

On departure from Port Said the convoy was made up of the merchant vessels; Anna Knudsen (Norwegian (tanker), 9057 GRT, built 1931), Burnside (British, 5659 GRT, built 1940), Caithness (British, 4970 GRT, built 1935), Dallington Court (British, 6889 GRT, built 1929), Egero (Norwegian (tanker), 7590 GRT, built 1929), Fort Bourbon (British, 7133 GRT, built 1942), Fort Dease Lake (British, 7126 GRT, built 1943), Fort Hudson's Hope (British, 7129 GRT, built 1942), Ima (Norwegian (tanker), 6842 GRT, built 1930), Moses Rogers (American, 7181 GRT, built 1942), Mount Revelstoke Park (Canadian, 7144 GRT, built 1943), Samfeugh (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Samtay (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Shickshinny (American, 5103 GRT, built 1919) and William Harper (American, 7191 GRT, built 1943).
On departure from Port Said the convoy was escorted by the corvettes HMS Bergamot (A/Lt.Cdr. W. McInnes, RNR) and HMS Myosotis (T/Lt. R. Lugg, RNR).

On 5 April 1944, the following merchant vessels departed Alexandria to join the convoy; Anne Bradstreet (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Beaconsfield (British, 4635 GRT, built 1938), Chiswick (British, 6006 GRT, built 1943), Cid (British, 1847 GRT, built 1922), Corfell (British, 1802 GRT, built 1934), David Stone (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Edward Rowland Sill (American, 7181 GRT, built 1942), Empire Glory (British, 7290 GRT, built 1943), Empire Grange (British, 6981 GRT, built 1943), Eric V. Hauser (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Euryades (British, 5801 GRT, built 1913), Ferdinand Westdahl (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Fernplant (British, 5274 GRT, built 1939), Fort St. Paul (British, 7137 GRT, built 1943), Glenstrae (British, 9460 GRT, built 1922), Henry Dearborn (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), John L. Stoddard (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), John Page (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Joshua L. Chamberlain (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Riverton (British, 7307 GRT, built 1943), Russell A. Alger (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Tatra (Norwegian, 4766 GRT, built 1937) and Van der Capelle (Dutch, 7037 GRT, built 1942).
They were escorted by the frigate HMS Barle (Lt.Cdr. A.H. Davies, RNVR) (S.O. 48th Escort Group) and the corvette HMS La Malouine (Lt. W.A. Ives, RNR).

On 10 April 1944, the following merchant vessels were detached to Augusta; Beaconsfield, Burnside, Caithness, Chiswick, Corfell, Dallington Court, Empire Glory, Empire Grange, Euryades, Fort Bourbon, Fort Dease Lake, Fort Hudson's Hope, Fort St. Paul, Riverton and Van der Capelle.

The following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Augusta; Amberton (British, 5377 GRT, built 1928), Asa Gray (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Askeladden (Norwegian, 2496 GRT, built 1920), Belgian Airman (Belgian, 6960 GRT, built 1942), Benito Juarez (American, 7244 GRT, built 1943), Benjamin Goodhue (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), British Promise (British (tanker), 8443 GRT, built 1942), Comedian (British, 5122 GRT, built 1929), Corcrest (British, 2373 GRT, built 1918), Crosby S. Noyes (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Edward Richardson (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Empire Addison (British, 7010 GRT, built 1942), Empire Rosalind (British, 7290 GRT, built 1943), Eugene Hale (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Fort Brandon (British, 7131 GRT, built 1943), Fort Cataraqui (British, 7130 GRT, built 1942), Fort Michipicoten (British, 7152 GRT, 1943), Fort Remy (British, 7127 GRT, built 1943), Hardingham (British, 7269 GRT, built 1942), Helencrest (British, 5233 GRT, built 1941), John Clarke (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Joseph Hewes (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Joshua Seney (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Lyminge (British, 2499 GRT, built 1919), Maihar (British, 7563 GRT, built 1917), Mark Twain (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Mirabeau B. Lamar (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Nelson Dingley (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Neuralia (British, 9182 GRT, built 1912), Norman Monarch (British, 7005 GRT, built 1943), Ocean Trader (British, 7178 GRT, built 1942), Peter V. Daniel (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), R.C. Brennan (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Roald Amundsen (Norwegian, 7191 GRT, built 1943), Robert Treat Paine (American, 7191 GRT, built 1942), Samythian (British, 7219 GRT, built 1944), Taria (Dutch (tanker), 10354 GRT, built 1939), Thomas B. Reed (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Thomas Lynch (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Thomas Sumter (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942) and Wiley Post (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943). The naval oil barge (self propelled) USS Pumper also joined the convoy.
On 10 April 1944, the merchant vessel Cid was detached to Malta.

The following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Malta; Balteako (British, 1328 GRT, built 1920), Belgian Veteran (British, 7174 GRT, built 1942), Graiglas (British, 4312 GRT, built 1940), Sameveron (British, 7219 GRT, built 1944) and Samkey (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943).

On 11 April 1944, the following merchant vessels were detached to Tunis / Bizerta; Empire Rosalind, Fort Brandon, Ima, Mark Twain, Ocean Trader, R.C. Brennan, Roald Amundsen and Thomas Lynch. USS Pumper was also detached to Bizerta.

The following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Tunis; Fylingdale (British, 3918 GRT, built 1924), George H. Thomas (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), John M. Harlan (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Maya (Honduran, 5528 GRT, built 1929) and William Kent (American, 7187 GRT, built 1942).

The following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Bizerta; Cyrus W. Field (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Darien (Panamanian, 3683 GRT, built 1924), Hugh Williamson (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Pallade (Italian, 1152 GRT, built 1899) and Paul H. Harwood (American (tanker), 7192 GRT, built 1918).

At 1245 Z/11 the British escort was detached to Bizerta [apparently HMS Myosotis proceeded to Malta] and a new escort joined. This escort was made up of the destroyers USS Decatur (Lt. D.G. Wright, USNR, with COMTASKFOR 64, Capt. H.S. Berdine, USCG on board), USS Whipple (T/Lt.Cdr. S.E. Woodard, USN, with COMDESDIV 57, T/Cdr. E.W. Yancey, USN, on board), USS Alden (Lt. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN), USS John D. Edwards (Lt. R.A. Norelius, USNR) and the destroyer escorts USS Sellstrom (Cdr. W.L. Maloney, USCG, with COMCORTDIV 23, Cdr. F.P. Vetterick, USNR, on board), USS Ramsden (Lt.Cdr. S.T. Baketel, USCGR), USS Mills (Lt.Cdr. J.S. Muzzy, USCG), USS Rhodes (Cdr. E.A. Coffin, Jr., USCG), USS Richey (Cdr. P.DuP. Mills, USCG), USS Savage (Cdr. O.C. Rohnke, USCG), USS Tomich (Lt. C.B. Brown, USNR) and USS Sloat (Lt.Cdr. W.A. Cashman, USNR). As additional escort the AA cruiser HrMs Jacob van Heemskerck (Capt. W. Harmsen, RNethN) also joined the convoy.
At 0015 Z/12, USS Tomich fouled her rudder and screws with an obstruction and she was ordered to proceed to Oran to clear. She later rejoined at 1130 Z/14.
At 0500 Z/12, the following merchant vessels were detached to Bone; Belgian Airman, Belgian Veteran, Comedian, Empire Addison, Fort Michipicoten, Hardingham, Lyminge and Norman Monarch.

At the same time the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Bone; Empire Trent (British, 5006 GRT, built 1927), Everleigh (British, 5222 GRT, built 1930), Janine (French, 2345 GRT, built 1918) and Novasli (Norwegian, 3204 GRT, built 1920).
At 1030 Z/12, the merchant vessel Barrgrove (British, 5222 GRT, built 1918) joined the convoy coming from Philippeville.
At the same time, the merchant vessel Janine was detached to Bougie.

At 1200 Z/13, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Algiers; Atjutant (British, 1931 GRT, built 1922), Atlantic City (British, 5133 GRT, built 1941), Clan Cameron (British, 7243 GRT, built 1937), Clearpool (British, 5405 GRT, built 1935), Donacilla (British (tanker), 8113 GRT, built 1939), Edward N. Hurley (American, 7191 GRT, built 1943), Empire Shearwater (British, 4970 GRT, built 1920), Empire Symbol (British, 7027 GRT, built 1943), Fort Maurepas (British, 7133 GRT, built 1942), Joseph Alston (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Lesto (British, 1893 GRT, 1918), Morrison R. Waite (American, 7181 GRT, built 1942), Nyhorn (Norwegian, 4494 GRT, built 1929), Robert Jordan (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Robert M.T. Hunter (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Samearn (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Taxiarchis (Greek, 4221 GRT, built 1913) and Walter E. Ranger (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943). Also joining from Algiers was the submarine HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN).
At the same time the following merchant vessels were detached to Algiers; Askeladden, Corcrest, Egero, Everleigh, Fort Remy, Glenstrae, Neuralia, Samythian and Thomas Sumter.

At 1130 Z/14, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Oran; Benjamin R. Milam (American, 7244 GRT, built 1943), Christopher Gadsen (American, 7177 GRT, built 1942), Dilworth (American (tanker), 7045 GRT, built 1920), Ezra Cornell (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Fanad Head (British, 5038 GRT, built 1941), Felipe de Neve (American, 7176 GRT, built 1942), Fort Frederick (British, 7135 GRT, built 1942), Fort Pine (British, 7133 GRT, built 1942), Francis L. Lee (American, 7191 GRT, built 1942), George B. McClellan (American, 7181 GRT, built 1942), Henry Durant (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Isaac Sharpless (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), John B. Gordon (American, 7201 GRT, built 1943), M. M. Guhin (American, 7180 GRT, 1943), Mary Wilkins Freeman (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Niobe (Panamanian (tanker), 7299 GRT, built 1915), Samson (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943) and Tanafjord (Norwegian, 5922 GRT, built 1921). The naval stores ship USS Polaris (T/Cdr. C.R. Miller, USN) also joined the convoy. Also joining were the destroyer USS Lansdale (T/Cdr. D.M. Swift, USN) and the minesweeper USS Pilot (Lt.Cdr. S.B. Wetmore, USNR), which had escorted the joiners from Oran, joined the convoy. Also USS Tomich rejoined the convoy.
At the same time the following merchant vessels were detached to Oran; Adjutant, Crosby S. Noyes, Empire Trent, Lesto and Taxiarchis.
At 2000 Z/14, USS John D. Edwards left to convoy to proceed ahead to Gibraltar to fuel. She rejoined at 1108 Z/15.
At 0200 Z/15, USS Lansdale parted company with the convoy.
At 0240 Z/15, USS Whipple left to convoy to proceed ahead to Gibraltar to fuel. She rejoined at 1255 Z/15.
At 0920 Z/15, USS Alden left to convoy to proceed ahead to Gibraltar to fuel. She rejoined at 1800 Z/15.
At 1108 Z/15, USS John D. Edwards rejoined the convoy having fuelled at Gibraltar.
At 1255 Z/15, USS Whipple rejoined the convoy having fuelled at Gibraltar.
At 1315 Z/15, HrMs Jacob van Heemskerck left to convoy to proceed to Gibraltar.

At 1600 Z/15, the destroyer USS John D. Ford (T/Lt.Cdr. J.S. Slaughter, USN) joined the convoy coming from Gibraltar where she had been undergoing temporary repairs to repair damage sustained from a collision on 29 March.
At 1800 Z/15, USS Decatur left to convoy to proceed ahead to Gibraltar to fuel. She rejoined at 2045 Z/15. USS Alden had just rejoined the convoy having fuelled at Gibraltar.
At 1845 Z/15, HMS Uproar parted company to proceed to Gibraltar escorted by USS Mills.

At 1900 Z/15, the merchant vessel Joshua Henry (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943) joined the convoy coming from Gibraltar. She was escorted to the rendezvous by the patrol vessels USS PC-471 (Lt. G.B. Calkins, USNR) and USS PC-480 (Lt.(jg) J.K. Miller, USNR) which also joined the convoy.
At the same time the merchant vessels Amberton, Anna Knudsen, Balteako, Empire Shearwater, Fylingdale, Graiglas, Novasli and Pallade were detached to Gibraltar.
At 2045 Z/15, USS Decatur rejoined the convoy having fuelled at Gibraltar.
At 2200 Z/15, rejoined the convoy after having escorted HMS Uproar to Gibraltar.

At 1200 Z/16, the following merchant vessels joined the convoy coming from Casablanca; Frederick Banting (American, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Lorraine (French (tanker), 9512 GRT, built 1937), Newton D. Baker (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943), Pan-Maryland (American (tanker), 7701 GRT, built 1938), Sambay (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Samchess (British, 7219 GRT, built 1944), Samota (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943), Samovar (British, 7219 GRT, built 1943) and William J. Bryan (American, 7176 GRT, built 1943). Also the naval tanker USS Kennebec (Cdr. M.D. Mullen, USNR) joined coming from Casablanca. They had been escorted to the rendezvous by the destroyers HMS Antelope (Cdr. J.G. Gould, RN), HMS Anthony (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Wallace, DSC, RN), HMS Brilliant (Lt.Cdr. J. Smallwood, RN), HMS Vanoc (Lt.Cdr. P.R. Ward, RN) and the patrol vessel USS PC-472 (Lt.Cdr. P.A. Dallis, Jr., USNR). These escort vessels temporary joined the convoy.

At 1800 Z/16, the following merchant vessels parted company with the convoy to proceed to Casablanca; Barrgrove, Clearpool, Empire Symbol, Fort Cataraqui, Fort Frederick, Nyhorn, Robert M.T. Hunter, Samearn and Sameveron. USS PC-471, USS PC-472 and USS PC-480 went with them as escorts. The four British destroyers which had joined at 1200 Z/16 also parted company in accordance with their orders.

At 0700 Z/17, the merchant vessel Niobe straggled from the convoy. She was ordered to continue on the the USA via the stragglers route but later information was received that she was ordered to proceed to the Azores.
On 19 April 1944, USS Decatur and USS Whipple fuelled from USS Kennebec.
On 20 April 1944, USS John D. Ford, USS John D. Edwards, USS Alden and USS Pilot fuelled from USS Kennebec.
At 1045 Z/20, the merchant vessels Fort Anne (British, 7134 GRT, built 1943) and Hoke Smith (American, 7199 GRT, built 1943) joined the convoy coming from the Azores.
On 23 April 1944, USS Decatur fuelled from USS Kennebec.
On 27 April 1944, USS Decatur, USS Whipple, USS Alden, USS John D. Edwards and USS John D. Ford fuelled from USS Kennebec.

At 1300 Z/29, USS Kennebec parted company with the convoy to proceed to Bermuda. The destroyer escorts USS Edmonds (T/Cdr. C.S. Barker, Jr., USN) and USS Shelton (Lt.Cdr. L.G. Salomon, USNR) had just made rendezvous with the convoy to escort the tanker to her destination.

At 0001 Z/1, the Hapton Roads section of the convoy, made up of 38 merchant vessels parted company. USS Whipple, USS Alden, USS John D. Edwards, USS John D. Ford and USS Pilot went with them as escorts.
At 1100 Z/1, the Delaware Capes section of 5 merchant vessels was detached. USS Ramsden and USS Savage went with them as escorts.
At 2100 Z/1, two merchant vessels were detached to St. John, New Brunswick.
At 0250 Z/2, one merchant vessel was detached to Philadelphia. The remainder of the convoy entered New York harbour later that morning. (91)
9 Apr 1944 At 0730 B/9, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed La Maddalena for Algiers. HMS Uproar was to proceed to the U.K. to refit.
She was routed as follows; Q.B.B. 312 and then trough position 41°10'N, 08°08'E at 1600 B/9 to make rendezvous with her escort to Algiers at 1100 B/11 in position 36°58'N, 03°03'E.
[No log is available for this period so no map of the passage to the U.K. can be displayed.] (7)
11 Apr 1944 Around 1300 B/11, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) arrived at Algiers from La Maddalena.
She had made rendezvous with her escort at 1045 B/11. (7)
13 Apr 1944 At 1115 B/13, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Algiers for Gibraltar.
At 1330 B/13, she joined convoy GUS 36 for the passage. She had been escorted to the rendezvous by HMML 273 (T/Lt. G.A. Randell, RNVR).
For more info on this convoy see the event ' Convoy GUS 36 ' for 4 April 1944. (7)
15 Apr 1944 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) arrived at Gibraltar. (7)
19 Apr 1944 In the morning, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for Plymouth.
She was to be escorted until 41°55'N by HMS Kilmelford (T/Lt. H. Brown, RNR) (7)

22 Apr 1944 At 1625 B/22, in approximate position 41°55'N, 12°22'W, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) and HMS Kilmelford (T/Lt. H. Brown, RNR) made rendezvous with HMS Sturdy (Lt. W.St.G. Anderson, DSC, RNR) and HMS Vox (Lt. J.M. Michell, RN).
HMS Uproar then parted company with HMS Kilmelford which now was to escort the other two submarines to Gibraltar. (7)
29 Apr 1944 At 0615 B/29, HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) made rendezvous with her escort, the submarine chaser Chasseur 15 which was to escort her to Plymouth.
At 1715 B/29, HMS Uproar entered Plymouth Sound. (7)
30 Apr 1944 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) departed Plymouth for Portsmouth. (7)

1 May 1944 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) arrived at Portland but shortly afterwards continued her passage to Portsmouth where she arrived later the same day. She then departed Portsmouth for Sheerness. (7)
2 May 1944 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) arrived at Sheerness.
4 May 1944 HMS Uproar (Lt. L.E. Herrick, DSC, RN) arrived at London (Tilbury) to refit at the Green and Silley Weir Ltd. Shipyard.

1 Sep 1944 Around 0700 B/1, HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) departed Sheerness for Dundee. She made the passage together with HMS Unshaken (Lt. J.S. Pearce, RNR). They were escorted by HMS Greenfly (T/Lt. D.B. Orr, RNVR). (92)
3 Sep 1944 Around 0845 B/3, HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) arrived at Dundee. (92)

6 Sep 1944 Around 1800 B/6, HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) departed Dundee for Scapa Flow. She made the passage together with HMS Unshaken (Lt. J.S. Pearce, RNR). They were escorted by HMS Loch Monteith (T/A/Lt.Cdr. K.W. Richardson, RNR). (92)
7 Sep 1944 Around 1700 B/7, HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) arrived at Scapa Flow from Dundee. (92)

8 Sep 1944 Around 0645 B/8, HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN), HMS Unshaken (Lt. J.S. Pearce, RNR) and HMS Varne (Lt. I.G. Raikes, DSC, RN) all departed Scapa Flow for Rothesay / Holy Loch. They made the passage together with HMS Shakespeare (Lt. D. Swanston, DSC and Bar, RN) which came from Lerwick. They were escorted by HMS Cutty Sark (Lt. H.J. Bartlett, DSC, RN). (92)
10 Sep 1944 Around 0900 B/10, HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) arrived at Rothesay. She was now assigned to training duties. (92)
11 Sep 1944 Around 1200 B/11, HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) departed Rothesay for Heysham. She was escorted by HMS Blade (T/A/Lt.Cdr. S.T. Wenlock, RNR) until 0720 B/12. (92)
12 Sep 1944 Around 0930 B/12, HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) arrived at Heysham. She was escorted in by HMML 145 (T/Lt. G.S. Sykes, RNVR) which had joined around 0810 B/12.
She departed later the same day to participate in exercises off Morecambe Bay. HMML 145 again being in company. (92)
15 Sep 1944 At 2130 B/15, HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) returned to Heysham from exercises. (92)

17 Sep 1944 Around 1230 A/17, HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) departed Heysham to participate in exercises off Morecambe Bay. HMML 148 (T/S.Lt. T.D. Stoner, RNVR) being in company as escort. (92)

18 Sep 1944 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) proceeded to Douglas, Isle of Man upon completion of the exercises off Morecambe Bay. She arrived at Douglas around 0940 B/18 being escorted to there by HMML 145 (T/Lt. G.S. Sykes, RNVR) which had joined around 0700 A/18.
Around 1230 A/18, she departed Douglas to return to the exercise area. HMML 145 and HMML 152 (T/S.Lt. T.O. Roberts, RNVR) being in company as escorts.
At 1925 A/18, she entered Douglas harbour once more escorted by HMML 152. (92)

Around 1500 A/26, she departed Rothesay for Ardrishaig where she arrived around 1810 A/26. (92)

Stoner, RN), HMS Foley (Lt.Cdr. C.A.H. Bird, RNVR) and HMS Loch Dunvegan (Cdr. E. Wheeler, RD, RNR). HMS Philante (Cdr. M.J. Evans, OBE, RN) was overseeing the exercises. (94)

Smillie, RCNVR), HMCS St. Thomas (T/A/Lt.Cdr. L.P. Denny, RCNR) and HMCS Trillium (T/Lt. K.E. Meredith, RCNVR). [Maybee not all vessels participated in the exercise though. 8 Mar 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises in Inner Sound (off Loch Alsh) with HMS Loch Shin (Cdr. J.P. de W. Kitcat, RN). (100)
11 Mar 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises in Inner Sound (off Loch Alsh) with HMS Hesperus (Lt. A.C.D. Leach, RN). (100)

14 Mar 1945 During 14/15 March 1945, HMS Vitality (Lt. W.T.J. Fox, RN) and HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted exercises in the area to the north of Rona Island in the Minches. These included night exercises.
They entered Portree Bay on the 15th and returned to Loch Alsh on the 16th. (99)
22 Mar 1945 HMS Vitality (Lt. W.T.J. Fox, RN) and HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Loch Alsh. (99)

23 Mar 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) participated in A/S exercises off Loch Alsh with ships of the 1st Escort Group. This group was made up of the frigates HMS Balfour (Cdr.(Retd.) C. Gwinner, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Bentley (Lt.Cdr. E.G. May, DSC, RN), HMS Garlies (T/A/Lt.Cdr. L.M.M. Stamp, RNVR), HMS Gore (Lt. J.V. Reeves-Brown, DSC, RN) and HMS Hoste (Lt. P.J.H. Hoare, RN) [A sixth frigate of this Escort Group was undergoing repairs at Belfast]. HMS Philante (Cdr. M.J. Evans, OBE, RN) would also have been present to coordinate the exercises and play the role of target ship. (101)

24 Mar 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Tobermory with the 1st Escort Group which was made up of the frigates HMS Balfour (Cdr.(Retd.) C. Gwinner, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Bentley (Lt.Cdr. E.G. May, DSC, RN), HMS Garlies (T/A/Lt.Cdr. L.M.M. Stamp, RNVR), HMS Gore (Lt. J.V. Reeves-Brown, DSC, RN) and HMS Hoste (Lt. P.J.H. Hoare, RN). HMS Philante (Cdr. M.J. Evans, OBE, RN) would also have been present to coordinate the exercises and play the role of target ship. (100)
27 Mar 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) participated in A/S exercises off Tobermory. (100)

28 Mar 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Loch Alsh with ships of the 30th Escort Group, which was made up of the corvettes HMS Pevensey Castle (Cdr. E. Hewitt, RD, RNR), HMS Caistor Castle (A/Lt.Cdr. B.J. Bowick, RNVR), HMS Kenilworth Castle (A/Lt.Cdr. J.O. Williams, RNR) and HMS Launceston Castle (Lt. R.M. Roberts, RNR) [a fifth corvette of the group was not present], as well as with HMS Philante (Cdr. M.J. Evans, OBE, RN). (100)

30 Mar 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Loch Alsh with ships of the 16th Escort Group which was made up of frigates HMCS Springhill (A/Cdr. W.C. Halliday, RCNR), HMCS Antigonish (T/Lt.Cdr. R.D. Barrett, RCNR), HMCS Charlottetown (T/Lt.Cdr. J. Harding, RCNR), HMCS Kirkland Lake (Lt.Cdr. N.V. Clark, OBE, RCNR) and HMCS Stettler (T/Lt.Cdr. D.G. King, RCNVR). HMS Philante (Cdr. M.J. Evans, OBE, RN) most likely would also have been present to coordinate and oversee the exercises. (100)
30 Mar 1945 HrMs O 23 (Lt.Cdr. A.J. Schouwenaar, RNN) conducted attack exercises off Loch Alsh during which HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) served as the target.

These attack exercises were followed by A/S exercises off Loch Alsh with HMCS Charlottetown (T/Lt.Cdr. J. Harding, RCNR), HMCS Antigonish (T/Lt.Cdr. R.D. Barrett, RCNR) and HMCS Kirkland Lake (Lt.Cdr. N.V. Clark, OBE, RCNR). (102)

1 Apr 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Loch Alsh with ships of the 16th Escort Group which was made up of frigates HMCS Springhill (A/Cdr. W.C. Halliday, RCNR), HMCS Antigonish (T/Lt.Cdr. R.D. Barrett, RCNR), HMCS Charlottetown (T/Lt.Cdr. J. Harding, RCNR), HMCS Kirkland Lake (Lt.Cdr. N.V. Clark, OBE, RCNR) and HMCS Stettler (T/Lt.Cdr. D.G. King, RCNVR). HMS Philante (Cdr. M.J. Evans, OBE, RN) was also present to coordinate and oversee the exercises and act as target ship. (103)
2 Apr 1945 HMS Vitality (Lt. W.T.J. Fox, RN) and HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Loch Alsh. (104)
3 Apr 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Loch Alsh. (103)

6 Apr 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) and HMS Vitality (Lt. W.T.J. Fox, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Loch Alsh ship of the 2nd Escort Group which was made up of the sloop HMS Wild Goose (Cdr. D.E.G. Wemyss, DSO, DSC and Bar, RN) and the frigates HMS Loch Fada (Cdr. B.A. Rogers, RD, RNR), HMS Loch Ruthven (A/Lt.Cdr. R.T. Horan, RNR), HMS Dominica (Lt.Cdr. H.N. Lawson, RD, RNR), HMS Labuan (T/A/Lt.Cdr. V.D.H. Bidwell, DSC, RNR) and HMS Tobago (T/A/Lt.Cdr. J.H.L. May, OBE, RNVR). (104)

7 Apr 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Loch Alsh with ships of the 8th Escort Group which was made up of the frigates HMS Loch Achray (T/A/Lt.Cdr. L.H. Stammers, RNVR, with the Commanding Officer of the group, A/Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC and two Bars, RN, on board), HMS Loch Glendhu (Lt.Cdr. E.G.P.B. Knapton, RN), HMS Loch More (Lt.Cdr. R.A.D. Cambridge, DSC, RNR) and HMSAS Natal (T/Lt.Cdr. D.A. Hall, DSC, SANF(V)) (a fifth frigate of the Escort Group was undergoing repairs at Belfast and was not present). (103)

9 Apr 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Loch Alsh with ships of the 8th Escort Group which was made up of the frigates HMS Loch Achray (T/A/Lt.Cdr. L.H. Stammers, RNVR, with the Commanding Officer of the group, A/Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC and two Bars, RN, on board), HMS Loch Glendhu (Lt.Cdr. E.G.P.B. Knapton, RN), HMS Loch More (Lt.Cdr. R.A.D. Cambridge, DSC, RNR) and HMSAS Natal (T/Lt.Cdr. D.A. Hall, DSC, SANF(V)) (a fifth frigate of the Escort Group was undergoing repairs at Belfast and was not present). (103)

11 Apr 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) and HMS Vitality (Lt. W.T.J. Fox, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Tobermory with ships of the 15th Escort Group which was at that moment made up of the frigates HMS Dacres (T/A/Lt.Cdr. R. Leigh-Wood, RNVR), HMS Inglis (T/A/Lt.Cdr. A.P. Cobbold, RNVR), HMS Loring (Lt. J.A. Ogilvy, RN) and HMS Mounsey (T/A/Lt.Cdr. G.C. Julian, RNZNVR). (104)

14 Apr 1945 At 1000 B/14, HMS Vitality (Lt. W.T.J. Fox, RN), HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) and HMS Satyr (Lt. T.S. Weston, DSO, DSC, RN) departed Loch Alsh for Oban where they arrived around 1930 B/14. They were escorted by HMS Kalan (T/Lt. H.E. Wagner, RNVR). (105)

15 Apr 1945 HMS Vitality (Lt. W.T.J. Fox, RN), HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) and HMS Satyr (Lt. T.S. Weston, DSO, DSC, RN) shifted from Oban to Rothesay. They were escorted by HMS Kihna (Cdr.(Retd.) T.J.T.C. Jenks, RN). (105)
1 May 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) served as target in the Clyde area for HMS Safari (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) J.R.G. Harvey, RN). (106)
2 May 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) served as target in the Clyde area for HMS Safari (Lt.Cdr.(Retd.) J.R.G. Harvey, RN). (106)
5 May 1945 Around 1000 B/5, HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) departed Rothesay Bay for Campbeltown where she arrived around 1415 B/5. (106)
6 May 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Campbeltown with HMS Radiant (T/A/Lt.Cdr. A. Wallace, RNVR) and HMS Spaniel (T/A/Skr. I.W. Sharp, RNR). (106)

10 May 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) ran aground off Campbeltown while en-route to the exercises area. She came off quickly but returned to harbour to be inspected for possible damage. (106)

11 May 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Campbeltown with HMS Clover (Lt. R.P. Booth, RNVR), HMS Radiant (T/A/Lt.Cdr. A. Wallace, RNVR), HMS Carnation (T/Lt. W.R. Fowler, RNVR) and HMS Bedlington (A/Ch.Skr. A.W. Peak, RNR). (106)

14 May 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Campbeltown with HMS Shemara (Cdr.(Retd.) H. Buckle, RN), HMS St. Modwen (T/A/Lt.Cdr. F.J.G. Rice, RNR), HMS Spaniel (T/A/Skr. I.W. Sharp, RNR) and HMS Boarhound (Skr. A.G.G. Jenner, RD, RNR). (106)

16 May 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Campbeltown with HMS Shemara (Cdr.(Retd.) H. Buckle, RN), HMS St. Modwen (T/A/Lt.Cdr. F.J.G. Rice, RNR), HMS Carnation (T/Lt. W.R. Fowler, RNVR) and HMS Clover (Lt. R.P. Booth, RNVR). (106)

17 May 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Campbeltown with HMML 472 (T/Lt. J.M.W. Hardie, RNVR), HMML 115 (T/Lt. V.H. Harris, RNVR), HMS Spaniel (T/A/Skr. I.W. Sharp, RNR) and HMS Bedlington (A/Ch.Skr. A.W. Peak, RNR). (106)

20 May 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Campbeltown with HMS Altair (T/Lt. J.L. James, RNR), HMS Radiant (T/A/Lt.Cdr. A. Wallace, RNVR), HMS La Cordeliere (T/Lt. F.A. Haworth, RNVR) and HMS Spaniel (T/A/Skr. I.W. Sharp, RNR). (106)
21 May 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Campbeltown with HMS Carnation (T/Lt. W.R. Fowler, RNVR) and HMS Borage (T/Lt. E.A. Hitchcock, RNR). (106)

22 May 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Campbeltown with HMS Ambuscade (A/Lt.Cdr. H.G. Chesterman, DSC and Bar, RNR) and HMS La Cordeliere (T/Lt. F.A. Haworth, RNVR). (106)

23 May 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Campbeltown with HMS Bedlington (A/Ch.Skr. A.W. Peak, RNR), HMS Astravel (T/Lt. D.E. Lewis, RNVR), HMS Ambuscade (A/Lt.Cdr. H.G. Chesterman, DSC and Bar, RNR) and HMS Carnation (T/Lt. W.R. Fowler, RNVR). (106)

24 May 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Campbeltown with HMS Borage (T/Lt. E.A. Hitchcock, RNR), HMS Clover (Lt. R.P. Booth, RNVR), HMS Shemara (Cdr.(Retd.) H. Buckle, RN) and HMS St. Modwen (T/A/Lt.Cdr. F.J.G. Rice, RNR). (106)

27 May 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Campbeltown with HMS Borage (T/Lt. E.A. Hitchcock, RNR), HMS Clover (Lt. R.P. Booth, RNVR), HMS Shemara (Cdr.(Retd.) H. Buckle, RN) and HMS St. Modwen (T/A/Lt.Cdr. F.J.G. Rice, RNR). (106)
28 May 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Campbeltown with HMS Shemara (Cdr.(Retd.) H. Buckle, RN) and HMS St. Modwen (T/A/Lt.Cdr. F.J.G. Rice, RNR). (106)

29 May 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Campbeltown with HMS La Cordeliere (T/Lt. F.A. Haworth, RNVR), HMS Borage (T/Lt. E.A. Hitchcock, RNR), HMS Oxlip (T/A/Lt.Cdr. J.K. Craig, RNVR) and HMS Ambuscade (A/Lt.Cdr. H.G. Chesterman, DSC and Bar, RNR). (106)
1 Jun 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Campbeltown with HMS Shemara (Cdr.(Retd.) H. Buckle, RN) and HMS Borage (T/Lt. E.A. Hitchcock, RNR). (107)
3 Jun 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted A/S exercises off Campbeltown with HMS Altair (T/Lt. J.L. James, RNR) and HMS Astravel (T/Lt. D.E. Lewis, RNVR). (107)
4 Jun 1945 At 1000 B/4, HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) departed Campbeltown for Rothesay where she arrived around 1420 A/4. (107)
7 Jun 1945 In the evening, HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted gunnery exercises in the Clyde area. (107)
8 Jun 1945 Around 2000 B/8, HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) departed Rothesay for Moelfre Bay. (107)
9 Jun 1945 Around 1340 B/9, HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) arrived at Moelfre Bay from Rothesay. (107)
10 Jun 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Moelfre Bay. (107)
11 Jun 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Llandudno. (107)
12 Jun 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Llandudno. (107)
13 Jun 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Llandudno. (107)
14 Jun 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Llandudno. Upon completion of these exercises she proceeded to Moelfre Bay. (107)
15 Jun 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Llandudno. (107)
16 Jun 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Llandudno. Upon completion of these exercises she proceeded to Moelfre Bay. (107)
17 Jun 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Llandudno. (107)
18 Jun 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Llandudno. (107)
19 Jun 1945 HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Llandudno. Upon completion of these exercises she set course for Larne. (107)
20 Jun 1945 Around 0745 B/20, HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) arrived at Larne.
Around 1200 B/20, she departed Larne for Rothesay where she arrived around 1930 B/20. This passage was made in company with HMS Varne (Lt. I.G. Raikes, DSC, RN). (108)
21 Jun 1945 Around 1115 B/21, HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) and HMS Urtica (Lt. R.C. Bucknall, RN) departed Rothesay for Portsmouth. (109)
25 Jun 1945 Around 0915 B/25, HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) and HMS Urtica (Lt. R.C. Bucknall, RN) arrived at Portsmouth from Rothesay. (109)
28 Jun 1945 At 1200 B/28, HMS Uproar (Lt. J.N. Devlin, DSC, RN) is reduced to reserve at Portsmouth (Gosport / Fort Blockhouse). (107)
29 Aug 1945 HMS Uproar, HMS Unrivalled, HMS Vitality and HMS Urtica departed Portsmouth for Londonderry (Lough Foyle).
1 Sep 1945 HMS Uproar, HMS Unrivalled, HMS Vitality and HMS Urtica arrived at Londonderry (Lough Foyle) from Portsmouth.
ADM numbers indicate documents at the British National Archives at Kew, London.
